topics.title: Familiarization of Operational Amplifiers - Inverting and Non-inverting Amplifiers, Integrator, Differentiator - Frequency Response, Adder, Comparators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of Operational Amplifiers - Inverting and Non-inverting Amplifiers, Integrator, Differentiator - Frequency Response, Adder, Comparators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)?

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and typically a single-ended output. It is built as an integrated circuit (IC) and serves as the fundamental building block of most analog linear circuits. The op-amp amplifies the difference between its two input terminals by an enormous open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ (typically **$10^5$ to $10^6$**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> An operational amplifier is a DC-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and, usually, a single-ended output. In its ordinary (open-loop) configuration, the op-amp produces an output potential that is typically **hundreds of thousands of times larger** than the potential difference between its input terminals.

### Functional Block Diagram of an Op-Amp

| Terminal | Symbol | Function |
|----------|--------|----------|
| Inverting Input | $V^{-}$ (Pin 2) | Output is $180^\circ$ out of phase with this input |
| Non-inverting Input | $V^{+}$ (Pin 3) | Output is in phase with this input |
| Output | $V_{out}$ (Pin 6) | Amplified output signal |
| Positive Supply | $V_{CC}$ (Pin 7) | Positive DC power (+12V or +15V) |
| Negative Supply | $-V_{EE}$ (Pin 4) | Negative DC power (-12V or -15V) |

### Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Cardinal Rules for Negative Feedback Analysis (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> 1. **Rule 1 — Infinite Input Impedance (No current into inputs):** $i^{+} = i^{-} = 0$
> 2. **Rule 2 — Virtual Short (Differential gain infinite):** $V^{+} = V^{-}$ (when negative feedback exists)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Balancing Seesaw"

Imagine a **seesaw on a playground** with a child sitting on each end. The op-amp's internal feedback mechanism is like an invisible hand that constantly adjusts the seesaw so that the height of both ends is **always equal**. 

- The **two children** represent the inverting ($V^{-}$) and non-inverting ($V^{+}$) inputs.
- The **ground level** is the reference (0V).
- The **invisible hand** represents the negative feedback that forces $V^{+} = V^{-}$.
- The **output** is the amplified "lever movement" needed to keep the seesaw balanced.

If you push one end down (apply input voltage), the hand works furiously to keep the seesaw level — this requires massive movement at the output, which is exactly what high gain $A_{OL}$ represents.

## 1.3 Why Op-Amp Familiarization is Critical in LIC Lab

The op-amp (IC 741 being the most common in KTU labs) is the **Swiss Army Knife** of analog electronics. From audio amplifiers to medical ECG machines, from filters to instrumentation — every linear application uses one of the seven configurations covered in this experiment.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Lab Tip:** Always use a dual power supply (±12V or ±15V) when working with the IC 741. Connecting the output directly to ground will short the internal transistors and destroy the IC.
>

topics.title: Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Op-Amp Parameter Measurement** refers to the systematic experimental determination of the static (DC) and dynamic (AC) non-ideal characteristics of an operational amplifier (typically the **µA741** or **LM741** in KTU labs) under standard test conditions. These parameters quantify the deviation of a real Op-Amp from the behavior of an *ideal* Op-Amp, and they are essential for predicting the performance of analog circuits built using these devices.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Manual, this experiment typically involves the measurement of the following **eight** critical parameters:

1. **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$)**
2. **Input Bias Current ($I_B$)**
3. **Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$)**
4. **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**
5. **Slew Rate (SR)**
6. **Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)**
7. **Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)**
8. **Output Voltage Swing ($V_{O(p-p)}$)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is designed to develop the CO (Course Outcome) of *designing and testing analog circuits using Op-Amps*. Students must record **tabulated readings**, draw the **circuit diagrams**, perform **calculations with units**, and present a **model graph** (especially for Slew Rate and CMRR vs. Frequency).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Faucet" Model

Imagine an **ideal water tap** that:
- Opens only when you turn the handle (infinite input resistance),
- Delivers exactly the requested amount of water (zero error),
- Never leaks sideways (perfect common-mode rejection),
- Responds instantly to your command (infinite slew rate).

A **real Op-Amp** is like a real-world tap that:
- Lets a tiny trickle of water leak through even when "off" (this is the **Input Bias Current**),
- Delivers water at a slightly different rate than asked (this is the **Offset Voltage**),
- Cannot fully block changes in inlet pressure (this is the **PSRR**),
- Takes a moment to open fully when you crank the handle (this is the **Slew Rate**).

In the lab, we **measure these "imperfections"** using carefully designed test circuits. Each test circuit isolates one specific non-ideality and forces the Op-Amp to reveal it as a measurable voltage or current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** All measurements are taken at $V_{CC} = +15\text{ V}$, $V_{EE} = -15\text{ V}$, ambient temperature $T = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$, unless otherwise specified in the manual.

### 1.3 The Ideal vs. Real Op-Amp — A Comparison Foundation

| Parameter | Ideal Value | Real µA741 Value (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Input Resistance ($R_i$) | $\infty$ | $\approx 2\,\text{M}\Omega$ |
| Output Resistance ($R_o$) | $0$ | $\approx 75\,\Omega$ |
| Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$) | $\infty$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^5$ (106 dB) |
| CMRR | $\infty$ | $\approx 90\,\text{dB}$ |
| Slew Rate | $\infty$ | $\approx 0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ |
| Input Offset Voltage | $0$ | $\approx 2\,\text{mV}$ |
| Input Bias Current | $0$ | $\approx 80\,\text{nA}$ |
| Bandwidth | $\infty$ | $\approx 1\,\text{MHz}$ (GBW) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Are These Parameters Non-Zero?

The internal structure of an Op-Amp contains **differential input transistors** (typically a BJT Darlington or MOSFET pair). Due to manufacturing imperfections:

- **Mismatched transistor pairs** → cause $V_{io}$ and $I_{io}$.
- **Base currents required to bias transistors** → cause $I_B$.
- **Parasitic capacitances** inside the compensation capacitor (typically 30 pF in µA741) → cause **finite Slew Rate** and **finite Bandwidth**.
- **Power supply ripple coupling** into the signal path → causes finite **PSRR**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each measurement circuit in the lab is engineered so that **only one parameter dominates** the output, allowing it to be back-calculated from a simple DC voltage or AC measurement.

### 2.2 The Eight Parameters — Logical Breakdown

**A. DC Parameters (measured using multimeter / DMM)**

1. **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$):** The small differential DC voltage that must be applied between the inputs to make the output exactly zero.
   - *Why it exists:* Mismatch in $V_{BE}$ of the input differential pair.
   - *Effect:* Adds an unwanted DC error to the output.

2. **Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** The average of the two DC base currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting inputs.
   $$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$
   - *Why it exists:* BJT inputs always require base current to stay active.
   - *Effect:* Causes output offset when the input source has finite resistance.

3. **Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$):** The difference between the two input bias currents.
   $$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$
   - *Effect:* Drift in output offset with temperature.

**B. AC Parameters (measured using function generator + CRO/DSO)**

4. **CMRR:** Ability of the Op-Amp to reject a signal common to both inputs.
   $$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_c} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)\,\text{dB}$$

5. **Slew Rate (SR):** Maximum rate of change of output voltage with time.
   $$\text{SR} = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{\max} = \frac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta t}\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$$

6. **Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$):** Gain of the Op-Amp with **no feedback** applied.

7. **PSRR:** Ability to reject variations in the supply voltage.
   $$\text{PSRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_{supply}}{\Delta V_{io}}\right)\,\text{dB}$$

8. **Output Voltage Swing ($V_{O(p-p)}$):** The maximum peak-to-peak output voltage before clipping.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Formula | Typical µA741 Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{io}$ | $V_o / (1 + R_f/R_i)$ | $2$ | mV |
| 2 | Input Bias Current | $I_B$ | $(I_{B1}+I_{B2})/2$ | $80$ | nA |
| 3 | Input Offset Current | $I_{io}$ | $\vert I_{B1}-I_{B2}\vert$ | $20$ | nA |
| 4 | CMRR | $\rho$ | $20\log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$ | $90$ | dB |
| 5 | Slew Rate | SR | $\Delta V_o / \Delta t$ | $0.5$ | V/$\mu$s |
| 6 | Open-Loop Gain | $A_{OL}$ | $V_o / V_{in}$ | $2\times 10^5$ | V/V (106 dB) |
| 7 | PSRR | PSRR | $20\log_{10}(\Delta V_{cc}/\Delta V_{io})$ | $90$ | dB |
| 8 | Output Swing | $V_{O(p-p)}$ | Measured peak-to-peak | $\pm 13$ | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Knowing these parameters lets a circuit designer choose appropriate **external offset-nulling techniques**, **feedback resistor values**, **compensation capacitors**, and **supply decoupling networks**. For example, knowing $I_B \approx 80\,\text{nA}$ tells the designer to keep the source resistance below $\approx 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ to limit output offset to under $1\,\text{mV}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Measurement Circuits & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Measurement 1: Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$)

**Test Circuit:** A unity-gain buffer (voltage follower) with both inputs grounded through identical resistors ($R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$).

```
        R = 1kΩ
   +Vcc ────────── Pin 7
   -Vee ────────── Pin 4
                  ┌──────┐
   Pin 3 ─── 1kΩ──┤  +   │
   (Non-Inv)      │ 741  ├──── Pin 6 ──── Vout (= Vio)
   Pin 2 ─── 1kΩ──┤  -   │
                  └──────┘
   Pins 1, 5, 8 ──── Not connected
```

**Working Principle:** With both inputs at the *same* potential (ground), the *ideal* output should be 0 V. Any non-zero DC output is solely due to $V_{io}$ being amplified by the closed-loop gain (which is unity here).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Closed-Loop Gain:}\quad A_{CL} &= 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1 \quad \text{(buffer)} \\
\\
\text{Output Voltage:}\quad V_o &= A_{CL} \cdot V_{io} = 1 \cdot V_{io} \\
\\
\therefore\quad \boxed{V_{io} = V_o}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Procedure:**
1. Connect the circuit on a breadboard.
2. Power up with $V_{CC} = +15\,\text{V}$, $V_{EE} = -15\,\text{V}$.
3. Measure $V_o$ using a DMM.
4. **Result:** If DMM reads, say, $V_o = 1.8\,\text{mV}$, then $V_{io} = 1.8\,\text{mV}$.

---

### 3.2 Measurement 2: Input Bias Current ($I_B$)

**Test Circuit:** Inverting amplifier with the **non-inverting input grounded through a large resistor $R$** and **inverting input also grounded** (so no current path except via the internal Op-Amp inputs).

**Working Principle:** The input bias current $I_B$ flows *into* the non-inverting pin through resistor $R$, creating a voltage drop $I_B \cdot R$ which is amplified by the non-inverting gain.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Voltage at Non-Inv Pin:}\quad V_+ &= -I_B \cdot R \\
\\
\text{Output Voltage:}\quad V_o &= \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_+ = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot (-I_B R) \\
\\
\therefore\quad \boxed{I_B = \frac{\vert V_o \vert}{R\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the standard KTU lab, with $R = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_f = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ (giving gain = 1001), if $V_o = 80\,\text{mV}$, then $I_B = 80\,\text{mV} / (1\,\text{M}\Omega \cdot 1001) \approx 80\,\text{nA}$. This is exactly the µA741 datasheet value.

---

### 3.3 Measurement 3: Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$)

**Test Circuit:** Same as $I_B$ measurement, but two separate measurements are taken:
- $I_{B1}$: current into inverting input,
- $I_{B2}$: current into non-inverting input.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B1} &= \frac{\vert V_{o1} \vert}{R \cdot A_{CL}} \\
\\
I_{B2} &= \frac{\vert V_{o2} \vert}{R \cdot A_{CL}} \\
\\
\therefore\quad \boxed{I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Measurement 4: CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)

**Test Circuit:** The Op-Amp is wired as a **difference amplifier** with gain = 1 (or a non-inverting configuration where $V_{in}$ is applied to *both* inputs simultaneously).

**Working Principle:** Apply a low-frequency sine wave (e.g., $1\,\text{kHz}$, $V_{in} = 5\,\text{V}_{p-p}$) to both inputs. The *ideal* output is 0 V. The real output is the **common-mode error voltage**, used to compute the common-mode gain $A_c$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_d &= 1 \quad \text{(difference amplifier gain)} \\
A_c &= \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} \\
\\
\text{CMRR (ratio)} &= \frac{A_d}{A_c} \\
\\
\boxed{\text{CMRR (in dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Variation Test (KTU Extension):** To plot CMRR vs. Frequency, repeat the measurement at $f = 100\,\text{Hz}, 1\,\text{kHz}, 10\,\text{kHz}, 100\,\text{kHz}, 1\,\text{MHz}$. CMRR rolls off at higher frequencies due to internal pole.

---

### 3.5 Measurement 5: Slew Rate (SR)

**Test Circuit:** Op-Amp as **unity-gain follower** (buffer) with a **high-frequency, large-amplitude square wave** input (e.g., $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$, $V_{in} = 10\,V_{p-p}$).

**Working Principle:** An *ideal* buffer would reproduce the square wave perfectly. The *real* Op-Amp cannot change its output instantaneously; the output rises/falls linearly during the transition. Measure the slope of this linear region.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Output Waveform:}\quad \text{Trapezoidal (not square)} \\
\\
\text{Slew Rate:}\quad \text{SR} &= \frac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta t} \\
\\
\boxed{\text{SR} = \frac{V_{o(\text{peak-to-peak})}}{T_{\text{transition}}}\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \Delta V_o &= 10\,\text{V,}\quad \Delta t = 20\,\mu\text{s} \\
\\
\text{SR} &= \frac{10\,\text{V}}{20\,\mu\text{s}} = 0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s} \quad \checkmark \text{(matches µA741 datasheet)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.6 Measurement 6: Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)

**Test Circuit:** Op-Amp with **no feedback** ($R_f$ removed). Apply a very small AC input ($\approx 50\,\mu\text{V}$ at $1\,\text{kHz}$), observe output on DSO.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= A_{OL} \cdot V_{in} \\
\\
\therefore\quad \boxed{A_{OL} = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Pitfall:** In open-loop, the Op-Amp saturates for any $V_{in} > 1\,\text{mV}$ (since $A_{OL} \approx 200{,}000$ and $V_{CC} = 15\,\text{V}$). Hence the input must be kept in the **microvolt range**, or the test must be done at a frequency above the dominant pole (e.g., 1 kHz) where the AC gain is lower.

**Gain in dB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{OL}(\text{dB}) &= 20\log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) \\
&= 20 \times 5.301 \\
&= 106.02\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.7 Measurement 7: Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)

**Test Circuit:** Op-Amp wired as a buffer with input grounded. Vary $V_{CC}$ (e.g., from +12 V to +18 V) and measure the resulting $\Delta V_{io}$ (which appears at the output).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{io} &= \frac{\Delta V_o}{A_{CL}} \quad \text{(for unity-gain buffer, } A_{CL}=1) \\
\\
\boxed{\text{PSRR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_{CC}}{\Delta V_{io}}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.8 Measurement 8: Output Voltage Swing ($V_{O(p-p)}$)

**Test Circuit:** Op-Amp in non-inverting amplifier mode (gain = 10). Apply a $1\,\text{kHz}$ sine wave of increasing amplitude. The output is observed on the DSO. The peak-to-peak output is measured just before the waveform begins to clip (flatten at the peaks).

**Reading:** $V_{O(p-p)} \approx 2 \times (V_{CC} - 2) = 2 \times 13 = 26\,\text{V}$ for µA741 with $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supplies.

---

### 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table for Record Book

| Sl. | Parameter | Circuit Used | Formula | Measured Value | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{io}$ | Unity buffer | $V_o$ | ___ | $2$ | mV |
| 2 | $I_B$ | Inv. amp + $R$ | $\vert V_o\vert/(R \cdot A_{CL})$ | ___ | $80$ | nA |
| 3 | $I_{io}$ | Difference | $\vert I_{B1}-I_{B2}\vert$ | ___ | $20$ | nA |
| 4 | CMRR | Diff. amp | $20\log(A_d/A_c)$ | ___ | $90$ | dB |
| 5 | SR | Buffer + sq. wave | $\Delta V_o / \Delta t$ | ___ | $0.5$ | V/$\mu$s |
| 6 | $A_{OL}$ | Open loop | $V_o/V_{in}$ | ___ | $10^5$–$10^6$ | V/V |
| 7 | PSRR | Buffer | $20\log(\Delta V_{cc}/\Delta V_{io})$ | ___ | $90$ | dB |
| 8 | $V_{O(p-p)}$ | Non-inv. amp | $V_{out(\max)}$ | ___ | $26$ | V |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Op-Amp Parameter Measurement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Power ON Op-Amp with ±15V] --> B[Verify Zero Output with Grounded Inputs]
    B --> C[Select Parameter to Measure]
    C --> D1[Test Circuit 1: Buffer]
    C --> D2[Test Circuit 2: Inv. Amp with R]
    C --> D3[Test Circuit 3: Diff. Amp]
    C --> D4[Test Circuit 4: Buffer + Square Wave]
    C --> D5[Test Circuit 5: Open Loop]
    
    D1 --> E1[Measure Vio from Vout]
    D2 --> E2[Measure IB from Vout and R]
    D3 --> E3[Measure CMRR from Vout/Vin]
    D4 --> E4[Measure SR from slope of Vout]
    D5 --> E5[Measure AOL from Vout/Vin]
    
    E1 --> F[Tabulate Results]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    
    F --> G[Compare with Datasheet Standard Values]
    G --> H[Calculate Percentage Deviation]
    H --> I[Record in Lab Manual with Signatures]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of µA741 (showing what causes each parameter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_STAGE[Input Differential Stage]
        A1[Q1: NPN Input] --- A2[Q2: NPN Input]
        A1 --- A3[Current Mirror Load]
        A2 --- A3
    end
    
    subgraph GAIN_STAGE[High Gain Stage]
        B1[Q3: Darlington Driver] --> B2[Active Load]
        B2 --> B3[Output of Gain Stage]
    end
    
    subgraph COMPENSATION[Frequency Compensation]
        C1[Internal 30 pF Capacitor]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT_STAGE[Class AB Output]
        D1[NPN Push] --- D2[PNP Pull]
    end
    
    A3 --> B1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    
    A1 --> P1[Pin 3: Non-Inverting]
    A2 --> P2[Pin 2: Inverting]
    D1 --> P3[Pin 6: Output]
    
    P1 -. Causes Vio, IB, Iio .-> INPUT_STAGE
    C1 -. Causes Slew Rate Limit, Bandwidth Limit .-> COMPENSATION
    P4[VCC Pin 7] -. Couples ripple, causes PSRR .-> INPUT_STAGE
```

### 4.3 Slew Rate Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Square Wave Input] --> B[Op-Amp Buffer]
    B --> C[Internal Capacitor Charging]
    C --> D[Slew Limited Output: Trapezoid]
    D --> E[Slope = SR = dV/dt]
    
    A -->|Vin step of DV| B
    C -->|Current I from internal stage| D
    E -->|SR = I / Cc| F[Measurement on DSO]
```

### 4.4 CMRR Test Setup Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Function Generator: 1kHz Sine] --> B[Both Inputs of Difference Amp]
    B --> C[Op-Amp µA741]
    C --> D[DSO Channel 1: Output Vo]
    C --> E[DSO Channel 2: Input Vin]
    
    E -->|Measure Vin peak| F[Calculate Ac = Vo/Vin]
    D -->|Measure Vo peak| F
    F --> G[CMRR dB = 20 log Ad/Ac]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Input Offset Voltage of an Op-Amp. Draw the circuit used to measure it.*

**Model Answer:**
Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$) is the small differential DC voltage that must be applied between the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of an Op-Amp to make the output voltage exactly zero volts.
The measurement circuit is a **unity-gain buffer** with both inputs grounded through identical resistors (typically $1\,\text{k}\Omega$). The measured output voltage equals $V_{io}$ directly since the closed-loop gain is unity. Typical value for µA741 is **2 mV**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between Input Bias Current and Input Offset Current. State one effect of each on circuit performance.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Input Bias Current ($I_B$) | Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Average of the two DC base currents | Difference between the two base currents |
| Formula | $I_B = (I_{B1}+I_{B2})/2$ | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1}-I_{B2}\vert$ |
| Effect on circuit | Creates DC output offset when source has finite resistance | Causes drift of output offset with temperature |

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to measure the **Slew Rate** of an Op-Amp. Derive the formula used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **CMRR**. With a suitable test circuit and waveforms, explain how CMRR of a µA741 is measured. Calculate the CMRR in dB if $A_d = 2\times 10^5$ and $A_c = 6.32$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — Slew Rate Measurement (7 marks):**

**Circuit:** The µA741 is connected as a **unity-gain voltage follower** (buffer). A **square wave** of frequency $1\,\text{kHz}$ and amplitude $10\,V_{p-p}$ is applied at the non-inverting input. The output is observed on a DSO.

**Procedure:**
1. Set the function generator to square wave, $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$, $V_{in} = 10\,V_{p-p}$.
2. Connect the Op-Amp as a buffer.
3. Observe the output on the DSO.
4. Measure the **peak-to-peak output voltage** $\Delta V_o$ and the **transition time** $\Delta t$ (the time taken for the output to swing from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$).
5. Compute SR.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SR} &= \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{\max} \\
\\
\text{For finite SR, output becomes trapezoidal:} \\
\\
\text{SR} &= \frac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta t} \\
\\
\text{Numerical: if } \Delta V_o = 10\,\text{V,}\quad \Delta t = 20\,\mu\text{s:} \\
\\
\text{SR} &= \frac{10}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The standard datasheet value for µA741 is **$0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$**, confirming the measurement.

---

**Model Solution (b) — CMRR (7 marks):**

**Definition:** CMRR is the ratio of differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to common-mode gain ($A_c$), expressed in dB:

$$
\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)\,\text{dB}
$$

**Test Circuit:** The Op-Amp is connected as a **difference amplifier** (using 4 matched resistors of $10\,\text{k}\Omega$). A sine wave of $1\,\text{kHz}$, $5\,V_{p-p}$ is applied to **both inputs simultaneously**. The output is observed on the DSO.

**Procedure:**
1. Connect the difference amplifier with gain = 1 ($R_f = R_g = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$).
2. Apply a $1\,\text{kHz}$ sine wave, measure $V_{in(p-p)}$.
3. Measure $V_{o(p-p)}$.
4. Calculate $A_c = V_o / V_{in}$.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_d &= 2 \times 10^5 = 200{,}000 \\
A_c &= 6.32 \\
\\
\text{CMRR} &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200{,}000}{6.32}\right) \\
&= 20 \times \log_{10}(31{,}645.6) \\
&= 20 \times 4.5001 \\
&= \boxed{90.00\,\text{dB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the standard µA741 value.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit used to measure **Input Bias Current** and **Input Offset Current** of an Op-Amp. Derive the relevant formula. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain how **Open-Loop Voltage Gain** is measured. Why must the input signal be very small? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — $I_B$ and $I_{io}$ (7 marks):**

**Circuit:** A non-inverting amplifier (gain = $1 + R_f/R_1$) is used. The inverting input is grounded; the non-inverting input is grounded through a large resistor $R$ (typically $1\,\text{M}\Omega$).

**Working Principle:** The bias current $I_B$ flowing into the non-inverting pin creates a voltage $I_B \cdot R$ at that pin, which is then amplified by the closed-loop gain.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_+ &= -I_B \cdot R \\
\\
V_o &= \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_+ = -\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) I_B R \\
\\
\therefore\quad I_B &= \frac{\vert V_o \vert}{R \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)} \quad \cdots \text{(1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $I_{io}$, perform two separate measurements (one for each input), then:

$$
I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert \quad \cdots \text{(2)}
$$

---

**Model Solution (b) — Open-Loop Gain (7 marks):**

**Circuit:** The Op-Amp is connected with **no feedback path** (open loop). The input is a very small AC signal (typically 50–100 $\mu$V) at $1\,\text{kHz}$. The output is measured on the DSO.

**Formula:**

$$
A_{OL} = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}}
$$

**Why small input?** Since $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$, even an input of $1\,\text{mV}$ would theoretically produce $200\,\text{V}$ at the output — but the supply is only $\pm 15\,\text{V}$. The Op-Amp would **saturate** instantly, giving a clipped (square) output that doesn't represent the linear gain. Hence, $V_{in}$ must be in the **microvolt range** to keep the output within the linear region.

**In dB:**

$$
A_{OL}(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) = 106\,\text{dB}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing "SR = 0.5" instead of "0.5 V/$\mu$s") → lose 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Using closed-loop circuit for $A_{OL}$** test → entire procedure marked wrong.
> 3. **Applying sine wave for Slew Rate** test (must be square wave) → 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting to state typical datasheet value** for comparison → lose 0.5 mark.
> 5. **CMRR calculation error** — confusing natural log ($\ln$) with log base 10. Always use $\log_{10}$ for dB.
> 6. **Not grounding both inputs identically** in $V_{io}$ test → creates extra error from $I_B$ flow.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Input Offset Voltage** $V_{io}$ is measured using a **unity-gain buffer** with both inputs grounded through identical resistors; $V_{io} = V_o$ directly.
- **Input Bias Current** $I_B$ uses a **large series resistor** ($1\,\text{M}\Omega$) at the non-inverting input so that the small $I_B$ produces a measurable voltage drop.
- **Input Offset Current** $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$ — found by taking two separate measurements.
- **CMRR** test uses a **difference amplifier** with both inputs driven by the **same** sine wave; result is in **dB** via $20\log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$.
- **Slew Rate** test uses a **square wave** at the buffer input; SR = slope of the linear portion of the output trapezoid.
- **Open-Loop Gain** $A_{OL}$ requires a **very small input** ($\mu$V range) to avoid saturation; can be expressed in **dB** as $20\log_{10}(A_{OL})$.
- **PSRR** measures how much supply variation $\Delta V_{CC}$ translates to input offset variation $\Delta V_{io}$, again in dB.
- **Output Swing** is found by increasing input amplitude until clipping just begins on the DSO; for µA741 with $\pm 15\,\text{V}$, $V_{O(p-p)} \approx 26\,\text{V}$.
- **Standard reference:** Typical µA741 values: $V_{io} \approx 2\,\text{mV}$, $I_B \approx 80\,\text{nA}$, $I_{io} \approx 20\,\text{nA}$, CMRR $\approx 90\,\text{dB}$, SR $\approx 0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$, $A_{OL} \approx 106\,\text{dB}$.
- **Always** include **units** in the final tabulated answer; **always** show the **formula derivation**; **always** draw the **circuit diagram with pin numbers** (Pin 2, 3, 4, 6, 7) for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Amplifier and Instrumentation Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Difference Amplifier and Instrumentation Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Difference Amplifier — Formal Definition

A **Difference Amplifier** is a fundamental op-amp based configuration that amplifies the *algebraic difference* between two input signals while (ideally) rejecting any signal that is common to both inputs. Built using a single op-amp with four precisely matched resistors, it forms the core building block of nearly every precision analog signal-conditioning circuit.

$$V_{out} = A_d(V_2 - V_1) + A_c \cdot V_{cm}$$

Where:
- $A_d$ = Differential Gain
- $A_c$ = Common-Mode Gain
- $V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ = Common-Mode Voltage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECL407):** The Difference Amplifier is the foundational stage of Experiment 2. Students must master the resistor matching condition and the CMRR derivation, as it is a recurring question in University ESE viva and lab record evaluations.

### 1.2 Instrumentation Amplifier — Formal Definition

An **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** is a precision, closed-loop gain block that provides high differential gain, exceptionally high input impedance, and outstanding **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**. The canonical implementation uses **three op-amps**: two input buffer stages followed by a unity-gain difference amplifier.

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The single external resistor $R_g$ sets the gain, making the INA highly flexible for transducer-based signal acquisition systems.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Difference Amplifier — The "Subtractor Judge":**
Imagine two candidates giving a speech to a judge. The judge (op-amp) listens to *both* speakers but only scores them on the *difference* in their arguments. If both raise their voices equally loud (common noise), the judge smartly ignores that — that's **common-mode rejection**. Only the *contrast* (difference) matters for the final output.

**Instrumentation Amplifier — The "Medical Stethoscope":**
Picture a doctor trying to hear a faint heartbeat from a noisy ICU room. A regular amplifier would amplify everything — including the ventilator beeps. An INA acts like a precision stethoscope: it amplifies the *tiny biological signal difference* picked up from two skin electrodes, while **rejecting the 50 Hz mains hum** that is common to both electrodes. This is exactly why ECGs, EEGs, and strain-gauge bridges always use an INA.

> [!TIP]
> The instrumentation amplifier is the industry-standard front-end for any sensor system where signal strength is in the **millivolt range** and noise is in the **volt range**.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Ideal CMRR** = $\infty$ (dB) → Practically **≥ 100 dB** for precision INAs
- **Input Impedance** of INA = $\infty$ (driven by op-amp non-inverting inputs)
- **Bandwidth** of standard INA ICs (e.g., AD620) = **120 kHz** at G = 100
- **Standard Supply Rails** = **±15 V** (lab) or **±5 V** to **±18 V** (industrial)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Difference Amplifier — Operational Breakdown

The classic op-amp difference amplifier uses four resistors arranged in a bridge-like topology:

- **Top feedback path** $R_2$ connects op-amp output to inverting input.
- **Bottom path** $R_1$ connects inverting input to $V_1$.
- **Top non-inverting path** $R_3$ connects $V_2$ to non-inverting input.
- **Bottom non-inverting path** $R_4$ grounds the non-inverting node.

**Why this topology?**
The op-amp enforces a *virtual short* between its inputs, so the inverting input voltage mirrors the non-inverting input voltage (set by the $R_3$–$R_4$ divider from $V_2$). Superposition then yields the output.

**Operating Principles:**
1. The op-amp's high open-loop gain (typically **$10^5$ to $10^6$**) forces the differential input voltage to nearly zero.
2. The $R_3$–$R_4$ voltage divider scales $V_2$ at the non-inverting input.
3. The $R_1$–$R_2$ network then amplifies the difference between the scaled $V_2$ and $V_1$.

### 2.2 Instrumentation Amplifier — Operational Breakdown

The 3 op-amp INA has two distinct stages:

**Stage 1 — Input Bufflers (A1, A2):**
- Provide **infinite input impedance** to the source signals $V_1$ and $V_2$.
- The gain-setting resistor $R_g$ between their inverting terminals creates differential gain.
- Both op-amps share $R_g$, so any common-mode signal produces *no current through $R_g$*.

**Stage 2 — Difference Amplifier (A3):**
- The standard difference amplifier from Section 2.1.
- Provides common-mode rejection and sets the overall differential gain via resistor ratio $R_3/R_2$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Difference Amplifier** | **Instrumentation Amplifier** |
|---|---|---|
| Differential Gain $A_d$ | $A_d = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$ | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$ |
| Match Condition (ideal CMRR) | $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$ | $R_2 = R_4$ and $R_3 = R_5$ |
| Common-Mode Gain $A_c$ | $A_c = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \cdot \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{R_1}$ (for mismatch) | $\to 0$ if Stage-2 matched |
| CMRR (dB) | $20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | $20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ |
| Input Impedance | $R_1 + R_3 \Vert R_4$ (finite) | $\infty$ (op-amp non-inverting) |
| Output Expression | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}(V_2 - V_1)$ |
| Single-Resistor Tunable Gain | ✗ | ✓ (via $R_g$ only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In the 3-op-amp INA, the common-mode voltage at the output of Stage 1 is *constant* and equal to the input common-mode voltage. This is because no current flows through $R_g$ for a common-mode signal — hence it gets rejected.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG, EEG, EMG front-ends (AD620, INA128).
- **Industrial Process Control:** Thermocouple and RTD signal conditioning.
- **Weighing Scales / Load Cells:** Strain-gauge bridge amplification.
- **Audio Engineering:** Balanced audio line receivers (e.g., THAT Corp INA series).
- **Automotive Sensors:** Pressure, accelerometer, and Hall-effect sensor readouts.

> [!NOTE]
> The INA is the *de facto* standard interface between sensors and ADCs in any precision data acquisition system.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Difference Amplifier Output Voltage

**Given:** Single op-amp with $R_1$ from $V_1$ to inverting node, $R_2$ as feedback, $R_3$ from $V_2$ to non-inverting node, $R_4$ from non-inverting node to ground.

**Step 1 — Voltage at the non-inverting input ($V^+$):**
Apply the voltage divider rule (no current flows into op-amp ideal input).

$$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 2 — Apply virtual short condition:**
Since op-amp is in linear region with negative feedback, $V^- = V^+$.

$$V^- = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the inverting node ($V^-$):**
Sum of currents leaving the node = 0.

$$\frac{V^- - V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V^-$ from Step 2:**

$$\frac{V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} - V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} - V_{out}}{R_2} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

Rearranging the KCL equation and isolating $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1} + 1\right)\left(\frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1}V_1$$

**Step 6 — Apply the matching condition** $\dfrac{R_1}{R_3} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_4}$, equivalently $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$:

Under matching, $\dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} = \dfrac{R_2/R_1}{1 + R_2/R_1}$, which simplifies to:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of conversion:** The matching condition ensures that the gain for $V_2$ (through the non-inverting path) equals the gain magnitude of the inverting path for $V_1$. Any common-mode component $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$ cancels out, yielding $V_{out} = 0$.

### 3.2 Derivation — Instrumentation Amplifier Output Voltage

**Given:** 3 op-amps with input buffer resistors $R_1$ on both A1/A2 inverting paths, $R_g$ between the two inverting nodes, Stage-2 difference amplifier with $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_2$, $R_3$.

**Step 1 — Analyze Stage 1 current through $R_g$:**

The op-amps A1 and A2 force their inverting inputs to follow the non-inverting inputs:

$$V^-_{A1} = V_1, \quad V^-_{A2} = V_2$$

The voltage across $R_g$ is therefore $(V_2 - V_1)$, giving a current:

$$I_{Rg} = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_g}$$

**Step 2 — Currents through $R_1$ resistors (KCL on A1, A2 inverting nodes):**

Since no current enters the op-amp inputs, the current through $R_1$ on A1's side equals $I_{Rg}$. Same for A2's side.

$$I_{R_1} = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_g}$$

**Step 3 — Output voltages of A1 and A2:**

The voltage drop across each $R_1$ resistor adds to the input voltage:

$$V_{o1} = V_1 - I_{R_1} \cdot R_1 = V_1 - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1)$$

$$V_{o2} = V_2 + I_{R_1} \cdot R_1 = V_2 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 4 — Differential output of Stage 1:**

$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Pass through Stage 2 difference amplifier (from §3.1):**

Stage 2 is a unity-gain difference amplifier if $R_2 = R_3$, providing:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_2 - V_1)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation of $R_g$ effect:** As $R_g \to \infty$, Stage 1 gain $\to 1$ (pure buffering). As $R_g \to 0$, Stage 1 gain $\to \infty$ — this is why $R_g$ is the *single* gain control and is also a *failure* mode if left open (infinite gain → saturation).

### 3.3 CMRR Derivation — Difference Amplifier with Resistor Mismatch

Let the resistor ratios deviate such that $\frac{R_2}{R_1} = A$ and $\frac{R_4}{R_3} = B$. Then:

$$V_{out} = A(V_2 - V_1) + (B - A)\left(\frac{V_1 \cdot R_3 + V_2 \cdot R_3}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$

For $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$:

$$A_{cm} = (B - A) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**CMRR Formula:**

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A}{B - A}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3 + R_4}{R_3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Gold:** A **1% resistor mismatch** in the difference amplifier yields a maximum CMRR of approximately **34 dB**. This is why the INA's matched thin-film resistor networks (inside ICs) achieve **>100 dB** CMRR.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Difference Amplifier Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    input1[V1 Input] --> r1A[R1]
    input2[V2 Input] --> r3A[R3]
    r1A --> invertingNode[Inverting Node]
    r3A --> noninvertingNode[Non Inverting Node]
    r4A[R4] --> gnd1[Ground]
    noninvertingNode --> gnd1
    invertingNode --> opamp1[Op Amp]
    noninvertingNode --> opamp1
    r2A[R2 Feedback] --> invertingNode
    opamp1 --> voutNode[Vout]
    r2A --> voutNode
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — 3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1Node[V1 Source] --> a1Input[A1 Non Inverting Input]
    v2Node[V2 Source] --> a2Input[A2 Non Inverting Input]
    a1Input --> buffer1[A1 Buffer Op Amp]
    a2Input --> buffer2[A2 Buffer Op Amp]
    buffer1 --> r1Left[R1]
    buffer2 --> r1Right[R1]
    r1Left --> rgNode[Rg Gain Resistor]
    r1Right --> rgNode
    rgNode --> groundRef[Common Mode Reference]
    buffer1 --> stage2InA[Stage 2 Input A]
    buffer2 --> stage2InB[Stage 2 Input B]
    stage2InA --> diffAmp[A3 Difference Amplifier]
    stage2InB --> diffAmp
    r2Path[R2] --> diffAmp
    r3Path[R3] --> diffAmp
    diffAmp --> finalVout[INA Output Vout]
```

### 4.3 Signal Processing Flow — Common-Mode Rejection Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    cmSignal[Common Mode Signal Vcm] --> stage1[INA Stage 1 Bufflers]
    diffSignal[Differential Signal Vd] --> stage1
    stage1 --> stage1Out[Stage 1 Outputs Vo1 and Vo2]
    stage1Out --> stage2[Stage 2 Difference Amplifier]
    stage2 --> filteredOut[Clean Differential Output]
    cmRejected[Common Mode Rejected to Zero]
    stage2 --> cmRejected
    stage1 -. preserves common mode .-> stage2
    stage1 -. amplifies differential .-> stage2
```

### 4.4 Pinout Reference — Typical INA IC (AD620 Family)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pin1[Pin 1 RG] --> rgExt[External Gain Resistor]
    pin2[Pin 2 IN1] --> in1[Input Signal 1]
    pin3[Pin 3 IN2] --> in2[Input Signal 2]
    pin4[Pin 4 VMinus] --> vneg[Negative Supply]
    pin5[Pin 5 GND] --> gnd2[Reference Ground]
    pin6[Pin 6 OUTPUT] --> finalOut[Buffered Output]
    pin7[Pin 7 VPlus] --> vpos[Positive Supply]
    pin8[Pin 8 RG] --> rgExt
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a difference amplifier. Why is it critical in instrumentation applications?*

**Model Answer:**
CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_c$, expressed in decibels:

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

It is critical in instrumentation because sensor signals (ECG, strain gauge, thermocouple) are very small (mV range) and are often corrupted by large common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz power-line interference). A high CMRR ensures that only the desired differential signal is amplified, while the common noise is rejected, preserving measurement accuracy.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *State two advantages of an instrumentation amplifier over a basic difference amplifier.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Infinite input impedance:** The INA uses op-amp non-inverting inputs at the source, preventing loading of high-impedance transducers.
2. **Single-resistor gain control:** The differential gain can be precisely tuned by varying only $R_g$, without disturbing the matched resistor bridge required for high CMRR.
3. (Bonus point) **Higher CMRR:** Commercial INA ICs achieve >100 dB CMRR due to integrated, laser-trimmed thin-film resistors.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the output voltage expression for a basic op-amp difference amplifier with all four resistors distinct. **State the condition for maximum CMRR.** (7 Marks)

**(b)** Design a difference amplifier with a differential gain of 10, using standard 1% E12 resistor values. Choose a sensible value of $R_1$ and compute $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$ such that the input bias currents are balanced. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (a):**
*Refer to derivation in Section 3.1. The output voltage expression is:*

$$V_{out} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1} + 1\right)\left(\frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1}V_1$$

*The condition for maximum CMRR (ideal common-mode rejection) is the resistor matching condition:*

$$\boxed{\frac{R_1}{R_3} = \frac{R_2}{R_4}}$$

*Under this condition, $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$, and the common-mode gain reduces to zero, giving infinite CMRR.*

**Model Solution (b):**
*Step 1: Choose $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (standard E12 value, gives reasonable input impedance).*

*Step 2: Compute $R_2$ for differential gain of 10:*

$$R_2 = A_d \cdot R_1 = 10 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 100\text{ k}\Omega$$

*Step 3: To balance input bias currents, set $R_1 \Vert R_2 = R_3 \Vert R_4$. The Thévenin resistance seen at $V_1$ is $R_1 \Vert R_2$, and at $V_2$ is $R_3 \Vert R_4$.*

$$R_1 \Vert R_2 = \frac{10 \cdot 100}{10+100} \approx 9.09\text{ k}\Omega$$

*Step 4: Apply matching condition $\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3} = 10$:

$$R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_4 = 100\text{ k}\Omega$$

*Step 5: Verify balance:* $R_3 \Vert R_4 = \dfrac{10 \cdot 100}{10+100} = 9.09\text{ k}\Omega$ ✓ *Bias currents are balanced.*

**Final Design:** $R_1 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_4 = 100\text{ k}\Omega$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 3 op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Derive its overall voltage gain. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For an INA, the following values are given: $R_1 = 25\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_g = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 20\text{ k}\Omega$. If $V_1 = 2.05\text{ V}$ and $V_2 = 2.15\text{ V}$, calculate (i) the differential gain, (ii) the output voltage, and (iii) the common-mode input voltage. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (a):**
*Refer to Section 4.2 for the circuit diagram and Section 3.2 for the derivation. The overall voltage gain is:*

$$\boxed{A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}}$$

*Operation: The first stage (A1, A2) provides high input impedance and differential gain via $R_g$. The second stage (A3) is a unity-gain difference amplifier (when $R_2 = R_3$) that performs common-mode rejection.*

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Differential Gain:**

$$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 25\text{k}}{5\text{k}}\right)\left(\frac{20\text{k}}{10\text{k}}\right) = (1 + 10)(2) = \boxed{22}$$

**(ii) Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out} = A_d (V_2 - V_1) = 22 \times (2.15 - 2.05) = 22 \times 0.10 = \boxed{2.2\text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Common-Mode Input Voltage:**

$$V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} = \frac{2.05 + 2.15}{2} = \boxed{2.10\text{ V}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the matching condition:** Students often write $V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ without stating the prerequisite $\frac{R_1}{R_3} = \frac{R_2}{R_4}$. Examiner deducts **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Stage 1 and Stage 2 gains:** The INA's gain is a *product* of two stages, not a sum. Writing $A_d = (1 + 2R_1/R_g) + (R_3/R_2)$ loses **3 marks**.
> 3. **Sign error in output polarity:** For $V_2 > V_1$, $V_{out}$ is *positive*; inverting one of them is a classic slip costing **1 mark**.
> 4. **Ignoring bias-current balancing:** In part (b)-type questions, omitting the condition $R_1 \Vert R_2 = R_3 \Vert R_4$ for a real op-amp design loses **1–2 marks**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Difference Amplifier** uses **1 op-amp + 4 matched resistors**; its gain is $A_d = R_2/R_1$ provided $\frac{R_1}{R_3} = \frac{R_2}{R_4}$.
- ✅ An **Instrumentation Amplifier** uses **3 op-amps**; its gain is $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$.
- ✅ **CMRR (dB)** $= 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$ is the single most important figure of merit.
- ✅ **Stage 1** of INA → high $Z_{in}$ + differential gain; **Stage 2** → common-mode rejection.
- ✅ **Single-resistor gain tuning** via $R_g$ is the INA's biggest design advantage.
- ✅ INA applications: **ECG, EEG, strain-gauge bridges, thermocouples, audio balanced lines**.
- ✅ For lab record: Always **plot $V_{out}$ vs. $V_{in}$** for both $V_1=0$ and $V_2=0$ cases, then **calculate CMRR** from the slopes.
- ✅ **Bias-current compensation** in real designs: ensure $R_1 \Vert R_2 = R_3 \Vert R_4$.
- ✅ **Never leave $R_g$ open** in an INA — gain → ∞ → output saturation.
- ✅ **Standard supply** in KTU lab: **±15 V dual supply** for op-amp ICs (µA741, LM358).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schmitt Trigger Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Schmitt Trigger Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Schmitt Trigger Circuit — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schmitt Trigger:** A Schmitt Trigger is a **regenerative comparator circuit** with **positive feedback** that employs hysteresis to convert a noisy, slowly varying input signal into a clean, sharp, noise-free digital output. It possesses **two distinct switching thresholds**: an **Upper Threshold Point (UTP)** and a **Lower Threshold Point (LTP)**, separated by a region called the **hysteresis width (V_H)**.

In the context of **PCECL407 (Linear Integrated Circuits Lab)**, the Schmitt Trigger is invariably realized using the **µA741 Op-Amp** configured with a **positive feedback network** (typically two resistors, $R_1$ and $R_2$). The output of the op-amp is driven into **saturation**, alternating between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$, producing a **square wave** when fed a sinusoidal or triangular input.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Step Staircase"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy):**
>
> Imagine you are standing inside a room and you want to switch on a fan. The ordinary comparator behaves like a **light switch that responds instantly** to your hand — even if your hand trembles near the boundary, the fan flickers on and off (this is the "chatter" problem, also called **contact bounce** in mechanical switches).
>
> Now imagine a **thermostat** controlling your air conditioner. It does NOT turn ON at 25 °C and OFF at 25 °C. Instead, it turns ON at 27 °C and OFF at 23 °C. This deliberate **gap (hysteresis)** prevents rapid, unwanted switching due to small temperature fluctuations around the set point.
>
> **The Schmitt Trigger is the electronic equivalent of this thermostat.** It has two different trigger points — a higher one to switch ON (UTP) and a lower one to switch OFF (LTP). Any noise smaller than the gap is simply ignored.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Op-Amp Supply Voltage:** Standard laboratory value is **$\pm 12$ V DC** or **$\pm 15$ V DC** (dual supply).
- **Saturation Voltage ($V_{sat}$):** For µA741 powered by $\pm 15$ V, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V (typically $\pm 13.5$ V); for $\pm 12$ V supply, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 10$ V.
- **Resistor Values:** $R_1$ (input/feedback resistor) and $R_2$ (feedback resistor) are typically chosen in the range **$1\text{ k}\Omega$ to $100\text{ k}\Omega$**.
- **Hysteresis Bandwidth:** Engineered to be greater than the peak noise amplitude of the input signal to guarantee noise immunity.

## 1.4 Two Topologies of the Schmitt Trigger

| Topology | Input Applied To | Polarity of Output w.r.t. Input | Common Name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Inverting Terminal ($V^-$) | **Inverted** (opposite phase) | Regenerative Inverting Comparator |
| **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Non-Inverting Terminal ($V^+$) | **Non-Inverted** (same phase) | Regenerative Non-Inverting Comparator |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **PCECL407 Lab viva**, examiners frequently ask: *"Which terminal does the input go to in your inverting Schmitt Trigger?"* The correct answer is the **inverting terminal ($V^-$)**, with the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$) connected to the voltage divider formed by $R_1$ and $R_2$ providing the positive feedback.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Reasoning

The Schmitt Trigger operates on the principle of **positive feedback**, also called **regenerative feedback**. The exact logical steps that govern its switching behavior are as follows:

- **Step 1 — Establishing the Reference Voltage at the Non-Inverting Terminal:**
  A fraction $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ of the output voltage $V_{out}$ is fed back to the non-inverting terminal. This fraction is called the **feedback fraction ($\beta$)** and it is always **positive and less than 1**.

- **Step 2 — Comparing the Two Inputs:**
  The op-amp continuously compares the voltage at the inverting terminal ($V^-$) with the reference voltage at the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$).
  - If $V^- > V^+$ → Output switches to **$-V_{sat}$**.
  - If $V^- < V^+$ → Output switches to **$+V_{sat}$**.

- **Step 3 — Identifying the Two Threshold Voltages:**
  Since $V^+$ is itself dependent on $V_{out}$, the threshold is not a single value but **two values**:
  - **Upper Threshold Voltage ($V_{UTP}$)** = $\beta \cdot (+V_{sat})$
  - **Lower Threshold Voltage ($V_{LTP}$)** = $\beta \cdot (-V_{sat})$

- **Step 4 — The Hysteresis Loop:**
  The **hysteresis width** $V_H$ is defined as the difference between these two thresholds. Any input fluctuation **smaller than $V_H$** is **completely rejected**, providing the noise immunity that makes the Schmitt Trigger indispensable.

- **Step 5 — Regenerative Action:**
  The moment the input crosses a threshold, the positive feedback **accelerates** the transition. This is why the output waveform has **near-vertical, sharp edges** (rise time $\approx$ few microseconds) rather than a gradual slope.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the high-yield equations you must memorize for the KTU lab report, viva, and ESE.**

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Feedback Fraction** | $\beta$ | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Dimensionless | $0 < \beta < 1$ |
| 2 | **Upper Threshold Voltage** | $V_{UTP}$ | $V_{UTP} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | When output is at $+V_{sat}$ |
| 3 | **Lower Threshold Voltage** | $V_{LTP}$ | $V_{LTP} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | When output is at $-V_{sat}$ |
| 4 | **Hysteresis Width** | $V_H$ | $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 2\beta V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | Always positive |
| 5 | **Slew Rate Limit (741)** | $SR$ | $SR = 0.5\text{ V/}\mu s$ (typical) | V/$\mu$s | Limits max. input frequency |
| 6 | **Max Switching Frequency** | $f_{max}$ | $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_{sat}}$ | Hz | For sinusoidal input |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Warning:** In the table above, the expression $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ uses a **forward slash**, NOT the vertical pipe symbol `\vert`. Always write ratios with `/` in your answer booklet to avoid markdown/LaTeX rendering issues in the digital valuation portal.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Schmitt Trigger is the **fundamental building block** in numerous analog and digital systems:

- **Waveform Shaping:** Converts a sine wave or triangular wave into a clean square wave.
- **Zero-Crossing Detectors with Hysteresis:** Used in motor control circuits to avoid relay chattering.
- **Function Generators:** The heart of the **astable multivibrator** (Experiment 3 of PCECL407) — the op-amp astable uses a Schmitt Trigger in its core.
- **Line Receivers / Signal Conditioning:** Cleans up signals transmitted over long, noisy cables (e.g., RS-232, RS-485).
- **Schmitt Trigger Logic Gates:** The 74HC14 and 74LS14 are dedicated Schmitt-trigger-input hex inverters used in debounce circuits.
- **Relaxation Oscillators:** The Schmitt Trigger, combined with an RC timing network, produces a free-running oscillator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Threshold Voltages for the Inverting Schmitt Trigger

### 3.1.1 Circuit Description

The Inverting Schmitt Trigger consists of:
- The input signal $V_{in}$ applied to the **inverting terminal ($V^-$)** through a series resistor $R_{in}$ (often omitted; $V_{in}$ is connected directly).
- A positive feedback network formed by resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ between the output $V_{out}$ and the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$).
- The non-inverting terminal ($V^+$) is held at the **feedback voltage $V_f$** given by the voltage divider rule.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Derivation

The voltage at the non-inverting terminal $V^+$ is governed by the superposition of the contributions from $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$. However, since the inverting terminal draws no current (ideal op-amp assumption) and the input resistor to the inverting terminal carries no current when the input is connected directly, the voltage at the non-inverting terminal is given purely by the **voltage divider rule** applied between $V_{out}$ and ground:

$$V^+ = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Define the **feedback fraction** as:

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Therefore:

$$V^+ = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

The switching condition is governed by the open-loop comparator equation:

$$V_{in} - V^+ = 0 \quad \text{(at the precise switching instant)}$$

Substituting $V^+ = \beta \cdot V_{out}$:

$$V_{in} = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

**Case A — When the output is at $+V_{sat}$:**

The input must rise to the value that just equals the feedback voltage. This is the **Upper Threshold Point**:

$$V_{UTP} = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat}) = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Case B — When the output is at $-V_{sat}$:**

The input must fall to the value that just equals the feedback voltage. This is the **Lower Threshold Point**:

$$V_{LTP} = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat}) = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Hysteresis Width

The hysteresis width is the **separation between the two thresholds** on the input voltage axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} \\
&= \left( +\beta \cdot V_{sat} \right) - \left( -\beta \cdot V_{sat} \right) \\
&= 2\beta \cdot V_{sat} \\
&= \frac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

This equation is the **single most important result** for the entire experiment. It directly determines the **noise margin** of the circuit.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — The "Board Exam Standard" Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design an **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** using a µA741 op-amp powered by $\pm 15$ V DC. Choose $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$. The op-amp saturates at $\pm 13$ V. A triangular wave of amplitude **5 V peak** at **1 kHz** is applied. Verify that the circuit produces a clean square wave and calculate the duty cycle.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the feedback fraction:**

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1\text{ k}\Omega}{10\text{ k}\Omega + 1\text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

**Step 2 — Compute the threshold voltages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UTP} &= +\beta \cdot V_{sat} = +\frac{1}{11} \times 13 \approx +1.182\text{ V} \\
V_{LTP} &= -\beta \cdot V_{sat} = -\frac{1}{11} \times 13 \approx -1.182\text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the hysteresis width:**

$$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 1.182 - (-1.182) = 2.364\text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result for noise immunity:**

Since the input triangular wave has a peak amplitude of **5 V**, it easily crosses both $+1.182$ V and $-1.182$ V during each cycle. Any noise superimposed on the input signal whose peak-to-peak amplitude is **less than 2.364 V** will be **completely rejected**.

**Step 5 — Determine the duty cycle (for symmetric triangular input):**

For a symmetric triangular wave centered at 0 V, the time spent above $V_{UTP}$ equals the time spent below $V_{LTP}$ by symmetry. Therefore:

$$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} = 50\%$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison — Inverting vs. Non-Inverting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following derivation is the inverting terminal comparison for the Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger. The threshold equations are identical, but the **input application point** is reversed.**

For the **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger**, the input $V_{in}$ is applied through $R_1$ to the non-inverting terminal, while the inverting terminal is grounded. The feedback voltage at the inverting terminal is:

$$V^- = \beta \cdot V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{out}$$

The switching condition $V_{in} = V^-$ yields the **same threshold equations**:

$$V_{UTP} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat} \quad , \quad V_{LTP} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Key difference:** In the inverting topology, the input must **cross** the threshold; in the non-inverting topology, the output is in the **same sense** as the input transition (input rising causes output to rise).

## 3.4 Design Constraint — Choosing Resistor Values

A common design scenario in the KTU exam is to **choose resistors** to obtain a given hysteresis. Given desired $V_H$ and known $V_{sat}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{V_H}{2 V_{sat}} \\
\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} &= \frac{V_H}{2 V_{sat}} \\
R_2 &= \frac{V_H}{2 V_{sat} - V_H} \cdot R_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constraint:** $R_1 \parallel R_2$ should be **large** (typically $\geq 10\text{ k}\Omega$) to avoid loading the op-amp output, and **small enough** (typically $\leq 100\text{ k}\Omega$) to keep the bias current errors negligible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional signal flow** of the Inverting Schmitt Trigger, mapping the interactions between the input, the feedback divider, the comparator core, and the saturated output. This representation is used in place of a physical circuit schematic for clarity in digital notes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sinusoidal or Triangular Input Source] --> B[Inverting Terminal V_minus of Op-Amp]
    C[Output V_out] --> D[Feedback Resistor R1]
    D --> E[Non-Inverting Terminal V_plus]
    F[Resistor R2] --> E
    G[Ground Node] --> F
    E --> H[Comparator Decision Logic]
    B --> H
    H --> I{V_in versus V_plus}
    I -->|V_in greater than V_plus| J[Output switches to negative V_sat]
    I -->|V_in less than V_plus| K[Output switches to positive V_sat]
    J --> C
    K --> C
    L[Upper Threshold V_UTP equals beta times V_sat] --> H
    M[Lower Threshold V_LTP equals minus beta times V_sat] --> H
    N[Hysteresis Width V_H equals 2 times beta times V_sat] --> H
```

## 4.2 Hysteresis Loop — Transfer Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Hysteresis Transfer Characteristic"
        P1[Input V_in rising] --> P2[Reaches V_UTP positive]
        P2 --> P3[Output snaps from positive V_sat to negative V_sat]
        P3 --> P4[Input V_in continues to fall]
        P4 --> P5[Reaches V_LTP negative]
        P5 --> P6[Output snaps from negative V_sat to positive V_sat]
        P6 --> P1
    end
    style P3 fill:#ffd166
    style P6 fill:#06d6a0
    style P2 fill:#ef476f
    style P5 fill:#118ab2
```

## 4.3 Physical Pin-Level Reference (µA741 Op-Amp DIP-8)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use the following Mermaid block to recall the µA741 pinout during your lab examination.** This is a frequent viva question: *"Which pins of the 741 are the inverting and non-inverting inputs?"*

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "µA741 Op-Amp DIP-8 Pinout"
        pin1[Pin 1: Offset Null]
        pin2[Pin 2: Inverting Input V_minus]
        pin3[Pin 3: Non-Inverting Input V_plus]
        pin4[Pin 4: V_minus Supply negative 15V]
        pin5[Pin 5: Offset Null]
        pin6[Pin 6: Output]
        pin7[Pin 7: V_plus Supply positive 15V]
        pin8[Pin 8: NC No Connection]
    end
    pin2 --> S1[Schmitt Trigger Input goes here for Inverting Type]
    pin3 --> S2[Schmitt Trigger Feedback Voltage goes here]
    pin6 --> S3[Square Wave Output]
    style S1 fill:#ef476f
    style S2 fill:#118ab2
    style S3 fill:#06d6a0
```

## 4.4 Waveform & Timing Diagram (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Input Signal Sinusoidal V_in"
        S1[Positive Half Cycle peak plus Vp] --> S2[Crosses V_UTP downward]
        S2 --> S3[Negative Half Cycle peak minus Vp]
        S3 --> S4[Crosses V_LTP upward]
        S4 --> S1
    end
    subgraph "Output Signal V_out"
        T1[At positive V_sat when V_in is between V_LTP and V_UTP] --> T2[Drops to negative V_sat when V_in crosses V_UTP]
        T2 --> T3[Stays at negative V_sat while V_in is below V_UTP]
        T3 --> T4[Rises to positive V_sat when V_in crosses V_LTP]
        T4 --> T1
    end
    style S2 fill:#ef476f
    style S4 fill:#06d6a0
    style T2 fill:#ef476f
    style T4 fill:#06d6a0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that the output transitions occur **exactly** at the moments when the input crosses $V_{UTP}$ (red marker) and $V_{LTP}$ (green marker). Between these two crossings, the output holds its previous state — this is the **memory effect** of hysteresis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Format:** Two-mark conceptual question + one-mark diagram/sketch. Model answers below are **board-exam standard**, approximately 80–100 words each.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Schmitt Trigger. What is hysteresis and why is it essential in comparator circuits?**

**Model Answer:**
A Schmitt Trigger is a regenerative comparator circuit with positive feedback that converts a slowly varying input signal into a sharp, noise-immune output. **Hysteresis** is the deliberate difference between the upper threshold voltage ($V_{UTP}$) and the lower threshold voltage ($V_{LTP}$). It is essential because ordinary comparators suffer from **chattering** (rapid unwanted switching) when the input hovers near the threshold due to noise. Hysteresis creates a **dead band** that rejects noise smaller than the hysteresis width, producing a clean, stable digital output.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the differences between a simple comparator and a Schmitt Trigger.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple Comparator | Schmitt Trigger |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback type | No feedback (open loop) | **Positive feedback** |
| Switching threshold | **Single threshold** (0 V) | **Two thresholds** ($V_{UTP}$, $V_{LTP}$) |
| Hysteresis | **Absent** | **Present** ($V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$) |
| Noise immunity | **Poor** (chatters near threshold) | **Excellent** (dead band rejects noise) |
| Output transitions | Slow, may be noisy | **Sharp and clean** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always show the feedback fraction** $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ explicitly in your derivation — students who skip this lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Sign convention is critical:** $V_{UTP}$ is **positive**, $V_{LTP}$ is **negative**. Mixing up signs loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Use $\pm V_{sat}$**, NOT the supply voltage $\pm V_{CC}$, in your formulas. The op-amp saturates at $\sim 1$–2 V below the rails.
> 4. **Mention the µA741 saturation value** explicitly in numerical problems (typically $\pm 13$ V for $\pm 15$ V supply).
> 5. **Drawing the hysteresis loop** on the input-output axes is mandatory — partial credit (2 marks) is awarded even if the derivation has errors.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** using an op-amp. Derive expressions for the upper and lower threshold voltages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an Inverting Schmitt Trigger, $R_1 = 15\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1.5\text{ k}\Omega$, and $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V. Calculate the threshold voltages, hysteresis width, and sketch the transfer characteristic. A triangular wave of $\pm 5$ V amplitude is applied. Will the output be a clean square wave? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The Inverting Schmitt Trigger has the input $V_{in}$ applied to the inverting terminal ($V^-$) and a positive feedback network of $R_1$ and $R_2$ from output to non-inverting terminal ($V^+$). The feedback fraction is:

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Upper Threshold Voltage** (output transitions from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ when $V_{in}$ rises):

$$V_{UTP} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Lower Threshold Voltage** (output transitions from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$ when $V_{in}$ falls):

$$V_{LTP} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Feedback fraction:**

$$\beta = \frac{1.5\text{ k}\Omega}{15\text{ k}\Omega + 1.5\text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{1.5}{16.5} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

**Step 2 — Threshold voltages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UTP} &= +\frac{1}{11} \times 13 \approx +1.18\text{ V} \\
V_{LTP} &= -\frac{1}{11} \times 13 \approx -1.18\text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Hysteresis width:**

$$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 1.18 - (-1.18) = 2.36\text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Check input compatibility:**

The triangular input has amplitude $\pm 5$ V, which **comfortably exceeds** both $|V_{UTP}|$ and $|V_{LTP}|$ of $\pm 1.18$ V. Therefore, the input crosses both thresholds during each cycle.

**Conclusion:** Yes, the output is a clean square wave alternating between $+13$ V and $-13$ V, with a **noise immunity of 2.36 V**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **hysteresis** in a Schmitt Trigger. How does the **positive feedback** enable sharp switching transitions? Derive the expression for the hysteresis width. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger to obtain a hysteresis width of **4 V** using a µA741 op-amp with $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V. Choose a suitable value of $R_1$ and determine $R_2$. Verify by computing the threshold voltages. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Conceptual + Derivation (7 marks):**

**Hysteresis** is the property of a system where the output state depends not only on the current input but also on the **history of the input** (its previous state). In a Schmitt Trigger, hysteresis manifests as **two different switching thresholds** — one for the rising input and one for the falling input.

**Positive feedback** works as follows: when $V_{in}$ approaches a threshold by a tiny amount $\epsilon$, the differential input becomes $\epsilon$, producing an output change of $A_{OL} \cdot \epsilon$. This change is fed back to the non-inverting terminal, which **further increases** the differential input. This **regenerative loop** drives the output to saturation almost instantaneously, producing the **sharp vertical edge** characteristic of the Schmitt Trigger.

**Hysteresis width derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} \\
&= \beta V_{sat} - (-\beta V_{sat}) \\
&= 2\beta V_{sat} = \frac{2R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Design Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_H = 4$ V, $V_{sat} = 13$ V.

**Step 1 — Solve for $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{V_H}{2 V_{sat}} = \frac{4}{2 \times 13} = \frac{4}{26} = \frac{2}{13} \approx 0.1538$$

**Step 2 — Choose $R_1$:**

Select $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value, satisfies $\geq 10\text{ k}\Omega$ load constraint).

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \\
\frac{2}{13} &= \frac{R_2}{10\text{ k}\Omega + R_2} \\
2(10\text{ k}\Omega + R_2) &= 13 R_2 \\
20\text{ k}\Omega + 2R_2 &= 13 R_2 \\
20\text{ k}\Omega &= 11 R_2 \\
R_2 &= \frac{20\text{ k}\Omega}{11} \approx 1.818\text{ k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Use the nearest standard value: **$R_2 = 1.8\text{ k}\Omega$** (E12 series).

**Step 4 — Verify by computing threshold voltages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{actual} &= \frac{1.8}{10 + 1.8} = \frac{1.8}{11.8} \approx 0.1525 \\
V_{UTP} &= +0.1525 \times 13 \approx +1.98\text{ V} \\
V_{LTP} &= -0.1525 \times 13 \approx -1.98\text{ V} \\
V_H &= 1.98 - (-1.98) = 3.96\text{ V} \approx 4\text{ V} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Bulletins — Memorize These Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- **Schmitt Trigger** = Comparator + **Positive Feedback** (regenerative).
- **Feedback fraction** $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ is the **single most important quantity**.
- **UTP** $= +\beta V_{sat}$ ; **LTP** $= -\beta V_{sat}$ — remember the **signs**.
- **Hysteresis width** $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$ — directly proportional to $V_{sat}$.
- **Inverting type** → input at Pin 2 (inverting), feedback at Pin 3 (non-inverting).
- **Non-inverting type** → input at Pin 3 (non-inverting), feedback at Pin 2 (inverting).
- **µA741** saturation for $\pm 15$ V supply is approximately **$\pm 13$ V** (NOT $\pm 15$ V).
- **Slew rate** of 741 is **$0.5\text{ V/}\mu s$** — limits max switching frequency.
- **Hysteresis loop** in transfer characteristic is a **clockwise rectangle** (for inverting type).
- **Duty cycle** for symmetric triangular/sinusoidal input is **50%** by symmetry.
- **Noise immunity** = hysteresis width $V_H$ — any noise smaller than this is rejected.
- **No coupling capacitor** is needed at the input for the inverting type (DC coupled).
- **Astabilization of the Schmitt Trigger** (when combined with an RC network) is the basis of the **op-amp astable multivibrator** (Experiment 3).
- **Draw the waveform diagrams** in your answer — the input sine/triangle and the output square wave **must be in time alignment** (same x-axis).
- **Lab viva tip:** If the examiner asks *"What happens if $R_2 = 0$?"* — answer: **the circuit becomes a simple comparator with no hysteresis**, and the op-amp output may oscillate in the linear region if the input is at the threshold (slewing back and forth).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — Schmitt Trigger Circuit | Module Experiment 2 | PCECL407**
> *Best of luck with your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations!*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Multivibrators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Multivibrators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Multivibrators?

A **Multivibrator** is an electronic sequential switching circuit that employs two amplifying devices (transistors, op-amps, or 555 timer comparators) cross-coupled by resistive or capacitive elements to produce stable, quasi-stable, or unstable (oscillating) output states. The circuit transitions between these states based on threshold triggering and is the fundamental building block of digital timing, pulse generation, and waveform-shaping subsystems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Multivibrators are regenerative switching circuits used to generate non-sinusoidal waveforms (square, rectangular, or pulse) characterized by sharp transitions between two distinct voltage levels."

### 1.2 The Three Classes of Multivibrators

| Type | Number of Stable States | Application | Waveform |
|------|------------------------|-------------|----------|
| **Bistable** | Two | Flip-Flops, Latches, Memory | Triggered output |
| **Monostable** | One | Pulse Stretcher, Timer | Single pulse |
| **Astable** | Zero (free-running) | Clock Generator, Oscillator | Continuous square wave |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Focus (PCECL407):** Module Experiment 2 specifically deals with the realization of **Astable** and **Monostable** multivibrators using the **IC 555 Timer** in its standard bipolar configuration.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Bucket System"

Imagine a **bucket with two holes**:
- **Astable Multivibrator = Automatic Faucet:** Water (charge) flows in, fills up to a high level (upper threshold), then drains automatically. The cycle repeats forever — no human intervention. This represents the **free-running oscillator** that produces continuous square waves.
- **Monostable Multivibrator = One-Shot Water Cannon:** The cannon sits idle (stable state). When a person shouts (trigger pulse), it fires *one* jet of water (timed pulse) and returns to idle. It waits for the next shout. This represents a **single pulse generator**.

> [!NOTE]
> The 555 Timer IC is essentially a combination of two comparators, an SR flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider — all packaged into an 8-pin chip that *acts* as the "brain" of both multivibrators.

### 1.4 The 555 Timer IC — Pin Configuration

```
            ┌──────────────┐
   GND  ──► │ 1          8 │ ◄── VCC (+5V to +15V)
            │              │
TRIG  ──►  │ 2          7 │ ◄── DISCHARGE
            │              │
  OUT  ◄── │ 3          6 │ ◄── THRESHOLD
            │              │
  RESET ►  │ 4          5 │ ◄── CONTROL VOLTAGE
            └──────────────┘
```

- **Pin 1 (GND):** Ground reference
- **Pin 2 (TRIGGER):** Active-low input; triggers when voltage drops below $V_{CC}/3$
- **Pin 3 (OUT):** Output pin (sources/sinks up to **200 mA**)
- **Pin 4 (RESET):** Active-low master reset
- **Pin 5 (CONTROL VOLTAGE):** Externally modulates threshold levels (default: $V_{CC}/2$)
- **Pin 6 (THRESHOLD):** Active-high input; resets when voltage rises above $2V_{CC}/3$
- **Pin 7 (DISCHARGE):** Open-collector transistor; sinks current from external timing capacitor
- **Pin 8 (VCC):** Supply voltage (**+5V to +15V**, typical)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Astable Multivibrator — Operational Theory

The Astable (free-running) multivibrator has **no stable state**. It continuously switches between HIGH and LOW, producing a rectangular wave without any external trigger.

**Internal Block Architecture of 555 in Astable Mode:**

1. **Voltage Divider Network:** Three equal internal resistors (each $5\,\text{k}\Omega$) divide $V_{CC}$ into two reference voltages:
   - Upper Threshold Reference: $V_{TH(REF)} = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$
   - Lower Trigger Reference: $V_{TR(REF)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$

2. **Two Comparators:**
   - Upper Comparator compares Pin 6 (Threshold) with $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$
   - Lower Comparator compares Pin 2 (Trigger) with $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$

3. **SR Flip-Flop:** Receives comparator outputs and toggles the output state.
