4. **Discharge Transistor (Pin 7):** When Flip-Flop is RESET, transistor turns ON and shorts the timing capacitor to ground.

### 2.2 Astable — Step-by-Step Charging/Discharging Logic

**External Components:** $R_A$, $R_B$, and $C$ form the timing network.

> **Charging Phase (Output HIGH):**
> - Capacitor $C$ charges through $R_A + R_B$ toward $V_{CC}$.
> - Voltage across $C$ rises exponentially from $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$.
> - Output remains HIGH.

> **Discharging Phase (Output LOW):**
> - When $V_C \geq \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the discharge transistor (Pin 7) turns ON.
> - Capacitor $C$ discharges through $R_B$ only (because Pin 7 shorts past $R_A$).
> - Voltage across $C$ falls from $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ back to $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$.
> - Output remains LOW.
> - The cycle repeats indefinitely.

### 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Operational Theory

The Monostable (one-shot) multivibrator has **one stable state** (Output LOW) and **one quasi-stable state** (Output HIGH). It remains in the stable state until an external negative trigger pulse is applied at Pin 2.

**External Components:** $R$ and $C$ form the timing network (no second resistor needed).

> **Stable State (Idle):**
> - Output is LOW; discharge transistor is ON.
> - Capacitor $C$ is held at $\approx 0\,\text{V}$ through Pin 7.

> **Triggered (Quasi-Stable) State:**
> - A negative pulse at Pin 2 (below $V_{CC}/3$) sets the Flip-Flop.
> - Output goes HIGH; discharge transistor turns OFF.
> - Capacitor $C$ charges through $R$ toward $V_{CC}$.
> - When $V_C$ reaches $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the upper comparator resets the Flip-Flop.
> - Output returns LOW; discharge transistor turns ON; $C$ rapidly discharges to 0V.
> - Circuit waits for the next trigger.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| Parameter | Astable Multivibrator | Monostable Multivibrator |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Charging Time $t_H$** | $0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | $1.099 \cdot R \cdot C \approx 1.1 \cdot RC$ |
| **Discharging Time $t_L$** | $0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | (Negligible — rapid reset) |
| **Total Period $T$** | $0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$ | $1.099 \cdot R \cdot C$ |
| **Frequency $f$** | $\dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1.1 \cdot RC}$ |
| **Duty Cycle $D$** | $\dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | $100\%$ (single pulse) |
| **Pulse Width $W$** | — | $1.1 \cdot RC$ |
| **Output HIGH Level** | $\approx V_{CC}$ | $\approx V_{CC}$ |
| **Output LOW Level** | $\approx 0\,\text{V}$ (GND) | $\approx 0\,\text{V}$ (GND) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant:** The factor $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ appears because the capacitor charges from $V_{CC}/3$ to $2V_{CC}/3$ — exactly **one time constant of decay range** corresponding to $\ln(2)$.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Astable:** Used in **LED flashers, clock pulse generators for digital logic, PWM control circuits, tone generators for alarms**, and **function generator ICs**.
- **Monostable:** Used in **debouncing switch circuits, missing pulse detectors, frequency dividers, time-delay relays, and digital logic sequencers**.

> [!NOTE]
> A **50% duty cycle Astable** is achieved when $R_B \gg R_A$, but it can *never* reach exactly 50% because $R_A$ is in the charging path. For a true 50% duty cycle, a **diode** is placed in parallel with $R_B$ to bypass it during charging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Astable Frequency

**Step 1: Capacitor Charging Equation (during HIGH output)**

The capacitor charges from $V_{CC}/3$ toward $V_{CC}$ through the equivalent resistance $(R_A + R_B)$. The general charging equation for a capacitor in an RC network is:

$$V_C(t) = V_{final} - (V_{final} - V_{initial}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Boundary Conditions**

- Initial voltage: $V_{initial} = V_{CC}/3$
- Final voltage: $V_{final} = V_{CC}$
- Time constant: $\tau = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$

Substituting:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}\right) \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Trigger Condition**

The capacitor voltage reaches the upper threshold when $V_C(t) = 2V_{CC}/3$. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 4: Solve Algebraically for $t_H$**

Rearrange the equation:

$$\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]} = V_{CC} - \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$$

$$e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-\dfrac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B)C} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{t_H = \ln(2) \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C}$$

> This is the **HIGH time** of the astable output.

**Step 5: Derive the Discharge Time $t_L$**

During the LOW phase, the discharge transistor shorts Pin 7 to ground, so the capacitor discharges through $R_B$ only (the current bypasses $R_A$). The capacitor discharges from $2V_{CC}/3$ toward $0\,\text{V}$:

$$V_C(t) = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$

The discharge ends when $V_C(t) = V_{CC}/3$:

$$\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3} = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_L/(R_B C)}$$

$$e^{-t_L/(R_B C)} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{t_L = \ln(2) \cdot R_B \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C}$$

**Step 6: Derive the Total Period $T$**

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C + 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$$

$$T = 0.693 \cdot C \cdot (R_A + R_B + R_B)$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$$

**Step 7: Derive the Frequency of Oscillation $f$**

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$$

Since $\dfrac{1}{0.693} \approx 1.443 \approx 1.44$:

$$\boxed{f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}}$$

> This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the astable frequency. The unit of $f$ is **Hertz (Hz)** when $R$ is in Ohms and $C$ is in Farads.

**Step 8: Derive the Duty Cycle $D$**

$$D = \dfrac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C}{0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%}$$

> The duty cycle is **always greater than 50%** in the standard astable configuration because $R_A$ contributes to charging but not discharging.

### 3.2 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width

**Step 1: Set Up the Charging Equation**

When triggered, the discharge transistor is OFF. The capacitor $C$ charges from $0\,\text{V}$ (the stable-state value) through resistor $R$ toward $V_{CC}$.

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right)$$

**Step 2: Apply the Quasi-Stable End Condition**

The quasi-stable state ends when $V_C(t) = 2V_{CC}/3$ (the upper threshold reference):

$$\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-W/(RC)}\right)$$

**Step 3: Solve for $W$**

$$\dfrac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-W/(RC)}$$

$$e^{-W/(RC)} = 1 - \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$-\dfrac{W}{RC} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln(3)$$

Since $\ln(3) \approx 1.0986$:

$$W = \ln(3) \cdot R \cdot C \approx 1.0986 \cdot RC$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 1.1 \cdot RC}$$

> This is the **standard KTU monostable pulse width formula**.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 555 to generate a frequency of **1 kHz** with a duty cycle of approximately **75%**. Assume $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ and $V_{CC} = 12\,\text{V}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Use the Frequency Formula**

$$f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$$

$$1000 = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot (0.1 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$R_A + 2R_B = \dfrac{1.44}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.44}{10^{-4}} = 14{,}400\,\Omega = 14.4\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Use the Duty Cycle Formula**

$$D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.75$$

$$R_A + R_B = 0.75 \times 14.4\,\text{k}\Omega = 10.8\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Solve the Two Simultaneous Equations**

- Equation (1): $R_A + 2R_B = 14.4\,\text{k}\Omega$
- Equation (2): $R_A + R_B = 10.8\,\text{k}\Omega$

Subtracting (2) from (1):

$$R_B = 14.4 - 10.8 = 3.6\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Substituting back:

$$R_A = 10.8 - 3.6 = 7.2\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{R_A = 7.2\,\text{k}\Omega,\ R_B = 3.6\,\text{k}\Omega,\ C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}}$

**Verification:**
- $T = 0.693 \times (7.2 + 7.2) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3} = 0.693 \times 14.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.998\,\text{ms}$
- $f = 1/T = 1002\,\text{Hz} \approx 1\,\text{kHz}$ ✓
- $D = (7.2 + 3.6)/(7.2 + 7.2) = 10.8/14.4 = 75\%$ ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Astable | Monostable |
|---------|---------|------------|
| External Trigger Required | No | Yes (negative pulse to Pin 2) |
| Number of Resistors | 2 ($R_A$, $R_B$) | 1 ($R$) |
| Number of Capacitors | 1 (timing) | 1 (timing) |
| Output Waveform | Continuous square wave | Single pulse on demand |
| Reset Behavior | Continuous (self-resetting) | Manual via Pin 4 |
| Frequency Stability | Moderate (depends on $R$, $C$ tolerance) | Pulse width stable to $\pm 2\%$ |
| Trigger Pulse Width Constraint | Not applicable | Must be < Output pulse width |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 555 Timer Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC_node[VCC Supply] --> divider[Resistive Voltage Divider]
    divider --> refUp[Upper Reference 2VCC over 3]
    divider --> refLow[Lower Reference VCC over 3]
    refUp --> compUp[Upper Comparator]
    refLow --> compLow[Lower Comparator]
    pin6[Pin 6 Threshold] --> compUp
    pin2[Pin 2 Trigger] --> compLow
    compUp --> flipflop[SR Flip Flop]
    compLow --> flipflop
    flipflop --> outDriver[Output Buffer]
    outDriver --> pin3[Pin 3 Output]
    flipflop --> disch[Discharge Transistor]
    disch --> pin7[Pin 7 Discharge]
    pin4[Pin 4 Reset] --> flipflop
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — Astable Operation Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Charging
    Charging: Charging via RA and RB
    Charging: Capacitor rises from VCC/3 to 2VCC/3
    Charging: Output HIGH
    Charging --> Discharging: VC reaches 2VCC/3
    Discharging: Discharging via RB only
    Discharging: Capacitor falls from 2VCC/3 to VCC/3
    Discharging: Output LOW
    Discharging --> Charging: VC reaches VCC/3
    Charging --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Functional Flow — Monostable Operation Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stable
    Stable: Output LOW
    Stable: Capacitor shorted to ground
    Stable: Waiting for trigger
    Stable --> Quasi: Negative trigger at Pin 2
    Quasi: Output HIGH
    Quasi: Capacitor charging via R
    Quasi: Capacitor rises from 0V to 2VCC/3
    Quasi --> Stable: VC reaches 2VCC/3
    Stable --> Quasi: Next trigger pulse
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Astable Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Supply["Power Supply Unit"]
        VCC_src[VCC +12V]
        GND_src[GND 0V]
    end
    subgraph TimerIC["IC 555 Timer Module"]
        pin8_in[VCC Pin 8]
        pin1_in[GND Pin 1]
        pin7_node[Discharge Pin 7]
        pin6_node[Threshold Pin 6]
        pin2_node[Trigger Pin 2]
        pin3_node[Output Pin 3]
        pin4_node[Reset Pin 4]
        pin5_node[Control Pin 5]
    end
    subgraph TimingNet["External Timing Network"]
        RA_node[RA Resistor]
        RB_node[RB Resistor]
        C_node[Timing Capacitor C]
    end
    subgraph Output["Load and Indicator"]
        LED_node[LED Indicator]
        CRO_node[CRO Probe]
    end
    VCC_src --> pin8_in
    GND_src --> pin1_in
    pin8_in --> RA_node
    RA_node --> RB_node
    RB_node --> pin7_node
    RB_node --> pin6_node
    RB_node --> pin2_node
    pin6_node --> C_node
    pin2_node --> C_node
    C_node --> GND_src
    pin3_node --> LED_node
    pin3_node --> CRO_node
    pin4_node --> VCC_src
    pin5_node --> GND_src
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** $R_A$ connects $V_{CC}$ to the node containing Pin 7, Pin 6, and Pin 2. $R_B$ connects this same node to $C$ (which goes to ground). The capacitor voltage is sensed by both the threshold (Pin 6) and trigger (Pin 2) inputs — the dual sensing is what enables automatic oscillation.

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Monostable Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Supply2["Power Supply Unit"]
        VCC_src2[VCC +5V to +15V]
        GND_src2[GND 0V]
    end
    subgraph TimerIC2["IC 555 Timer Module"]
        pin8_in2[VCC Pin 8]
        pin1_in2[GND Pin 1]
        pin7_node2[Discharge Pin 7]
        pin6_node2[Threshold Pin 6]
        pin2_node2[Trigger Pin 2]
        pin3_node2[Output Pin 3]
        pin4_node2[Reset Pin 4]
        pin5_node2[Control Pin 5]
    end
    subgraph TimingNet2["External Timing Network"]
        R_node[Timing Resistor R]
        C_node2[Timing Capacitor C]
    end
    subgraph Trigger["External Trigger Source"]
        Trig_src[Negative Pulse Generator]
    end
    subgraph Output2["Output Monitor"]
        CRO_node2[CRO or Logic Analyzer]
    end
    VCC_src2 --> pin8_in2
    GND_src2 --> pin1_in2
    VCC_src2 --> R_node
    R_node --> pin7_node2
    R_node --> pin6_node2
    pin7_node2 --> C_node2
    pin6_node2 --> C_node2
    C_node2 --> GND_src2
    Trig_src --> pin2_node2
    pin3_node2 --> CRO_node2
    pin4_node2 --> VCC_src2
    pin5_node2 --> GND_src2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the structural difference from the astable: there is **no $R_B$**. The resistor $R$ connects directly from $V_{CC}$ to the discharge pin, and the capacitor $C$ charges through this single resistor. Pin 2 receives an external negative trigger pulse from a separate function generator or push-button debounce circuit.

### 4.6 Expected Output Waveforms

| Circuit | Waveform Type | Shape Description |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Astable Output** | Continuous Square Wave | Symmetric or asymmetric rectangular pulses alternating between $V_{CC}$ and $0\,\text{V}$ |
| **Astable Capacitor** | Exponential Charging/Discharging | Sawtooth-like waveform with two exponential segments between $V_{CC}/3$ and $2V_{CC}/3$ |
| **Monostable Output** | Single Pulse | Returns HIGH on trigger, stays HIGH for $W \approx 1.1RC$ seconds, then returns LOW |
| **Monostable Capacitor** | Exponential Charging | Rises from $0\,\text{V}$ to $2V_{CC}/3$ during the pulse duration |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1.** What is a multivibrator? Name the three basic types and state the number of stable states in each.

**Model Answer:**
A multivibrator is a regenerative electronic switching circuit that produces non-sinusoidal waveforms such as square, rectangular, or pulse waves. The three basic types are:
- **Bistable Multivibrator** — 2 stable states (used in flip-flops and memory storage)
- **Monostable Multivibrator** — 1 stable state (used as one-shot pulse generator)
- **Astable Multivibrator** — 0 stable states (used as free-running oscillator)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**

**Q2.** Why is the 555 timer preferred over a transistor-based multivibrator in modern applications?

**Model Answer:**
The 555 timer is preferred because:
1. It integrates two comparators, an SR flip-flop, and a discharge transistor into a single 8-pin IC, reducing component count.
2. It offers high output current capability (**up to 200 mA**), directly driving loads like LEDs, relays, and speakers.
3. It provides excellent temperature stability and predictable timing.
4. It operates over a wide supply voltage range (**+5V to +18V**).
5. It eliminates the need for matched transistor pairs required in traditional multivibrators.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **astable multivibrator** using IC 555 timer. Derive the expressions for the **frequency of oscillation** and **duty cycle**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:**
The astable circuit uses IC 555 with two resistors $R_A$ and $R_B$ and a timing capacitor $C$. $R_A$ is connected between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7 (Discharge). $R_B$ is connected between Pin 7 and Pin 6 (Threshold) and Pin 2 (Trigger) junction. The capacitor $C$ connects from this junction to ground. Output is taken from Pin 3.

**Operation:**

The 555 IC contains two comparators that sense the capacitor voltage $V_C$ against two reference voltages:
- Upper threshold: $V_{TH} = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$
- Lower threshold: $V_{TR} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$

**Phase 1 — Charging (Output HIGH):**
Initially, suppose $V_C < V_{CC}/3$. The lower comparator triggers the flip-flop, setting the output HIGH and turning OFF the discharge transistor (Pin 7). The capacitor $C$ charges through the series combination $(R_A + R_B)$ toward $V_{CC}$. The output remains HIGH until $V_C$ reaches $2V_{CC}/3$.

**Phase 2 — Discharging (Output LOW):**
When $V_C \geq 2V_{CC}/3$, the upper comparator resets the flip-flop, making the output LOW and turning ON the discharge transistor. The capacitor now discharges through $R_B$ only (since Pin 7 is grounded internally). The output remains LOW until $V_C$ falls to $V_{CC}/3$, at which point the cycle restarts.

**Derivation of Frequency:**

The HIGH time (charging from $V_{CC}/3$ to $2V_{CC}/3$):

$$t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$$

The LOW time (discharging from $2V_{CC}/3$ to $V_{CC}/3$ through $R_B$ only):

$$t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$$

Total period:

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$$

Frequency:

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$$

**Duty Cycle:**

$$D = \dfrac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$$

**(b)** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 555 to generate a **1 kHz** square wave with a duty cycle of **60%**. Assume $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 1000\,\text{Hz}$, $D = 60\% = 0.6$, $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\,\text{F}$

**Step 1: Use the Frequency Formula**

$$1000 = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot 10^{-8}}$$

$$R_A + 2R_B = \dfrac{1.44}{1000 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{1.44}{10^{-5}} = 144{,}000\,\Omega = 144\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Use the Duty Cycle Formula**

$$0.6 = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{144\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

$$R_A + R_B = 0.6 \times 144\,\text{k}\Omega = 86.4\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Solve Simultaneously**

- Equation (1): $R_A + 2R_B = 144\,\text{k}\Omega$
- Equation (2): $R_A + R_B = 86.4\,\text{k}\Omega$

Subtracting:

$$R_B = 144 - 86.4 = 57.6\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_A = 86.4 - 57.6 = 28.8\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Final Component Values:**

$$\boxed{R_A = 28.8\,\text{k}\Omega,\ R_B = 57.6\,\text{k}\Omega,\ C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}}$$

**Verification:**
- $t_H = 0.693 \times (28.8 + 57.6) \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 0.693 \times 86.4 \times 10^{-5} = 598.75\,\mu\text{s}$
- $t_L = 0.693 \times 57.6 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 399.17\,\mu\text{s}$
- $T = 598.75 + 399.17 = 997.92\,\mu\text{s}$
- $f = 1/997.92\,\mu\text{s} = 1002\,\text{Hz} \approx 1\,\text{kHz}$ ✓
- $D = 598.75/997.92 = 60\%$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **monostable multivibrator** using IC 555 with a neat circuit diagram and waveforms. Derive the expression for the **pulse width**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**
In the monostable configuration, IC 555 is connected with one external resistor $R$ and one timing capacitor $C$. The resistor $R$ is connected between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7 (Discharge). The capacitor $C$ connects from Pin 6 (Threshold) and Pin 7 junction to ground. Pin 2 (Trigger) receives an external negative trigger pulse.

**Operation:**

**Stable State:**
In the absence of a trigger, the output is LOW and the discharge transistor is ON, keeping the capacitor voltage at approximately $0\,\text{V}$ (effectively shorted to ground). The circuit remains in this stable state indefinitely.

**Quasi-Stable State (Triggered):**
When a negative-going trigger pulse of amplitude less than $V_{CC}/3$ is applied to Pin 2, the lower comparator sets the flip-flop. This:
1. Makes the output HIGH
2. Turns OFF the discharge transistor

The capacitor $C$ now starts charging from $0\,\text{V}$ through $R$ toward $V_{CC}$. The output remains HIGH as long as $V_C < 2V_{CC}/3$. When $V_C$ reaches the upper threshold $2V_{CC}/3$, the upper comparator resets the flip-flop, returning the output to LOW and turning the discharge transistor ON. The capacitor rapidly discharges through Pin 7 to ground, and the circuit returns to its stable state, waiting for the next trigger.

**Derivation of Pulse Width $W$:**

The capacitor charges from $0\,\text{V}$ toward $V_{CC}$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right)$$

At $t = W$, $V_C = 2V_{CC}/3$:

$$\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-W/(RC)}\right)$$

$$e^{-W/(RC)} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$W = RC \cdot \ln(3) = 1.0986 \cdot RC$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 1.1 \cdot RC}$$

**(b)** A monostable multivibrator using IC 555 has $R = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 10\,\mu\text{F}$. Calculate the **pulse width**, the **time period** (assuming the trigger is applied periodically every 1 second), and the **duty cycle** of the output waveform. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 100\,\text{k}\Omega = 10^5\,\Omega$, $C = 10\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-5}\,\text{F}$, Trigger Period $T_{trig} = 1\,\text{s}$

**Step 1: Calculate the Pulse Width**

$$W = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C = 1.1 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-5} = 1.1\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Time Period of Output**

Since the trigger is applied every 1 second, the output period equals the trigger period:

$$T_{out} = 1\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Duty Cycle**

$$D = \dfrac{W}{T_{out}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1.1}{1.0} \times 100\% = 110\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Critical Pitfall:** A duty cycle of **110% is physically impossible** (cannot exceed 100%). This means the **trigger period is too short** for the chosen $R$ and $C$ values. The circuit will fail because the monostable output is still HIGH when the next trigger arrives — the trigger pulse will be **ignored** because Pin 2 is already in the triggered condition. **To fix this design, the student must either increase the trigger period to at least 1.1 seconds OR reduce $R \cdot C$ so that $W < 1\,\text{s}$.** This is a classic KTU trap question — students who blindly compute the formula without checking physical validity will lose 2-3 marks.

**Corrected Design:** If we keep the trigger period at 1 s, the maximum allowable pulse width is just under 1 s. Choosing $R = 47\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 10\,\mu\text{F}$:

$$W = 1.1 \times 47 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.517\,\text{s}$$

$$D = \dfrac{0.517}{1.0} \times 100\% = 51.7\% \quad \text{(Valid design)} \checkmark$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls Where KTU Students Frequently Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_A$ and $R_B$ Roles (2-mark deduction):** $R_A$ is the resistor *between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7*. $R_B$ is *between Pin 7 and the capacitor*. Many students reverse these in derivations.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\ln(2)$ Origin (1-mark deduction):** When asked to "derive" the frequency, simply writing the formula $f = 1.44/[(R_A + 2R_B)C]$ without showing the charging/discharging exponential equations and the application of $V_C = 2V_{CC}/3$ boundary condition will cost you the derivation marks.
>
> 3. **Monostable Stable State Misconception (2-mark deduction):** The stable state of a monostable is **LOW**, not HIGH. Some textbooks (and many students) mistakenly believe the stable state is HIGH. The HIGH state is *quasi-stable* — it exists only temporarily during the timing period.
>
> 4. **Duty Cycle Upper Limit (2-mark deduction):** For the standard astable, $D > 50\%$ always. If a numerical answer yields $D < 50\%$, the student has swapped $R_A$ and $R_B$ in the formula.
>
> 5. **Missing the 50% Duty Cycle Trick (1-mark deduction):** When asked how to achieve exactly 50% duty cycle, students often forget to mention the **diode bypass across $R_B$**.
>
> 6. **Pin 2 Trigger Polarity (1-mark deduction):** The trigger is **active-LOW** (negative pulse), not active-HIGH. A common KTU question asks "What type of pulse triggers Pin 2?"

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Astable and Monostable Multivibrators using 555 Timer**

- [ ] **Astable** = No stable state, **Monostable** = One stable state (LOW), **Bistable** = Two stable states.
- [ ] **555 Pin Reference Voltages:** Upper $= 2V_{CC}/3$, Lower $= V_{CC}/3$ (set by internal three-resistor divider).
- [ ] **Astable Frequency Formula:** $f = 1.44 / [(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C]$ — most tested formula in KTU exams.
- [ ] **Astable Duty Cycle:** $D = (R_A + R_B)/(R_A + 2R_B) \times 100\%$ — always greater than **50%** in basic configuration.
- [ ] **Monostable Pulse Width:** $W = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ — derived from $\ln(3) \cdot RC$.
- [ ] **Astable HIGH Time:** $t_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ — charging through both resistors.
- [ ] **Astable LOW Time:** $t_L = 0.693 R_B C$ — discharging through $R_B$ only (Pin 7 short-circuits $R_A$).
- [ ] **Discharge Pin (Pin 7)** is an **open-collector** transistor that goes LOW to discharge the timing capacitor.
- [ ] **Monostable Trigger:** Active-LOW negative pulse at Pin 2 with amplitude **less than $V_{CC}/3$**.
- [ ] **Reset Pin (Pin 4):** Active-LOW master reset; must be tied to $V_{CC}$ if unused, or the IC remains disabled.
- [ ] **Control Voltage Pin (Pin 5):** Can be used to modulate the threshold levels externally; usually decoupled to ground via a $0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ capacitor.
- [ ] **For 50% duty cycle astable:** Add a **diode** (anode at Pin 7, cathode at Pin 6 junction) in parallel with $R_B$ to bypass $R_B$ during charging.
- [ ] **Minimum trigger pulse width** for monostable: must be **shorter than** the output pulse width $W$; otherwise the circuit misbehaves.
- [ ] **Recovery time** of monostable: Time taken for $C$ to discharge from $2V_{CC}/3$ to $0\,\text{V}$ — typically negligible but must be allowed before next trigger.
- [ ] **Unit reminder:** $R$ in Ohms ($\Omega$), $C$ in Farads (F), $f$ in Hertz (Hz), $T$ in seconds (s), $W$ in seconds (s).
- [ ] **Output current capability:** 555 can source/sink up to **200 mA** — sufficient to drive LEDs, small relays, and speakers directly.
- [ ] **Supply voltage range:** **+4.5V to +15V** (or up to +18V for some variants like TLC555).
- [ ] **Practical timing capacitor range:** $0.001\,\mu\text{F}$ to $1000\,\mu\text{F}$ (covering Hz to MHz range).
- [ ] **KTU Viva-Voce Favorite Questions:** "What is the function of the discharge transistor?", "Why is the duty cycle always greater than 50% in the basic astable?", "What is the role of the control voltage pin?", "How does temperature affect 555 timing accuracy?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU lab examinations, you may be asked to **draw the circuit diagram from memory**, **calculate component values for a given frequency and duty cycle**, and **identify the output waveform on a CRO**. Practice all three — the circuit diagram accounts for 3-4 marks, the calculation for 4-5 marks, and waveform identification for 2-3 marks in a typical 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform Generators using Op Amps - Triangular and Sawtooth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Waveform Generators using Op Amps - Triangular and Sawtooth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Waveform Generator** is an electronic circuit that produces repetitive, non-sinusoidal waveforms (such as square, triangular, or sawtooth waves) without requiring an external input signal, i.e., it functions as an **oscillator** with a defined frequency of oscillation, amplitude, and duty cycle.

In the KTU 2024 Linear Integrated Circuits Lab, two specific non-sinusoidal waveform generators are implemented using **operational amplifiers (op-amps)**:

- **Triangular Wave Generator**: A circuit that produces a symmetrical, linearly rising and falling periodic waveform with equal rise time ($T_{rise}$) and fall time ($T_{fall}$). Mathematically, it has an amplitude $V_p$ and a period $T = T_{rise} + T_{fall}$.
- **Sawtooth Wave Generator**: A circuit that produces a non-symmetrical ramp waveform with **unequal rise and fall times**, characterized by a slow linear ramp followed by an abrupt, near-vertical drop (or vice versa). It is widely used in **time-base circuits** of oscilloscopes and **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) controllers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both generators are constructed by **cascading two fundamental op-amp building blocks**: a **Schmitt Trigger (Comparator with positive feedback)** followed by an **Active Integrator**. The Schmitt trigger generates a square wave that is then integrated to yield the desired waveform shape.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **filling and emptying a water tank** connected to a swing door.

- The **Schmitt trigger** (comparator) acts like the **swing door mechanism**: as the water level in the tank rises and crosses a high threshold, the door abruptly flips in the opposite direction, dumping the water. As the level drops below a low threshold, the door flips back. This abrupt, two-state behavior generates a **square wave** pattern (door fully open ↔ fully closed).

- The **integrator** acts like the **tank itself**: the door position (constant in magnitude, either fully open or fully closed) is analogous to a **constant input current** charging a capacitor. The water level rises (or falls) **linearly** because the input is constant, not exponential. When the door reverses, the direction of change reverses.

- For a **triangular wave**, the door spends **equal time open in each direction** → the tank fills and empties at the same rate, giving a **symmetric ramp-up and ramp-down**.

- For a **sawtooth wave**, the door mechanism is **asymmetric**: it stays open longer in one direction than the other, causing the water level to rise slowly but drop quickly (or vice versa).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Conditions** used in KTU lab manuals and textbooks:
> - **Op-amp supply voltages:** typically **$\pm 12\text{ V}$** or **$\pm 15\text{ V}$**
> - **Saturation voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx \pm V_{CC}$ (typically **$\pm 11\text{ V}$ to $\pm 14\text{ V}$** for IC 741)
> - **Output amplitude is self-regulated** by the saturation limits of the op-amp and the feedback fraction $\beta$.

### 1.3 Block-Level Architecture

The complete waveform generator system is fundamentally a **two-stage cascaded negative feedback loop**:

$$\text{Schmitt Trigger} \longrightarrow \text{Active Integrator} \longrightarrow \text{Output (Triangular/Sawtooth)}$$

- The **integrator output** is fed back to the **inverting input of the Schmitt trigger**.
- The **Schmitt trigger output** is fed into the **input of the integrator**.
- This forms a **closed regenerative loop** that sustains oscillation once power is applied.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Block 1: Inverting Schmitt Trigger (Comparator with Hysteresis)

The Schmitt trigger is a comparator with **positive feedback** that produces a **square wave** at its output, oscillating between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$. Its output is connected to the integrator.

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

- The Schmitt trigger has two threshold voltages due to hysteresis:
  - **Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP):** $+\beta V_{sat}$
  - **Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP):** $-\beta V_{sat}$
- where $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ is the **positive feedback fraction**.

- When the integrated output $V_o(t)$ rises above $+\beta V_{sat}$, the Schmitt output $V_{sq}$ snaps from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$.
- When $V_o(t)$ falls below $-\beta V_{sat}$, $V_{sq}$ snaps back to $+V_{sat}$.

### 2.2 Building Block 2: Active Integrator (Miller Integrator)

The integrator receives the square wave from the Schmitt trigger and produces a **linearly ramping output**.

**Operating Principle:**

- The input is the square wave ($\pm V_{sat}$).
- The output is the **time integral** of the input (scaled by $-1/RC$):
$$V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{R_3 C} \int V_{sq} \, dt$$
- For a constant input $+V_{sat}$ over time $T_1$, the output is a **negative-going ramp**:
$$V_o(T_1) = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C} \cdot T_1$$
- For a constant input $-V_{sat}$ over time $T_2$, the output is a **positive-going ramp**.

### 2.3 Frequency of Oscillation Derivation (Triangular Wave)

For a **symmetric** triangular wave, the rise time $T_1$ equals the fall time $T_2 = T/2$. The total peak-to-peak swing is $2\beta V_{sat}$.

Setting the time taken to swing from $-\beta V_{sat}$ to $+\beta V_{sat}$:

$$\frac{2\beta V_{sat}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{T_1}{R_3 C} \implies T_1 = 2\beta R_3 C$$

Since $T = 2T_1$:

$$T = 4\beta R_3 C = \frac{4 R_1 R_3 C}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_1 R_3 C}}$$

### 2.4 Sawtooth Wave Generator: The Asymmetric Modification

In a sawtooth generator, the integrator has a **discharge path** that allows the output to reset faster than it charges. This is typically done by:

- **Adding a resistor $R_f$ in parallel with the integrating capacitor $C$**, or
- **Using a JFET/MOSFET switch** in parallel with the capacitor to quickly discharge it, or
- **Asymmetric power supply** to the Schmitt trigger.

The two half-periods become:

- $T_1$ (slow ramp) and $T_2$ (fast ramp) with $T_1 \gg T_2$
- Frequency: $f_o = \dfrac{1}{T_1 + T_2}$

For a typical modified circuit with $R_f$ across the capacitor:

$$T_1 = 2 \beta R_3 C \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_f - R_3} \quad \text{(slow ramp, charging)}$$

$$T_2 = 2 \beta R_3 C \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_f + R_3} \quad \text{(fast ramp, discharging)}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all **exam-critical formulas** for this experiment. Memorize them with their boundary conditions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Positive feedback fraction | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | dimensionless | $0 < \beta < 1$ |
| 2 | Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP) | $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | Set by Schmitt trigger |
| 3 | Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP) | $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | Set by Schmitt trigger |
| 4 | Peak output amplitude | $V_p = \pm \beta V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | Same as threshold voltages |
| 5 | Time period (triangular) | $T = \dfrac{4 R_1 R_3 C}{R_1 + R_2}$ | seconds (s) | Symmetric waveform |
| 6 | Frequency (triangular) | $f_o = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_1 R_3 C}$ | Hertz (Hz) | For symmetric design |
| 7 | Integrator output ramp | $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C} \cdot t$ | Volts (V) | Linear ramp equation |
| 8 | Duty cycle (sawtooth) | $D = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 + T_2}$ | dimensionless | $0 < D < 1$ |
| 9 | RC time constant | $\tau = R_3 C$ | seconds (s) | Determines ramp slope |
| 10 | Sawtooth frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{T_1 + T_2}$ | Hertz (Hz) | With $T_1 \neq T_2$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)**: Sawtooth waves are the heart of the **time-base generator**, sweeping the electron beam across the screen linearly.
- **Function Generators**: Bench instruments use this exact op-amp topology as a low-cost waveform source up to a few hundred kHz.
- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**: Variable-duty-cycle sawtooth/triangular waves compare with a reference to generate PWM control signals for **motor drives, SMPS, and inverters**.
- **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converters)**: Dual-slope and successive-approximation ADCs use integrator-ramp concepts derived from this same circuit.
- **Voltage-Controlled Oscillators (VCO)**: Replacing $R_3$ with a JFET or photoresistor yields a voltage- or light-controlled oscillator.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hysteresis Threshold Voltages of the Schmitt Trigger

**Given:** Op-amp with output saturation $\pm V_{sat}$ and positive feedback resistors $R_1$ (from output to non-inverting input) and $R_2$ (from non-inverting input to ground).

**Step 1:** Apply KCL at the non-inverting input node ($V^+$).

The current through $R_1$ equals the current through $R_2$ (no current into ideal op-amp input):

$$\frac{V_{sq} - V^+}{R_1} = \frac{V^+ - 0}{R_2}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $V^+$ in terms of $V_{sq}$:

$$V^+ (R_1 + R_2) = V_{sq} \cdot R_2$$

$$V^+ = V_{sq} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the **feedback factor** $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ (note: textbook conventions vary; some define $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ when $R_1$ is from output and $R_2$ to ground. We use the convention where the resistor from output is $R_1$, giving $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$). Adopting the convention $\beta = R_1/(R_1 + R_2)$:

$$V^+ = \beta \cdot V_{sq}$$

**Step 4:** For the comparator to switch, $V^+ = V^-$ (the inverting input equals the non-inverting input, which is the integrating output $V_o$).

$$V_o = \beta \cdot V_{sq}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the two possible values of $V_{sq}$:

$$\boxed{V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}, \quad V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}}$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency of Triangular Wave Generator

**Step 1:** During the first half-period, the Schmitt output is $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$ (constant). The integrator output is a negative-going linear ramp:

$$V_o(t) = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C} \cdot t + V_o(0)$$

**Step 2:** Initially, suppose $V_o(0) = +\beta V_{sat}$ (the upper threshold). The output ramps downward. The switching condition is reached when $V_o(t) = -\beta V_{sat}$.

**Step 3:** Solve for the time $T_1$ taken to reach the lower threshold:

$$-\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C} \cdot T_1 + \beta V_{sat}$$

$$-2\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C} \cdot T_1$$

$$T_1 = 2\beta R_3 C$$

**Step 4:** By symmetry, the second half-period (rising ramp) is also:

$$T_2 = 2\beta R_3 C$$

**Step 5:** Total time period:

$$T = T_1 + T_2 = 4\beta R_3 C = \frac{4 R_1 R_3 C}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 6:** Frequency of oscillation:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_1 R_3 C}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sawtooth Wave Generator with Reset Resistor

**Given:** A modified triangular generator with a resistor $R_f$ connected in parallel with the integrating capacitor $C$, where $R_f \gg R_3$ typically.

**Step 1:** During the charging phase (Schmitt output $= +V_{sat}$), the capacitor charges through $R_3$ but also partially discharges through $R_f$. The **effective time constant** is:

$$\tau_1 = \frac{R_3 R_f C}{R_f - R_3} \approx R_3 C \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_f - R_3}$$

**Step 2:** The charging time $T_1$ is:

$$T_1 = 2\beta \cdot \tau_1 = \frac{2 R_1 R_3 R_f C}{R_1 (R_f - R_3) + R_2 (R_f - R_3)} \cdot \frac{1}{R_f - R_3} \cdot R_f$$

For a typical approximation with $R_f \gg R_3$:

$$T_1 \approx 2\beta R_3 C \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_f - R_3}$$

**Step 3:** During the discharge phase, the capacitor discharges quickly through $R_f$ since the Schmitt output is now $-V_{sat}$ and the parallel paths add up:

$$\tau_2 = \frac{R_3 R_f C}{R_f + R_3}$$

**Step 4:** The discharge time $T_2$ is:

$$T_2 = 2\beta \cdot \tau_2 = 2\beta R_3 C \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_f + R_3}$$

**Step 5:** Total time period:

$$T = T_1 + T_2 = 2\beta R_3 C \left( \frac{R_f}{R_f - R_3} + \frac{R_f}{R_f + R_3} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Frequency:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\beta R_3 C \left( \dfrac{R_f}{R_f - R_3} + \dfrac{R_f}{R_f + R_3} \right)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For practical sawtooth generation, $R_f$ is chosen to be just slightly greater than $R_3$ to make the **rise time slow** and the **fall time fast**, producing the characteristic ramp-drop shape.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Triangular vs. Sawtooth

| Feature | Triangular Wave | Sawtooth Wave |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Symmetric (linear up, linear down) | Asymmetric (slow ramp, fast reset) |
| **Rise Time** | $T_1 = 2\beta R_3 C$ | $T_1 = 2\beta R_3 C \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{R_f - R_3}$ (slow) |
| **Fall Time** | $T_2 = 2\beta R_3 C$ | $T_2 = 2\beta R_3 C \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{R_f + R_3}$ (fast) |
| **Symmetry** | $T_1 = T_2$ | $T_1 \gg T_2$ |
| **Frequency** | $f_o = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_1 R_3 C}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{T_1 + T_2}$ |
| **Trigger Source** | Square wave | Modified square wave with discharge path |
| **Duty Cycle** | 50% | Variable (10%–90%) |
| **Amplitude** | $\pm \beta V_{sat}$ | $\pm \beta V_{sat}$ (same as triangular) |
| **Key Modification** | None (standard integrator) | Reset resistor $R_f$ across $C$ or JFET switch |
| **Application** | Function generators, audio modulation | CRO time-base, PWM, ADC, motor control |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Design a triangular wave generator using op-amp 741 with $V_{sat} = \pm 12\text{ V}$, $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\mu F$. Find the frequency, amplitude, and verify the design.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the feedback fraction $\beta$:

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10}{10 + 20} = \frac{10}{30} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Output amplitude:

$$V_p = \pm \beta V_{sat} = \pm \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = \pm 4\text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Frequency of oscillation:

$$f_o = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_1 R_3 C} = \frac{10\text{k} + 20\text{k}}{4 \times 10\text{k} \times 10\text{k} \times 0.1\mu}$$

$$f_o = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{4 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{40 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3 \times 10^4}{4 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.75 \times 10^6 = 750\text{ kHz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $f_o = 750\text{ Hz}$ (correcting the order of magnitude: $\frac{30{,}000}{0.04} = 750{,}000\text{ Hz}$... recalculating): $f_o = \dfrac{30{,}000}{4 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.5\text{ MHz}$ — this is **impractical** for IC 741 (slew rate limit ~ 0.5 V/µs). **Practical KTU lab designs** use $R_3$ in the range of 10 k$\Omega$–100 k$\Omega$ and $C$ in nF–µF range to get **kHz-range oscillations**.

**Step 4 (Corrected Practical Design):** For $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\mu\text{F}$:

$$f_o = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{4 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{30{,}000}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.5\text{ kHz}$$

**This is a practical KTU lab frequency.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Triangular Wave Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Power Supply plus or minus 12V] --> B[Schmitt Trigger Op-Amp U1]
    B -->|Square Wave Vsat| C[Active Integrator Op-Amp U2]
    C -->|Triangular Wave Vo| D[CRO Display]
    C -->|Feedback to inverting input| B
    E[Resistor R1 feedback] --> B
    F[Resistor R2 to ground] --> B
    G[Resistor R3 input] --> C
    H[Capacitor C feedback] --> C

    classDef opamp fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px

    class A power
    class B,C opamp
    class D output

```

### 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAGE1["STAGE 1: SCHMITT TRIGGER U1 LM741"]
        V1[Input Vin from Integrator Output] --> N1[Inverting Input Pin 2 of U1]
        N2[Non-Inverting Input Pin 3 of U1] --> CMP[Comparator with Hysteresis]
        R1A[R1 from Vsq to Pin 3] --> N2
        R2A[R2 from Pin 3 to GND] --> N2
        CMP --> VSQ[Square Wave Output Vsq at Pin 6 of U1]
    end

    subgraph STAGE2["STAGE 2: ACTIVE INTEGRATOR U2 LM741"]
        VSQ --> R3A[R3 input resistor]
        R3A --> N3[Inverting Input Pin 2 of U2]
        N4[Non-Inverting Input Pin 3 of U2] --> GND1[Ground]
        N3 --> CAP[Feedback Capacitor C]
        CAP --> OUT[Triangular Wave Output Vo at Pin 6 of U2]
    end

    OUT --> V1
    OUT --> CRO[CRO or DSO Display]

    class STAGE2 stage2

```

### 4.3 Signal Flow and Waveform Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SQ[Square Wave from Schmitt Trigger] -->|Constant plus Vsat| INT1[Integrator Charges C]
    INT1 -->|Linear negative ramp| TR1[Triangular Down Slope]
    SQ2[Square Wave flips to minus Vsat] -->|Constant minus Vsat| INT2[Integrator Discharges C]
    INT2 -->|Linear positive ramp| TR2[Triangular Up Slope]
    TR1 --> FINAL[Combined Triangular Wave]
    TR2 --> FINAL

    class INT1,INT2 process
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BASE["BASE TRIANGULAR GENERATOR"]
        SCH[Schmitt Trigger Stage] --> INT[Integrator Stage]
        INT --> OUT_VO[Output Vo]
        OUT_VO --> FB[Feedback to Schmitt]
    end

    subgraph MOD["SAWTOOTH MODIFICATION"]
        RF[Reset Resistor Rf in parallel with C] --> INT
        RF -.->|Fast discharge path| CAP2[Capacitor C discharges quickly]
    end

    SCH2[Modified Schmitt Trigger Output] --> R3B[R3] --> INT
    INT --> VO2[Sawtooth Output: slow ramp up, fast drop]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define a triangular wave generator. List the two op-amp building blocks used to construct it.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A triangular wave generator is an oscillator circuit that produces a **periodic, linearly rising and falling waveform** with equal rise and fall times. It is constructed using two op-amp building blocks: **(1) a Schmitt trigger (comparator with positive feedback)** which generates a square wave, and **(2) an active integrator** which integrates the square wave to produce a triangular output. The two stages are cascaded in a closed loop where the integrator output is fed back to the Schmitt trigger's inverting input. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for block 1, 1 for block 2)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
What is the role of the positive feedback fraction $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ in a Schmitt trigger used in waveform generation?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The feedback fraction $\beta$ determines the **threshold voltages** at which the Schmitt trigger switches state, given by $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$ and $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$. **(1 mark)** It sets the **peak amplitude of the triangular output** because the integrator's output must cross these thresholds to cause switching. **(1 mark)** It also influences the **frequency of oscillation** since $T = 4\beta R_3 C$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **frequency of oscillation** of a triangular wave generator using two op-amps. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a triangular wave generator to produce a **1 kHz** signal with **$\pm 4\text{ V}$** peak amplitude. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 12\text{ V}$ and $C = 0.1\mu\text{F}$. Choose appropriate resistor values. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The Schmitt trigger uses positive feedback through $R_1$ and $R_2$, giving a feedback fraction:
$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The Schmitt output $V_{sq}$ saturates at $\pm V_{sat}$, producing threshold voltages:
$$V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}, \quad V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The integrator output is given by:
$$V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{R_3 C} \int V_{sq} \, dt$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** When $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$ (constant), the output ramps linearly from $+\beta V_{sat}$ to $-\beta V_{sat}$:
$$-\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C} T_1 + \beta V_{sat} \implies T_1 = 2\beta R_3 C$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** By symmetry, $T_2 = 2\beta R_3 C$, so total period $T = 4\beta R_3 C$.

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Final frequency:
$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_1 R_3 C}}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) ideal op-amp with infinite gain and zero offset, (ii) saturation at $\pm V_{sat}$, (iii) symmetric integrator with $R_3$ and $C$ only (no parasitic paths), (iv) op-amp slew rate is sufficiently high to follow the ramp.

---

**Part (b) — Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Determine $\beta$ from amplitude requirement:
$$V_p = \beta V_{sat} \implies \beta = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Choose $R_1$ and $R_2$ for $\beta = 1/3$:
$$\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1}{3} \implies 3R_1 = R_1 + R_2 \implies R_2 = 2R_1$$

Choose $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega \implies R_2 = 20\text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply frequency formula:
$$f_o = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_1 R_3 C} = 1\text{ kHz}$$

$$1000 = \frac{10\text{k} + 20\text{k}}{4 \times 10\text{k} \times R_3 \times 0.1\mu}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{4 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{30{,}000}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.5 \times 10^6 \Omega$$

This is too high. **Re-choose $C$:** Let $C = 0.1\mu\text{F}$, then:

$$R_3 = \frac{30{,}000}{4000 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{30{,}000}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 75 \times 10^6 \Omega = 75\text{ M}\Omega$$

Still impractical. **Practical redesign:** Use $C = 0.01\mu\text{F}$ and increase $R_1, R_2$:

With $R_1 = 100\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 200\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$f_o = \frac{300 \times 10^3}{4 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times R_3 \times 0.01 \mu} = 1000 \implies R_3 = 75\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Final Design:
- $R_1 = 100\text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_2 = 200\text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_3 = 75\text{ k}\Omega$
- $C = 0.01\mu\text{F}$
- $f_o = 1\text{ kHz}$, $V_p = \pm 4\text{ V}$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the operation of a **sawtooth wave generator** using op-amps. Compare it with a triangular wave generator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A triangular wave generator has $R_1 = 15\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 30\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 1\text{ nF}$, and $V_{sat} = \pm 14\text{ V}$. Calculate the **(i) feedback fraction**, **(ii) peak output amplitude**, **(iii) time period**, and **(iv) frequency of oscillation**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sawtooth Generator (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** A sawtooth wave generator has a structure similar to a triangular generator (Schmitt trigger + integrator) but with an **asymmetric charging/discharging mechanism**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The modification involves:
- A **reset resistor $R_f$ in parallel with the integrating capacitor $C$**, OR
- A **JFET switch** in parallel with $C$ that briefly turns on to rapidly discharge it, OR
- **Asymmetric reference voltages** at the Schmitt input.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** This causes:
- **Slow ramp** ($T_1 \gg T_2$): capacitor charges slowly through $R_3$ only.
- **Fast reset** ($T_2$ very small): capacitor discharges rapidly through $R_f \parallel R_3$ (or via switch).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Applications include CRO time-base and PWM.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Feedback fraction:
$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{15}{15 + 30} = \frac{15}{45} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Peak amplitude:
$$V_p = \beta V_{sat} = \frac{1}{3} \times 14 = 4.67\text{ V}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Time period:
$$T = 4\beta R_3 C = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}$$
$$T = \frac{4 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-9}}{3} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{3} = 1.33 \times 10^{-5}\text{ s} = 13.33\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Frequency:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.33 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 75\text{ kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention error**: Students often write $V_{UTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$ and $V_{LTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$ (swapped). The UTP is the threshold **reached during the upward ramp**, which is **positive**.
> 2. **Feedback fraction confusion**: Two conventions exist — $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ or $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ depending on which resistor is from output. **Always verify the schematic before writing the formula.**
> 3. **Unit errors in C**: $1\text{ nF} = 10^{-9}\text{ F}$, $0.1\mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}\text{ F}$. Mixing up $n$, $\mu$, $p$ is the #1 source of arithmetic errors.
> 4. **Slew rate limitation**: IC 741 has a slew rate of $0.5\text{ V}/\mu\text{s}$. For triangular waves with sharp slopes, the actual output will be **truncated/flattened** if the frequency exceeds the slew rate limit. Always check: $\dfrac{dV_o}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C}$ should be less than $0.5\text{ V}/\mu\text{s}$.
> 5. **Confusing triangular and sawtooth formulas**: A sawtooth has $T_1 \neq T_2$, but the basic triangular formula $T = 4\beta R_3 C$ **assumes symmetry**. Do not apply it directly to a sawtooth.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Two building blocks**: Schmitt Trigger (square wave) + Active Integrator (triangular wave).
> - **Cascade order matters**: Schmitt output → Integrator input, Integrator output → Schmitt inverting input.
> - **Feedback fraction**: $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$, must be between 0 and 1.
> - **Threshold voltages**: $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$, $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$.
> - **Amplitude of triangular wave**: $V_p = \pm \beta V_{sat}$ — **self-regulated by the circuit**, not by the supply voltage directly.
> - **Time period**: $T = \dfrac{4 R_1 R_3 C}{R_1 + R_2} = 4\beta R_3 C$.
> - **Frequency**: $f_o = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_1 R_3 C}$.
> - **Sawtooth modification**: Add a parallel reset resistor $R_f$ across $C$ to create asymmetric ramp.
> - **Symmetric vs. Asymmetric**: Triangular = $T_1 = T_2$ (50% duty); Sawtooth = $T_1 \gg T_2$.
> - **To increase frequency**: Decrease $R_3$, decrease $C$, or increase the ratio $R_2/R_1$.
> - **To increase amplitude**: Increase the ratio $R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ (i.e., increase $R_1$ relative to $R_2$).
> - **Op-amp limitations**: Slew rate (IC 741 = 0.5 V/µs) and finite GBW limit high-frequency operation.
> - **Common ICs**: LM741, LM324, TL081, LF353 (for higher frequencies).
> - **Lab observation skills**: Always observe **both waveforms simultaneously** on a **dual-trace CRO** — square wave on CH1 (Schmitt output), triangular wave on CH2 (integrator output).
> - **Verify frequency** using CRO: $f = \dfrac{1}{\text{Time/Div setting} \times \text{number of divisions for one cycle}}$.
> - **Verify amplitude**: $V_p = \dfrac{\text{Volts/Div} \times \text{peak-to-peak divisions}}{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wien Bridge Oscillator - Without & With Amplitude Stabilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Wien Bridge Oscillator - Without & With Amplitude Stabilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is a low-frequency, sinusoidal LC-free (actually RC) electronic oscillator that generates continuous, low-distortion sine waves by exploiting the frequency-dependent behavior of a **Wien Bridge Network** combined with a **positive feedback loop** through an operational amplifier. It is the most widely used audio-frequency oscillator (20 Hz to 20 kHz) in laboratories and commercial test equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECL407):** The Wien Bridge Oscillator is a mandatory experiment under Module 2 of the Linear Integrated Circuits Lab. Students are expected to design, wire, and verify both *unregulated* and *amplitude-stabilized* versions on a breadboard/PCB using an Op-Amp (typically μA741 or equivalent).

The circuit fundamentally consists of:
1. **A non-inverting amplifier stage** (built using an op-amp with gain-setting resistors $R_f$ and $R_1$).
2. **A Wien Bridge feedback network** — a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm acting as a frequency-selective filter.

For sustained sinusoidal oscillation, the **Barkhausen Criterion** demands two simultaneous conditions:
- The **loop gain** magnitude $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
- The **loop phase shift** $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ \text{ (or } 360^\circ\text{)}$

---

## 🍕 Real-World Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing. To keep the swing moving smoothly forever, you must:
- Push with **exactly the right amount of force** (not too strong, not too weak) — this is the **gain condition**.
- Push at **exactly the right moment** in the swing cycle (when it is at the middle passing forward) — this is the **phase condition** of $0^\circ$.

If you push too hard, the swing goes wild (oscillation grows). If you push too softly, it dies down (oscillation decays). If you push at the wrong moment, the swing motion becomes chaotic. The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is the electronic "swing" — the op-amp provides the "push," and the RC network decides *when* to push and at what frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called a "Bridge"?** Because the original 1931 Hewlett-Packard design used a **Wheatstone-bridge-style circuit** with four arms (two RC arms and two resistors) where the bridge was initially balanced at the oscillation frequency. Today, we use a simpler half-bridge (two RC arms), but the historical name stuck.

## ⚙️ Key Physical/Design Constants

- **Oscillation Frequency (f₀)** = $\mathbf{\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}}$
- **Required Amplifier Gain (A)** = $\mathbf{3}$ (for ideal sustained oscillation)
- **Phase shift through feedback network at f₀** = $\mathbf{0^\circ}$
- **Standard test op-amp supply** = $\mathbf{\pm 12V \text{ to } \pm 15V}$
- **Standard KTU lab component values** = **R = 10 kΩ, C = 0.01 μF** (gives f₀ ≈ **1.59 kHz**, well within audio range)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

### **Part A: The Wien Bridge (Frequency Selective) Network**

The Wien Bridge feedback network consists of two arms:
- **Series Arm:** $R$ in series with $C$
- **Parallel Arm:** $R$ in parallel with $C$

The transfer function of this network (taking output from the junction of the two arms to ground) is:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{Z_{parallel}}{Z_{series} + Z_{parallel}}$$

Where:
- $Z_{series} = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$
- $Z_{parallel} = R \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$

After algebraic simplification, the feedback factor becomes:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

At the **resonant/critical frequency** where $\omega RC = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$, the imaginary part vanishes, and the feedback factor becomes purely real and maximum:

$$\beta_{max} = \dfrac{1}{3} \quad \text{(real, no phase shift)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** At $f_0$, the network produces **zero phase shift** and **maximum feedback ratio of 1/3**. This is the *only* frequency that satisfies the Barkhausen phase criterion through this network alone.

### **Part B: The Non-Inverting Amplifier**

To satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, since $\beta = 1/3$, the amplifier gain must be:

$$A = \dfrac{1}{\beta} = 3$$

For a non-inverting op-amp configuration:
$$A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \implies R_f = 2R_1$$

For matched KTU lab standard values:
- $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$

### **Part C: Why We Need Amplitude Stabilization**

In a *theoretical/ideal* circuit with **exactly A = 3**, the oscillator produces a perfect sine wave of constant amplitude forever. In *reality*:
- Component tolerances, temperature drift, and supply variations cause gain to drift above or below 3.
- If $A > 3$: amplitude grows until **op-amp saturation** (output clipped, distorted waveform).
- If $A < 3$: oscillation decays and dies out.

Hence, practical circuits use **non-linear amplitude stabilization** to dynamically adjust the gain around 3, keeping amplitude constant.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Typical Value / Unit** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency of oscillation | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **1.59 kHz** (for 10kΩ, 0.01μF) | Sets output sine frequency |
| Angular frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/sec | Used in transfer functions |
| Feedback factor at $f_0$ | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | dimensionless | Maximum feedback |
| Phase shift at $f_0$ | $\phi = 0^\circ$ | degrees | Barkhausen phase condition |
| Required gain | $A = 3$ | dimensionless | Barkhausen magnitude condition |
| Amplifier design equation | $R_f = 2R_1$ | ohms | Non-inverting op-amp gain |
| Period of oscillation | $T = 2\pi RC$ | seconds | Inverse of frequency |
| Quality factor (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | dimensionless | Selectivity measure of network |

---

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|
| **Audio Test Equipment** | Function/signal generators producing pure tones (e.g., HP 200CD, the original commercial Wien Bridge oscillator) |
| **Musical Instrument Synthesizers** | Stable low-frequency tone generation |
| **Bio-medical Instrumentation** | ECG/EEG calibration signals |
| **Communication Systems** | Carrier generation in transmitters (lower freq bands) |
| **Phase-Locked Loops (PLL)** | Reference oscillator inside PLL ICs |
| **Voltage-Controlled Oscillators (VCO)** | Core sine wave generator in ICs like XR-2206, ICL8038 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔢 Derivation 1: Frequency of Oscillation from the Wien Bridge Network

Let us derive $f_0$ rigorously. We start with the impedance of the series and parallel arms.

**Step 1: Define the impedances.**

The impedance of the **series arm** (R in series with C) is:

$$Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$

The impedance of the **parallel arm** (R in parallel with C) is:

$$Z_p = \dfrac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2: Form the feedback factor.**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

Substituting:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Step 3: Common denominator in the denominator.**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)}}$$

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{R \cdot j\omega C}{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 4: Expand $(1 + j\omega RC)^2$.**

$$(1 + j\omega RC)^2 = 1 + 2j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 = 1 + 2j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2$$

**Step 5: Sum with the extra $j\omega RC$ term.**

$$(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC = 1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega RC$$

**Step 6: Substitute back into β.**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega RC}$$

**Step 7: Factor out and divide numerator and denominator by $j\omega RC$.**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

**Step 8: Apply the Barkhausen phase condition.**

For the loop phase shift to be zero, the imaginary part of the denominator must vanish:

$$\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC} = 0$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \implies \omega RC = 1$$

$$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$$

**Step 9: Convert angular frequency to frequency in Hz.**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Step 10: Substitute typical KTU values.** With $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \mu F$:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}$$

---

## 🔢 Derivation 2: Barkhausen Gain Condition

The non-inverting op-amp gain is:

$$A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$$

At resonance, $\beta = 1/3$. For sustained oscillation:

$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \implies A \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} = 1 \implies A = 3$$

Therefore:

$$1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 3$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \implies R_f = 2R_1}$$

**Numerical example:** Choose $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 3: Phase Response of the Wien Network

The phase angle of $\beta(j\omega)$ is:

$$\phi(\omega) = -\arctan\left(\dfrac{\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}}{3}\right)$$

At $\omega = \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$, the numerator is zero:

$$\phi(\omega_0) = -\arctan(0) = 0^\circ$$

For $\omega \ll \omega_0$ (low frequency), $\omega RC \to 0$:

$$\phi \to -\arctan\left(\dfrac{-\infty}{3}\right) \to +90^\circ$$

For $\omega \gg \omega_0$ (high frequency), $\omega RC \to \infty$:

$$\phi \to -\arctan\left(\dfrac{+\infty}{3}\right) \to -90^\circ$$

So the Wien network sweeps from $+90^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$, passing through $0^\circ$ at $\omega_0$ — exactly the behavior required for the op-amp (which adds $0^\circ$ in non-inverting mode) to give a total loop phase of $0^\circ$.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 4: Output Amplitude With Stabilization (Zener Limiter Method)

Let two back-to-back Zener diodes (Zener voltage $V_Z$, forward drop $V_D \approx 0.7V$) replace the lower feedback resistor $R_1$. Define the "softening voltage":

$$V_{lim} = V_Z + V_D$$

For small output amplitudes $\vert V_o \vert < V_{lim}$, the Zeners are OFF, and the gain is:

$$A_{small} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} > 3 \text{ (say } 3.1\text{)}$$

For large amplitudes $\vert V_o \vert > V_{lim}$, the Zeners conduct, and the effective resistance across them becomes small (essentially the dynamic Zener impedance $r_z$ in parallel with $R_1$):

$$A_{large} \approx 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1 \parallel r_z} \to 3$$

The stabilized peak output amplitude becomes:

$$\boxed{V_{out(peak)} \approx V_Z + V_D \approx V_{lim}}$$

This gives a predictable, distortion-controlled amplitude.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 5: Amplitude Stabilization Using JFET (Voltage-Controlled Resistor)

A **JFET** with drain and source tied together acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor (VCR)**, where:

$$R_{DS} = \dfrac{r_{DS(on)}}{1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}}$$

Where $V_P$ is the pinch-off voltage (typically $-2$ to $-5V$ for 2N3819 JFET).

In the AGC version of the Wien Bridge Oscillator:
- The peak output is detected by a **peak detector** (diode + capacitor).
- The detector output drives the gate of the JFET, increasing $V_{GS}$ (more negative).
- As the oscillation grows, $R_{DS}$ increases, decreasing the effective gain $A$.
- When gain stabilizes at exactly 3, the loop reaches equilibrium.

**Steady-state gain equation with JFET stabilization:**

$$A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{DS}} = 3$$

$$R_{DS} = \dfrac{R_f}{2}$$

Setting $R_f = 20\text{k}\Omega$, we get $R_{DS} = 10\text{k}\Omega$ at equilibrium.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
