# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Precision Rectifier. Why is it needed when ordinary diode rectifiers are available? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **precision rectifier** is an active rectifier circuit that uses an **op-amp in combination with diodes** to rectify AC signals whose amplitude is less than the diode forward voltage drop (0.7 V for silicon).

**Why it is needed (2 marks):**
- Ordinary diode rectifiers **fail to rectify signals below 0.7 V** because the diode never becomes forward biased.
- In precision rectifiers, the op-amp's high open-loop gain ($A_{OL} \approx 10^5$) effectively **reduces the diode drop to $V_F / A_{OL} \approx 7\text{ μV}$** at the input, making it possible to rectify signals as small as a few millivolts.
- This makes precision rectifiers essential in **biomedical instrumentation, sensor signal conditioning, and low-level signal processing** where input amplitudes are in the mV range.

---

### Question 2: What is a "Super Diode"? Draw its circuit and explain its operation. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Super Diode** is the basic building block of precision rectifiers, formed by placing a **diode inside the negative feedback loop of an op-amp**.

**Circuit:** Op-amp with diode $D_1$ in feedback path (anode at inverting input, cathode at op-amp output).

**Operation (2 marks):**
- The op-amp's enormous gain **multiplies the input signal** before it reaches the diode.
- The op-amp automatically adjusts its output to overcome the diode's 0.7 V drop, so the diode always operates in its linear region when conducting.
- The result is an **ideal diode behavior** with effectively **zero forward voltage drop** and **zero forward resistance** when viewed from the input terminals.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Design Problem

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **half-wave precision rectifier** using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the output voltage in both half-cycles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a half-wave precision rectifier to rectify a $2\text{ kHz}$ sine wave of peak amplitude $V_m = 50\text{ mV}$. Use a $\mu$A741 op-amp (slew rate $= 0.5\text{ V/μs}$). Verify whether the op-amp slew rate is sufficient. State the limitations of this circuit. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Circuit Diagram (3 Marks):** Draw the standard half-wave precision rectifier with:
- $R_1$ from input to inverting input (Pin 2)
- $D_1$ in feedback (anode to Pin 2, cathode to Pin 6)
- $D_2$ from Pin 2 to ground (anode to ground, cathode to Pin 2)
- $R_f$ from Pin 6 to Pin 2
- Output taken from Pin 6
- $\pm 15\text{ V}$ supplies on Pins 7 and 4

**Operation (2 Marks):**

**Case 1: $V_s > 0$**
- Op-amp output swings negative.
- $D_1$ conducts, $D_2$ off.
- KCL at virtual ground:

$$
\frac{V_s - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} \Rightarrow V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_s
$$

**Case 2: $V_s < 0$**
- Op-amp output swings positive.
- $D_1$ blocks, $D_2$ conducts (clamps inverting input to $-0.7\text{ V}$, protects op-amp).
- $V_{out} = 0\text{ V}$

**Final Expression (2 Marks):**

$$
V_{out} = \begin{cases} -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_s, & V_s > 0 \\ 0, & V_s < 0 \end{cases}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Design — Choose components (3 Marks)**

For unity gain rectification of a $50\text{ mV}$ signal:

$$
R_1 = R_f = 10\text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard 1% tolerance)}
$$

Diodes: $1\text{N}4148$ (signal diode, low forward drop) or $1\text{N}34}$ (germanium for better performance).

**Step 2: Slew rate verification (2 Marks)**

Maximum rate of change of a sine wave occurs at zero crossing:

$$
\left.\frac{dV}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_m = 2\pi \times 2000 \times 0.05 = 628.3\text{ V/s} = 6.28 \times 10^{-4}\text{ V/μs}
$$

Since op-amp slew rate = $0.5\text{ V/μs} \gg 6.28 \times 10^{-4}\text{ V/μs}$, the slew rate is **more than sufficient** ✅.

**Maximum frequency possible:**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 0.05} \approx 1.59\text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3: Limitations (2 Marks)**

1. **Crossover Distortion** — during the zero-crossing of input, the op-amp must slew from one saturation region to another, causing brief distortion.
2. **Slew Rate & Bandwidth Limitation** — restricts high-frequency operation.
3. **Op-amp Offset Voltage** — directly adds to output, causing inaccuracy for very small signals.
4. **Output Current Limitation** — op-amp can source/sink only ~20–25 mA typically.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **full-wave precision rectifier (absolute value circuit)** using op-amps. Explain its operation with relevant waveforms. Derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A precision rectifier is used to rectify a $5\text{ kHz}$ sine wave of peak $V_m = 200\text{ mV}$. The op-amp has a slew rate of $1\text{ V/μs}$ and an open-loop gain of $2 \times 10^5$. Calculate: (i) the effective diode drop referred to the input, (ii) the maximum frequency at which the circuit can operate, and (iii) the minimum input signal that can be rectified. Assume silicon diodes. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Circuit Diagram (3 Marks):** Three op-amp configuration:
- A1: inverting half-wave precision rectifier (with $D_1, D_2$, gain = 1)
- A2: non-inverting half-wave precision rectifier (with $D_3, D_4$, gain = 1)
- A3: summing amplifier with $R_a = R_b = 2R$ and feedback $R$

**Operation with Waveforms (2 Marks):**

| Time | $V_s$ | $V_1$ (A1 out) | $V_2$ (A2 out) | $V_o$ (A3 out) |
|------|-------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Positive half-cycle | $+V_m$ | $-V_s$ | $0$ | $\vert V_s \vert$ |
| Negative half-cycle | $-V_m$ | $0$ | $+V_s$ | $\vert V_s \vert$ |

**Derivation (2 Marks):**

For positive $V_s$: $V_1 = -V_s$, $V_2 = 0$

$$
V_o = -\frac{R}{2R}(V_1 + V_2) = -\frac{1}{2}(-V_s + 0) = \frac{V_s}{2} \text{ (after gain correction from A2's +1 stage) }
$$

For negative $V_s$: $V_1 = 0$, $V_2 = -V_s$

$$
V_o = -\frac{1}{2}(0 + (-V_s)) = \frac{V_s}{2} \cdot 2 = -V_s = |V_s|
$$

**Final expression:**

$$
\boxed{V_o = \vert V_s \vert}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $f = 5\text{ kHz}$, $V_m = 200\text{ mV} = 0.2\text{ V}$, $SR = 1\text{ V/μs} = 10^6\text{ V/s}$, $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $V_F = 0.7\text{ V}$ (silicon)

**(i) Effective diode drop referred to input (2 Marks):**

$$
V_{F,eff} = \frac{V_F}{A_{OL}} = \frac{0.7}{2 \times 10^5} = 3.5 \times 10^{-6}\text{ V} = 3.5\text{ μV}
$$

**(ii) Maximum operating frequency (3 Marks):**

For slew rate not to limit operation:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m} = \frac{10^6}{2\pi \times 0.2}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{10^6}{1.2566} = 7.96 \times 10^5\text{ Hz} \approx 796\text{ kHz}
$$

**(iii) Minimum input signal that can be rectified (2 Marks):**

The minimum signal is determined by the effective diode drop:

$$
V_{in,min} = V_{F,eff} = 3.5\text{ μV}
$$

**However**, in practice, the op-amp's input offset voltage $V_{io}$ (typically $\sim 1\text{–}5\text{ mV}$ for $\mu$A741) dominates. So practically:

$$
V_{in,min} \approx V_{io} \approx 1\text{–}5\text{ mV}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where Students Lose Marks in Precision Rectifier Questions:**

1. **❌ Forgetting the Clamp Diode $D_2$** — KTU examiners specifically check for both diodes $D_1$ AND $D_2$. Omitting $D_2$ costs **2 marks** as the op-amp protection mechanism is incomplete.

2. **❌ Wrong Polarity of Diodes** — Drawing $D_1$ with cathode at Pin 2 and anode at Pin 6 (backwards) makes the circuit non-functional. The direction must be: **anode at virtual ground, cathode at output** for $D_1$.

3. **❌ Confusing Inverting vs Non-Inverting Output** — In the half-wave rectifier, the output is the **inverted** positive half-cycle. Writing $V_o = +V_s$ instead of $-V_s$ is a common error. **Always check the sign.**

4. **❌ Not Mentioning Slew Rate Limitation** — When asked about "limitations," 80% of students forget to mention slew rate and crossover distortion. This alone can cost **3 marks**.

5. **❌ No Power Supply Connections** — Failing to show $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$ (typically $\pm 15\text{ V}$) on Pins 7 and 4 of the op-amp loses **1 mark**.

6. **❌ Writing "0.7 V" Without Justification** — When asked why ordinary rectifiers fail, you must say: "Because the diode forward voltage drop ($V_F \approx 0.7\text{ V}$) is **greater than** the signal amplitude, the diode never conducts." This phrasing is critical for full credit.

7. **❌ Not Drawing Output Waveform** — In "explain with waveforms" questions, drawing only the input waveform and not the output loses **2 marks**. Always draw **both** waveforms with proper labels ($V_m$, time axis, output amplitude).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and keep it with you for last-minute revision before the KTU lab exam.**

### ⚡ Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- ✅ Precision rectifier = **Op-amp + Diode in feedback** (called "Super Diode")
- ✅ Purpose: **Rectify signals with amplitude < 0.7 V**
- ✅ Half-wave output: $V_o = -V_s$ for $V_s > 0$, else $0$
- ✅ Full-wave output: $V_o = \vert V_s \vert$
- ✅ Two diodes required: $D_1$ (conduction), $D_2$ (protection/clamp)
- ✅ Effective diode drop = $V_F / A_{OL} \approx 7\text{ μV}$
- ✅ Op-amp must be powered with $\pm 15\text{ V}$ (typical)

### ⚡ Critical Formulas (Must Memorize)

- ✅ Unity gain condition: $R_f = R_1$
- ✅ Slew rate limit: $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_m)$
- ✅ Maximum rate of change: $dV/dt = 2\pi f V_m$
- ✅ Effective threshold: $V_{F,eff} = V_F / A_{OL}$

### ⚡ Key Terms (Must Define)

- ✅ **Super Diode** — op-amp with diode in feedback path
- ✅ **Absolute Value Circuit** — full-wave precision rectifier
- ✅ **Crossover Distortion** — brief output error during zero crossing
- ✅ **Virtual Ground** — inverting input held at 0 V by feedback
- ✅ **Slew Rate** — maximum rate of change of op-amp output (V/μs)
- ✅ **Clamp Diode** — protects op-amp from damage during off half-cycle

### ⚡ Components Used (KTU Lab Inventory)

- ✅ IC $\mu$A741 (Op-amp) — 1 per circuit
- ✅ Diode 1N4148 (signal diode) — 2 per circuit
- ✅ Resistors: $R_1 = R_f = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (or 1 kΩ for higher current)
- ✅ Breadboard, connecting wires, function generator, CRO/DSO, $\pm 15\text{ V}$ DC supply

### ⚡ Expected Lab Outcomes (What You Must Demonstrate)

- ✅ Build the circuit on breadboard
- ✅ Apply sine wave input (e.g., $1\text{ kHz}$, $100\text{ mV}$ peak)
- ✅ Observe rectified output on CRO
- ✅ Verify that input < 0.7 V still produces output (proof of precision)
- ✅ Compare with ordinary diode rectifier output
- ✅ Measure and tabulate $V_{in,peak}$ vs $V_{out,peak}$
- ✅ Calculate and verify gain $V_o / V_s = -R_f / R_1$

### ⚡ Common Viva Questions (KTU Lab Exam)

1. *Why can't a normal diode rectify a 50 mV signal?* → Because $V_F = 0.7\text{ V} > 50\text{ mV}$.
2. *What is the role of diode $D_2$?* → It clamps the inverting input during the off half-cycle, preventing damage to the op-amp from saturation.
3. *Why use a precision rectifier in ECG?* → ECG signals are ~1 mV, far below 0.7 V diode threshold.
4. *What happens if you increase the input frequency beyond slew rate limit?* → The output becomes a **triangular wave** (slewing distortion) instead of following the sine wave.
5. *Difference between half-wave and full-wave precision rectifier?* → Half-wave gives only one polarity; full-wave gives $\vert V_s \vert$ (absolute value).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Final KTU Strategy:** In the lab exam, the moment you read *"small signal"* or *"below 0.7 V"* or *"precision"* — your hand should automatically draw the **op-amp + 2 diodes** circuit. This single recognition can save you 14 marks!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Multivibrator using Timer IC NE555
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Multivibrator using Timer IC NE555

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Timer IC NE555 — Academic Definition

The **NE555** is a highly stable, monolithic **8-pin timing circuit** introduced by Signetics in 1972, capable of producing accurate time delays or oscillation. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCECL407)**, it is used as the core building block for two fundamental multivibrator configurations: the **Astable Multivibrator** (free-running oscillator) and the **Monostable Multivibrator** (one-shot pulse generator).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module Experiment 2):**
> The student is expected to *design, hardware-implement, and measure* the output frequency and duty cycle of an Astable Multivibrator and the pulse width of a Monostable Multivibrator using NE555. Internal architecture questions, derivation of timing equations, and design of `R` and `C` for a given frequency are the most frequently asked concepts in KTU University Examinations.

A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative electronic circuit used to implement a bistable, monostable, or astable function, depending on how the stages are coupled.

| Configuration | Number of Stable States | Common Name | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bistable | 2 stable states | Flip-Flop | Memory element, latch |
| Monostable | 1 stable state | One-Shot | Pulse generation, timer |
| Astable | 0 stable states | Free-Running Oscillator | Clock generator, LED flasher, tone generation |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Water Tank Threshold Switch"

Imagine a **water tank** with two sensors:
- A **high-level sensor** at the 2/3 mark (this is the **THRESHOLD pin — pin 6**).
- A **low-level sensor** at the 1/3 mark (this is the **TRIGGER pin — pin 2**).
- A **drain valve** at the bottom controlled by an electric motor (this is the **DISCHARGE pin — pin 7**).

A control logic (the **internal SR Flip-Flop**) operates the valve as follows:
- When water rises above the **high sensor (2/3 Vcc)** → the valve **opens** and water drains out.
- When water falls below the **low sensor (1/3 Vcc)** → the valve **closes** and water starts filling again.

This continuous **fill–drain** cycle generates a **square-wave-like oscillation** at the output (pin 3). This is exactly how the NE555 operates as an **Astable Multivibrator**.

For the **Monostable** mode, the analogy changes: water is filled continuously, but a **trigger pulse** momentarily opens a "dump valve" that empties the tank completely. The tank then refills steadily until the high sensor is reached again, after which the system returns to its **single stable state** (full tank). The time taken to refill is the **pulse width** of the output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants to Remember Forever:**
> - Upper Threshold Voltage $\rightarrow V_{TH} = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$
> - Lower Trigger Voltage $\rightarrow V_{TR} = \dfrac{1}{3} V_{CC}$
> - Hysteresis Window $\rightarrow \Delta V = \dfrac{1}{3} V_{CC}$
> - Logic Inversion at Output $\rightarrow$ HIGH when flip-flop is Reset (for Astable charging phase)

### 1.3 Pin Configuration of NE555 — Quick Reference

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | GND | Ground reference (0 V) |
| 2 | TRIGGER | Activates timing when input falls below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ |
| 3 | OUTPUT | Drives load (up to 200 mA sink/source) |
| 4 | RESET | Active-low reset; tie to $V_{CC}$ for normal operation |
| 5 | CONTROL VOLTAGE | Allows external modulation of threshold (decouple with 0.01 µF to GND) |
| 6 | THRESHOLD | Disables timing when input rises above $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ |
| 7 | DISCHARGE | Open-collector transistor to GND; used to discharge timing capacitor |
| 8 | $V_{CC}$ | Supply voltage (**+5 V to +15 V**) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of NE555

The NE555 comprises three primary sub-blocks:

1. **A Voltage Divider** — Three identical **5 kΩ** resistors (hence the name "**555**") that produce reference voltages of $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$.
2. **Two Comparators** — A **Threshold Comparator** (compares pin 6 with $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$) and a **Trigger Comparator** (compares pin 2 with $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$).
3. **An SR Flip-Flop** — Its $\overline{Q}$ output drives the output stage (pin 3) and the discharge transistor (pin 7).
4. **A High-Current Output Stage** — A totem-pole driver capable of sourcing/sinking **200 mA**.
5. **A Discharge Transistor** — An open-collector NPN transistor connected between pin 7 and GND.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Logic (Truth Table):**
> - If $V_2 < \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ → Trigger comparator → SET flip-flop → Output = HIGH, Discharge transistor OFF.
> - If $V_6 > \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ → Threshold comparator → RESET flip-flop → Output = LOW, Discharge transistor ON.
> - If $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC} < V_6 < \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ → Both comparators inactive → Output **holds its previous state**.

### 2.2 Astable Multivibrator — Operating Principle

In the Astable configuration, pins **2 and 6 are tied together**, and the capacitor `C` is charged through resistors `R1` (from $V_{CC}$) and `R2` (in series), and discharged through `R2` only (via pin 7).

**Charging Cycle (Output HIGH):**
- Capacitor charges from $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ toward $V_{CC}$ through $(R_1 + R_2)$.
- Charging stops when $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (Threshold comparator fires).
- Time duration: $t_H = 0.693 (R_1 + R_2) C$

**Discharging Cycle (Output LOW):**
- Discharge transistor turns ON, shorting pin 7 to GND.
- Capacitor discharges from $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ toward 0 V through $R_2$ only.
- Discharging stops when $V_C$ reaches $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ (Trigger comparator fires).
- Time duration: $t_L = 0.693 R_2 C$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why can the capacitor never reach 0 V or $V_{CC}$?**
> Because the flip-flop toggles the moment the capacitor voltage crosses the **upper threshold** ($\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$) or the **lower threshold** ($\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$), the capacitor voltage is always bounded between these two reference levels. This is the essence of **Schmitt-Trigger Action** built into the NE555.

### 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Operating Principle

In the Monostable configuration, pin **6 (Threshold)** is connected to the timing capacitor, and pin **2 (Trigger)** receives an external negative-going trigger pulse.

**Stable State (Output LOW):**
- In the resting state, the discharge transistor is ON, so the capacitor is shorted to GND, holding $V_C = 0$ V.
- Output remains LOW indefinitely until a trigger arrives.

**Quasi-Stable State (Output HIGH):**
- A negative trigger pulse on pin 2 ($V_2 < \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$) SETs the flip-flop.
- Output goes HIGH, and discharge transistor turns OFF.
- Capacitor now charges from 0 V toward $V_{CC}$ through resistor `R`.
- This state **automatically terminates** when $V_C = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ → Threshold comparator fires → flip-flop resets → Output returns LOW, capacitor discharges rapidly to 0 V.
- Pulse width: $T = 1.1 R C$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:**
> The Monostable Multivibrator is widely used in **digital logic systems for pulse stretching, debouncing mechanical switches, and generating precise time delays** in sequential control circuits. The Astable version is used as a **clock generator** for digital systems, an **audio tone generator**, and an **LED/buzzer flasher** in alarm circuits.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Configuration | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Astable** | HIGH time ($t_H$) | $0.693 (R_1 + R_2) C$ | seconds | Charging path: $V_{CC} \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow C$ |
| **Astable** | LOW time ($t_L$) | $0.693 R_2 C$ | seconds | Discharging path: $C \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow$ pin 7 |
| **Astable** | Total Period ($T$) | $0.693 (R_1 + 2R_2) C$ | seconds | $T = t_H + t_L$ |
| **Astable** | Frequency ($f$) | $\dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) C}$ | Hz | Inverse of $T$ |
| **Astable** | Duty Cycle ($D$) | $\dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$ | percent | Always $> 50\%$ if $R_1 \neq 0$ |
| **Monostable** | Pulse Width ($T$) | $1.1 R C$ | seconds | Charging from 0 V to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ |
| **Both** | Threshold voltage | $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | volts | Pin 6 reference |
| **Both** | Trigger voltage | $\dfrac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ | volts | Pin 2 reference |
| **Both** | Charging constant | $t = RC \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{\infty} - V_{initial}}{V_{\infty} - V_{final}}\right)$ | seconds | General RC charging equation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often confuse the factor **0.693** with **1.1**. Remember:
> - Astable uses **0.693** because it charges **between 1/3 and 2/3** $V_{CC}$ (a ratio of 2 → ln 2 = 0.693).
> - Monostable uses **1.1** because it charges **from 0 to 2/3** $V_{CC}$ (ln 3 ≈ 1.0986 ≈ 1.1).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Astable Multivibrator Timing Equations

**General RC Charging/Discharging Equation:**

For a capacitor charging from $V_i$ to $V_f$ through resistance `R` toward a final voltage $V_{\infty}$, the instantaneous voltage is:

$$V_C(t) = V_{\infty} + (V_i - V_{\infty}) e^{-t/RC}$$

Solving for time `t`:

$$t = RC \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{\infty} - V_i}{V_{\infty} - V_f}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Derive $t_H$ (Charging Duration, Output HIGH):**

During charging:
- Initial capacitor voltage: $V_i = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$
- Final capacitor voltage (when comparator fires): $V_f = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$
- Asymptotic voltage (target supply): $V_{\infty} = V_{CC}$
- Charging resistance: $R = R_1 + R_2$ (since both are in series with the supply)
- Charging capacitance: $C$

Substituting into the general equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_H &= (R_1 + R_2) C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{CC} - \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}}{V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= (R_1 + R_2) C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}}{\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= (R_1 + R_2) C \cdot \ln(2) \\[6pt]
&= (R_1 + R_2) C \cdot 0.693 \\[6pt]
\therefore t_H &= 0.693 (R_1 + R_2) C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Derive $t_L$ (Discharging Duration, Output LOW):**

During discharging:
- Initial capacitor voltage: $V_i = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$
- Final capacitor voltage: $V_f = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$
- Asymptotic voltage (toward GND): $V_{\infty} = 0$ V
- Discharging resistance: $R = R_2$ only (because discharge transistor shorts pin 7 to GND, bypassing $R_1$)

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_L &= R_2 C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{0 - \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}}{0 - \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= R_2 C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}}{\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= R_2 C \cdot \ln(2) \\[6pt]
&= R_2 C \cdot 0.693 \\[6pt]
\therefore t_L &= 0.693 R_2 C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive Total Period `T`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= t_H + t_L \\[4pt]
&= 0.693 (R_1 + R_2) C + 0.693 R_2 C \\[4pt]
&= 0.693 [(R_1 + R_2) + R_2] C \\[4pt]
\therefore T &= 0.693 (R_1 + 2R_2) C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive Frequency `f`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \dfrac{1}{T} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.693 (R_1 + 2R_2) C} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) C} \quad \left[\text{since } \dfrac{1}{0.693} \approx 1.44\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive Duty Cycle `D`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.693 (R_1 + R_2) C}{0.693 (R_1 + 2R_2) C} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Monostable Multivibrator Pulse Width

**Conditions for the Monostable Charging Cycle:**
- Initial capacitor voltage: $V_i = 0$ V (capacitor was shorted to GND by discharge transistor)
- Final capacitor voltage: $V_f = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ (Threshold comparator fires)
- Asymptotic voltage: $V_{\infty} = V_{CC}$
- Charging resistance: $R = R$ (the single timing resistor)

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= R C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{CC} - 0}{V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= R C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= R C \cdot \ln(3) \\[6pt]
&= R C \cdot 1.0986 \\[6pt]
\therefore T &\approx 1.1 R C
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Design Problem)

> **Problem:** Design an Astable Multivibrator using NE555 to generate a square wave of frequency **1 kHz** with a duty cycle of **60%**. Assume $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Write the duty cycle equation:

$$D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} = 0.60$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 + R_2 &= 0.60 (R_1 + 2R_2) \\
R_1 + R_2 &= 0.60 R_1 + 1.20 R_2 \\
0.40 R_1 &= 0.20 R_2 \\
R_2 &= 2 R_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Use the frequency equation $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C} = 1000$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 + 2R_2 &= \dfrac{1.44}{f \cdot C} \\[4pt]
R_1 + 2(2R_1) &= \dfrac{1.44}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
5 R_1 &= \dfrac{1.44}{10^{-4}} \\[4pt]
5 R_1 &= 14400 \\[4pt]
R_1 &= 2880 \, \Omega \approx 2.88 \, k\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Find $R_2$:

$$R_2 = 2 \times R_1 = 5.76 \, k\Omega$$

Step 4 — Practical values (using standard E12 resistor series):

$R_1 \approx 2.7 \, k\Omega$ and $R_2 \approx 5.6 \, k\Omega$.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Astable | Monostable |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Number of stable states | 0 (continuously oscillates) | 1 (output rests LOW) |
| Trigger input | None (self-triggered) | Required (negative pulse on pin 2) |
| Threshold connection | Tied to pin 2 | Independent |
| Output frequency | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$ | Not applicable (single pulse) |
| Output pulse width | N/A | $T = 1.1 RC$ |
| Charging resistor | $R_1 + R_2$ | $R$ |
| Discharging resistor | $R_2$ | 0 (direct to GND via pin 7) |
| Minimum duty cycle | $> 50\%$ (theoretically) | N/A |
| Used for | Clock, oscillator, flasher | Timer, delay, debounce |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NE555 Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVCC[VCC - Pin 8] --> nodeR1a[5k Resistor 1]
    nodeR1a --> nodeR1b[5k Resistor 2]
    nodeR1b --> nodeR1c[5k Resistor 3]
    nodeR1c --> nodeGND[GND - Pin 1]
    nodeR1a -.->|2/3 VCC ref| nodeCompTH[Threshold Comparator]
    nodeR1b -.->|1/3 VCC ref| nodeCompTR[Trigger Comparator]
    nodePin6[Pin 6 THRESHOLD] --> nodeCompTH
    nodePin2[Pin 2 TRIGGER] --> nodeCompTR
    nodeCompTH --> nodeFF[SR Flip Flop]
    nodeCompTR --> nodeFF
    nodeFF --> nodeOut[Pin 3 OUTPUT]
    nodeFF --> nodeDischarge[Pin 7 DISCHARGE Transistor]
    nodePin4[Pin 4 RESET] --> nodeFF
    nodeFF --> nodeOutStage[Totem Pole Output Driver]
    nodeOutStage --> nodeOut
    nodeCV[Pin 5 CTRL VOLT] -.-> nodeCompTH
```

### 4.2 Astable Multivibrator — Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeVCCA[VCC] --> nodeR1a[R1]
    nodeR1a --> nodeNodeA[Node A]
    nodeNodeA --> nodeR2[R2]
    nodeR2 --> nodeNodeB[Node B]
    nodeNodeB --> nodeC[Capacitor C]
    nodeC --> nodeGND2[GND]
    nodeNodeA --> nodePin7[Pin 7 DISCHARGE]
    nodeNodeA --> nodePin6[Pin 6 THRESHOLD]
    nodeNodeA --> nodePin2[Pin 2 TRIGGER]
    nodePin8[Pin 8 VCC] --> nodeVCCA
    nodePin1[Pin 1 GND] --> nodeGND2
    nodePin4[Pin 4 RESET] --> nodeVCCA
    nodePin3[Pin 3 OUTPUT] --> nodeLoad[Load LED or CRO]
    nodeLoad --> nodeGND2
    nodePin5[Pin 5 CTRL VOLT] --> nodeCV[0.01uF to GND]
```

### 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeTrig[External Trigger Pulse] --> nodePin2M[Pin 2 TRIGGER]
    nodeVCCM[VCC] --> nodePin8M[Pin 8 VCC]
    nodeVCCM --> nodeRM[Timing Resistor R]
    nodeRM --> nodePin6M[Pin 6 THRESHOLD]
    nodePin6M --> nodePin7M[Pin 7 DISCHARGE]
    nodePin6M --> nodeCM[Timing Capacitor C]
    nodeCM --> nodeGNDM[GND]
    nodePin1M[Pin 1 GND] --> nodeGNDM
    nodePin4M[Pin 4 RESET] --> nodeVCCM
    nodePin3M[Pin 3 OUTPUT] --> nodePulseOut[One Shot Pulse Output]
    nodePin5M[Pin 5 CTRL VOLT] --> nodeCVM[0.01uF to GND]
```

### 4.4 Astable Waveform — Timing Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph timeAxis[Time Domain]
        direction LR
        nodeT0[T0] --> nodeT1[T1]
        nodeT1 --> nodeT2[T2]
        nodeT2 --> nodeT3[T3]
        nodeT3 --> nodeT4[T4]
    end
    nodeCapWave[Capacitor Voltage Vc] -.->|Charges from 1/3 VCC to 2/3 VCC| nodeCapRamp[Linear Looking Exponential Ramp]
    nodeOutWave[Output Pin 3] -.->|HIGH during charging| nodeOutHigh[Stays HIGH for tH]
    nodeOutWave -.->|LOW during discharging| nodeOutLow[Stays LOW for tL]
    nodeCapWave2[Vc DROPS from 2/3 VCC to 1/3 VCC] --> nodeOutWave2[Output switches LOW]
    nodeCapWave3[Vc RISES from 1/3 VCC to 2/3 VCC] --> nodeOutWave3[Output switches HIGH]
```

### 4.5 Monostable Waveform — Timing Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStable[Stable State Output LOW Vc = 0V] --> nodeTrigger[Negative Trigger on Pin 2]
    nodeTrigger --> nodeSetFlip[Flip Flop SETS]
    nodeSetFlip --> nodeCapCharge[Capacitor charges 0 to 2/3 VCC through R]
    nodeCapCharge --> nodeThresholdFire[Threshold Comparator fires at 2/3 VCC]
    nodeThresholdFire --> nodeResetFlip[Flip Flop RESETS]
    nodeResetFlip --> nodeReturn[Output returns LOW]
    nodeReturn --> nodeCapDischarge[Capacitor discharges rapidly via Pin 7]
    nodeCapDischarge --> nodeStable
```

### 4.6 State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StableLow : Power ON
    StableLow --> QuasiHigh : Negative Trigger on Pin 2
    QuasiHigh --> StableLow : Vc reaches 2/3 VCC after time T = 1.1 RC
    StableLow --> StableLow : No Trigger or Vc less than 2/3 VCC
    note right of QuasiHigh : Output is HIGH for duration T
    note right of StableLow : Output is LOW indefinite
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *List any three applications of the NE555 timer IC in monostable and astable modes.*

**Model Answer:**

The NE555 timer IC has wide-ranging applications:

1. **In Astable Mode:** Used as a square-wave generator, clock pulse generator for digital circuits, LED/buzzer flasher, tone generator in alarm circuits, and PWM signal generation.
2. **In Monostable Mode:** Used for pulse stretching, switch debouncing in digital inputs, missing pulse detection, and precise time-delay generation in sequential control circuits.
3. **In Bistable Mode (with external wiring):** Used as a Schmitt trigger and flip-flop for signal conditioning.

*(Each correct application carries 1 mark. Mentioning the mode for each is essential to fetch full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *What is the function of the CONTROL VOLTAGE pin (Pin 5) of NE555? What precaution must be taken while using it?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Control Voltage pin (Pin 5)** allows the user to externally modulate the threshold and trigger reference voltages (which are normally fixed at $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ respectively by the internal 5 kΩ divider). Applying an external DC voltage to this pin changes the upper threshold to that voltage and the lower threshold to half of it.

**Precaution:** Pin 5 must always be **bypassed to ground through a 0.01 µF capacitor** to filter out any noise picked up from the supply rails. Without this capacitor, the timing becomes erratic and the output waveform gets distorted.

*(Function = 2 marks; Precaution = 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern, attempt any ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Design and Analysis

**(a)** *With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an Astable Multivibrator using NE555. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the duty cycle.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Design an Astable Multivibrator using NE555 to generate a square wave of frequency 5 kHz and duty cycle 75%. Choose $C = 10 \, nF$.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Circuit Operation:**
In the Astable configuration, pins **2 (Trigger)** and **6 (Threshold)** are tied together. Resistor $R_1$ is connected between $V_{CC}$ and the discharge node, $R_2$ is between the discharge node and the timing capacitor $C$, and pin 7 (Discharge) is connected to the junction of $R_1$ and $R_2$.

**Charging Phase:** Initially, the flip-flop is in the SET state (output HIGH), discharge transistor is OFF. Capacitor `C` charges toward $V_{CC}$ through the series combination $(R_1 + R_2)$. The moment the capacitor voltage reaches $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the threshold comparator fires and resets the flip-flop. Output becomes LOW.

**Discharging Phase:** With the flip-flop RESET, the discharge transistor turns ON, connecting pin 7 to GND. The capacitor now discharges through $R_2$ only. When $V_C$ drops to $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, the trigger comparator fires and sets the flip-flop, repeating the cycle.

**Derivation:** (Use derivation from Section 3.1, Steps 1–5)

$$f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) C} \quad ; \quad D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$$

*(Marks distribution: Circuit diagram = 2 marks, Charging/Discharging explanation = 2 marks, Frequency derivation = 2 marks, Duty cycle derivation = 1 mark.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

Given: $f = 5 \, kHz$, $D = 75\% = 0.75$, $C = 10 \, nF = 10 \times 10^{-9} \, F$.

**Step 1:** Express duty cycle:

$$\dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} = 0.75$$

Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 + R_2 &= 0.75 R_1 + 1.5 R_2 \\
0.25 R_1 &= 0.5 R_2 \\
R_2 &= 0.5 R_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Use the frequency equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C} = 5000 \\[4pt]
R_1 + 2R_2 &= \dfrac{1.44}{5000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} \\[4pt]
R_1 + 2(0.5 R_1) &= \dfrac{1.44}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \\[4pt]
2 R_1 &= 28800 \\[4pt]
R_1 &= 14400 \, \Omega = 14.4 \, k\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $R_2$:

$$R_2 = 0.5 \times 14.4 \, k\Omega = 7.2 \, k\Omega$$

**Practical Standard Values:** $R_1 = 15 \, k\Omega$ and $R_2 = 6.8 \, k\Omega$ (nearest E12 values).

*(Marks distribution: Duty cycle relation setup = 2 marks, Frequency equation substitution = 3 marks, Final answer with units = 2 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Monostable Analysis and Design

**(a)** *Explain the working of a Monostable Multivibrator using NE555 with a neat circuit diagram and waveforms. Derive the expression for the pulse width.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Design a Monostable Multivibrator using NE555 to generate an output pulse of width 2 milliseconds. Assume $C = 1 \, \mu F$. Find the required value of R.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Circuit:** Pin 2 (Trigger) is connected to an external pulse source through a coupling capacitor with a pull-up resistor to $V_{CC}$. Pin 6 (Threshold) and Pin 7 (Discharge) are tied together and connected to the timing capacitor `C`. The other end of `C` goes to GND. A resistor `R` is connected between $V_{CC}$ and pins 6/7.

**Operation:**

- **Stable State:** Discharge transistor ON, $V_C = 0$ V, Output = LOW.
- **Triggering:** A negative pulse ($< \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$) on pin 2 SETs the flip-flop. Output = HIGH, discharge transistor OFF.
- **Quasi-Stable State:** Capacitor charges through `R` from 0 V toward $V_{CC}$. When $V_C = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, threshold comparator fires, flip-flop RESETS, output = LOW, and capacitor rapidly discharges.

**Pulse Width Derivation:** (Use derivation from Section 3.2)

$$T = R C \ln(3) \approx 1.1 R C$$

**Waveforms:** (Describe — Trigger pulse (narrow negative spike), Capacitor voltage (rising exponential reaching 2/3 $V_{CC}$), Output pulse (HIGH for duration `T`))

*(Marks distribution: Circuit diagram = 2 marks, Operation explanation = 2 marks, Waveform description = 1 mark, Derivation = 2 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

Given: Pulse width $T = 2 \, ms = 2 \times 10^{-3} \, s$, $C = 1 \, \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-6} \, F$.

**Formula:**

$$T = 1.1 R C$$

**Solving for R:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{T}{1.1 \, C} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
&= 1.818 \, k\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Standard Value:** $R \approx 1.8 \, k\Omega$ (E12 series).

*(Marks distribution: Formula selection = 2 marks, Substitution and unit conversion = 3 marks, Final answer with proper units = 2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include $R_1$ in the charging path** — Many students write $t_H = 0.693 R_2 C$ instead of $0.693 (R_1 + R_2) C$, leading to completely wrong duty cycles and frequencies.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — Always convert kHz to Hz, µF to F, kΩ to Ω **before** substituting into the formula. Mixing units is the #1 reason for wrong answers.
> 3. **Confusing 0.693 with 1.1** — Astable uses 0.693 (because of ln 2), Monostable uses 1.1 (because of ln 3).
> 4. **Misidentifying the discharge transistor location** — Pin 7 is the DISCHARGE pin (open collector), NOT the output. Output is Pin 3.
> 5. **Omitting the 0.01 µF bypass capacitor on Pin 5** — Many students draw the circuit without this capacitor, leading to noise-injected erratic output. KTU examiners deduct marks for this.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for NE555 Multivibrators (KTU 2024)**

- ✅ The NE555 has **8 pins**. Pin 8 = $V_{CC}$, Pin 1 = GND, Pin 3 = OUTPUT, Pin 4 = RESET, Pin 5 = CTRL VOLT, Pin 7 = DISCHARGE.
- ✅ Pin 6 is the **Threshold** input (fires at $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$); Pin 2 is the **Trigger** input (fires at $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$).
- ✅ The internal divider uses **three 5 kΩ resistors** — hence the name "**555**".
- ✅ **Astable:** $t_H = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C$, $t_L = 0.693 R_2 C$, $T = 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C$, $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$, $D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2}$.
- ✅ **Monostable:** $T = 1.1 R C$ — the pulse width depends on **one resistor and one capacitor**.
- ✅ Astable **duty cycle is always > 50%** when $R_1 > 0$. To achieve $D < 50\%$, use a diode in parallel with $R_2$.
- ✅ Always bypass **Pin 5 to GND with 0.01 µF** to prevent noise injection.
- ✅ Always tie **Pin 4 (RESET) to $V_{CC}$** during normal operation; never leave it floating.
- ✅ Output stage (Pin 3) is a **totem-pole** driver capable of sourcing/sinking up to **200 mA** — sufficient to drive an LED or small relay directly.
- ✅ Monostable requires a **negative-going trigger pulse** of width less than the output pulse width.
- ✅ Practical component ranges: $R = 1 \, k\Omega$ to $1 \, M\Omega$; $C = 100 \, pF$ to $1000 \, \mu F$ — gives timing range from microseconds to hours.
- ✅ Standard supply voltage $V_{CC}$ = **+5 V to +15 V**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Power Supply using IC 723: Low Voltage and High Voltage Configurations, Short Circuit and Fold-back Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** DC Power Supply using IC 723: Low Voltage and High Voltage Configurations, Short Circuit and Fold-back Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **IC 723** (µA723 / LM723) is a monolithic, floating, precision voltage regulator manufactured by Texas Instruments, designed primarily for use in **series-pass regulator** configurations. It is capable of delivering output currents up to **150 mA** without external pass transistors, and up to **10 A** with external current-boosting transistors. Internally, it consists of a **zener diode reference (≈ 7.15 V)**, an **error amplifier**, a **series pass transistor**, and a **current-limiting transistor**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab (PCECL407)**, this experiment mandates the design, hardware realization, and performance evaluation of:
1. **Low-Voltage Regulator (Vout ≤ 7 V):** Where the unregulated DC input is low enough to keep $V_{IN} - V_{OUT} \geq V_{dropout}$.
2. **High-Voltage Regulator (Vout > 7 V):** Where $V_{IN}$ must be substantially higher, requiring careful biasing of the internal reference.
3. **Short-Circuit Protection (Constant Current Limit):** A safety circuit that prevents catastrophic damage when the output is shorted.
4. **Fold-Back Current Limiting:** An advanced protection scheme where the current limit *decreases* as output voltage drops, protecting both the load and the pass element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Students are expected to *design* the resistor values (R1, R2, Rsc) using formulas, *verify* them on a breadboard/PCB, and *plot* load regulation and line regulation characteristics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap With a Pressure Governor"

Imagine a **municipal water pipeline** that supplies raw, fluctuating pressure to your house. You connect a **pressure-reducing valve (governor)** at the inlet, and a **safety relief valve** at the outlet.

- The **unregulated DC supply** = the raw, surging water main.
- The **IC 723 error amplifier** = a vigilant plumber who constantly compares the output pressure (voltage) with a reference (a calibrated spring inside the governor). If the output pressure rises, the plumber *squeezes* the main pipe harder; if it falls, he *releases* it.
- The **series-pass transistor** (Q1 inside the 723 or external 2N3055) = the actual rotating valve handle doing the heavy mechanical work.
- The **short-circuit protection** = a circuit breaker that trips if you accidentally connect a hose directly to the main without any restriction, preventing flooding (overcurrent burn-out).
- **Fold-back protection** = a *smart* circuit breaker that doesn't just trip at full current; instead, as pressure (voltage) drops, the trip threshold *folds back* (lowers), so even a partial short doesn't fry the wires. This is far gentler on the supply.

This is precisely the architecture of a regulated DC power supply — and the IC 723 is one of the most classical, transparent, and pedagogically valuable chips to study it.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Reference Voltage | $V_{REF}$ | **7.15** | V |
| Reference Drift | $\Delta V_{REF}/\Delta T$ | **0.003** | %/°C |
| Maximum Input Voltage (non-boosted) | $V_{IN(max)}$ | **40** | V |
| Maximum Output Current (internal) | $I_{OUT(max)}$ | **150** | mA |
| Line Regulation | $S_V$ | **0.01** | %/V |
| Load Regulation | $R_{LO}$ | **0.03** | % |
| Dropout Voltage | $V_{DO}$ | **~3** | V |

> [!NOTE]
> The internal reference of **7.15 V** is the *pivotal boundary* between the low-voltage and high-voltage configurations of the 723. Whenever your desired $V_{OUT}$ is below this, you operate in low-voltage mode; above it, you must use the high-voltage configuration.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Internal Architecture of IC 723 (14-Pin DIP)

The IC 723 is a hierarchical sub-system containing **four distinct functional blocks**:

1. **Zener Reference Source** — A temperature-compensated buried zener (≈ 7.15 V) accessible at Pin 6 ($V_{REF}$). The cathode is at Pin 6, anode at Pin 7 (also acts as $V_{CC}$ for the chip).
2. **Error Amplifier (Differential)** — Inverting input at Pin 4, non-inverting at Pin 5, output at Pin 9. High open-loop gain (~74 dB).
3. **Series Pass Transistor (Q1)** — Collector at Pin 11, Base at Pin 10 (driven by error amplifier), Emitter at Pin 13.
4. **Current-Limiting Transistor (Q2)** — Base at Pin 2 (current-sense), Emitter at Pin 3, Collector at Pin 10 (sinks base-drive from Q1 to limit current).

### 2.1.1 Pin Configuration Map (14-Pin DIP, Top View)

| Pin | Function | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | NC (No Connection) | NC |
| 2 | Current Limit Sense | $C_{L}$ |
| 3 | Current Sense (Emitter) | $C_{S}$ |
| 4 | Inverting Input | INV |
| 5 | Non-Inverting Input | NI |
| 6 | Reference Voltage | $V_{REF}$ |
| 7 | $V_{CC}$ (− for low V) | $-V_{CC}$ |
| 8 | NC | NC |
| 9 | Frequency Compensation | COMP |
| 10 | Series Pass Base | $V_{B}$ |
| 11 | $V_{IN}$ (Collector of Q1) | $V_{IN}$ |
| 12 | $V_{IN}$ (Collector of Q1) | $V_{IN}$ |
| 13 | $V_{OUT}$ (Emitter of Q1) | $V_{OUT}$ |
| 14 | NC | NC |

---

## 2.2 Configuration Theory — Low Voltage Mode ($V_{OUT} \leq 7$ V)

In the **low-voltage configuration**, the regulated output $V_{OUT}$ is taken directly from Pin 13 (emitter of internal pass transistor Q1). The voltage divider (R1, R2) samples $V_{OUT}$ and feeds it back to the inverting input of the error amplifier. The non-inverting input is tied to the internal reference (Pin 6).

**Operating Principle (Logic Flow):**

- The error amplifier enforces the "virtual short" condition: $V_{INV} = V_{NI}$.
- Since $V_{NI} = V_{REF}$, the voltage at the junction of R1 and R2 must equal $V_{REF}$.
- $V_{OUT}$ appears across (R1 + R2), and $V_{REF}$ appears across R2 alone.
- Therefore, the ratio defines the output.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table — No Pipes Inside)

| Configuration | Output Voltage Equation | Boundary Condition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Voltage** | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$ | $V_{OUT} < V_{REF} \approx 7.15\text{ V}$ | 5 V logic, op-amp rails |
| **High Voltage (Follower)** | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$ | $V_{OUT} > V_{REF}$ | 12 V, 15 V rails |
| **High Voltage (Non-Follower)** | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \right) + \dfrac{R_1}{R_3} \cdot (V_{IN} - V_{REF})$ | Requires bootstrap R3 | 24 V industrial rails |
| **Current Limit (Short Ckt)** | $I_{LIMIT} = \dfrac{0.65\text{ V}}{R_{SC}}$ | $I_{LIM}$ constant | Basic protection |
| **Fold-back Limit** | $I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{OUT(NL)} \cdot R_2}{(R_1 + R_2) \cdot R_3} + \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_3}$ | $I_{LIM}$ drops as $V_{OUT}\downarrow$ | Advanced protection |

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **0.65 V** in the current-limit formula is the $V_{BE}$ of the internal current-sensing transistor Q2 — when the voltage across $R_{SC}$ reaches 0.65 V, Q2 turns on and shunts base drive away from Q1, clamping the output current.

---

## 2.3 Configuration Theory — High Voltage Mode ($V_{OUT} > 7$ V)

In the high-voltage configuration, the output cannot be tapped from Pin 13 (because the unregulated input $V_{IN}$ can be 30+ V, exceeding the $V_{CE}$ rating of the internal pass transistor). The solution is to use an **external NPN pass transistor** (e.g., 2N3055, BD139) whose collector is tied to the unregulated $V_{IN}$, base driven from Pin 10, and emitter delivers $V_{OUT}$.

**Two Sub-Cases Exist:**

1. **Follower Configuration (Floating Load):** The internal pass transistor Q1 acts as a follower; the external pass transistor is the master. Feedback is taken directly from the load to the inverting input.
2. **Non-Follower Configuration:** A bootstrap resistor R3 is added to provide base bias to the external pass transistor, allowing $V_{IN}$ to swing high without saturating the internal error amplifier.

---

## 2.4 Short-Circuit Protection (Constant Current Limit)

A sense resistor $R_{SC}$ is placed in series with the load current path. The voltage developed across $R_{SC}$ is $V_{SENSE} = I_{OUT} \cdot R_{SC}$. When $V_{SENSE}$ exceeds the $V_{BE}$ of Q2 (≈ 0.65 V), Q2 conducts, shunting base current away from Q1, preventing $I_{OUT}$ from rising further.

**Design Equation:**
$$I_{LIMIT} = \frac{V_{BE(Q2)}}{R_{SC}} = \frac{0.65 \text{ V}}{R_{SC}}$$

**Trade-off:** Choosing a small $R_{SC}$ wastes less power but allows higher fault current; a large $R_{SC}$ improves protection but increases quiescent dissipation.

---

## 2.5 Fold-Back Current Limiting (The Advanced Scheme)

In constant-current limiting, even at full short, the regulator still dissipates $P = V_{IN} \cdot I_{LIMIT}$, which is substantial. In **fold-back limiting**, the current limit is *not* constant — it *folds back* (decreases) as $V_{OUT}$ falls.

**Behavioural Curve:**
- At nominal load (full $V_{OUT}$): current limit = $I_{MAX}$ (e.g., 1 A).
- As load resistance decreases, $V_{OUT}$ drops; the current limit *reduces* linearly.
- At full short ($V_{OUT} = 0$): current limit = $I_{SC}$ (e.g., 0.1 A).
- Thus, the *worst-case* dissipation is $V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC}$, much lower than the constant scheme.

The fold-back network uses an additional voltage divider (R1, R2) such that as $V_{OUT}$ drops, the bias on the sense transistor is reduced, lowering the trip threshold.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Laboratory Bench Power Supplies:** Almost every commercial lab PSU (0–30 V, 0–3 A) uses an IC 723 or its modern equivalent (LM317 + op-amp).
- **Avionics & Military:** The 723's wide temperature range and radiation tolerance make it suitable for legacy aerospace systems.
- **Telecom -48 V Buses:** Fold-back protection is critical in centralized office power plants where a single short could otherwise cascade.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage (Low Voltage Configuration)

**Starting Point:** The error amplifier is configured as a non-inverting amplifier with the feedback network (R1, R2) sampling $V_{OUT}$.

**Step 1 — Virtual Short Condition:**
The op-amp inside the 723 drives the pass transistor such that the differential input voltage is zero. Therefore, the inverting input voltage equals the non-inverting input voltage.

$$V_{INV} = V_{NI}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Each Voltage:**
- $V_{NI}$ is tied to $V_{REF}$ (Pin 6 to Pin 5).
- $V_{INV}$ is the voltage at the junction of R1 and R2, sampled by Pin 4.

$$V_{REF} = V_{R2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Voltage Divider:**
The current through R1 and R2 (assuming negligible input bias) is:

$$I = \frac{V_{OUT}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The voltage across R2 is:

$$V_{R2} = I \cdot R_2 = V_{OUT} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve:**

$$V_{REF} = V_{OUT} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Design Example (KTU Lab):**
*Design a 5 V regulator using the low-voltage configuration.*

Let $R_2 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value). Then:

$$5 = 7.15 \cdot \frac{R_1 + 4.7\text{k}}{4.7\text{k}}$$

$$\frac{5 \cdot 4.7\text{k}}{7.15} = R_1 + 4.7\text{k}$$

$$3.286\text{k} = R_1 + 4.7\text{k}$$

$$R_1 = 3.286\text{k} - 4.7\text{k} = -1.414\text{k} \quad (\text{physically impossible!})$$

**Interpretation:** A 5 V output is *below* 7.15 V, so this is fine, but $R_1$ came out negative. The error is that the equation requires $V_{OUT} > V_{REF}$ *only if* both R1 and R2 are positive. Actually, in low-voltage mode, the output is taken from Pin 13 (emitter), and $R_1$ can be replaced by a wire + a small resistor. The correct low-voltage formula for $V_{OUT} < V_{REF}$ uses a different topology (non-inverting configuration where output is *between* R1 and R2):

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{(when } V_{OUT} \leq V_{REF}\text{)}$$

Re-solving: With $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$5 = 7.15 \cdot \frac{1\text{k}}{R_1 + 1\text{k}}$$

$$R_1 + 1\text{k} = \frac{7.15 \cdot 1\text{k}}{5} = 1.43\text{k}$$

$$R_1 = 0.43\text{k} = 430 \, \Omega$$

**Final Design (Low-Voltage 5 V):** $R_1 = 430 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{SC} = 0.65/0.15 \approx 4.3 \, \Omega$ (for 150 mA internal limit).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sense Resistor for Current Limit

**Goal:** Design $R_{SC}$ such that the output current never exceeds $I_{LIMIT}$ = 100 mA.

**Step 1 — Critical Sense Voltage:**
The internal transistor Q2 turns on when $V_{SENSE} = V_{BE} \approx 0.65 \text{ V}$.

**Step 2 — Ohm's Law on $R_{SC}$:**

$$V_{SENSE} = I_{LIMIT} \cdot R_{SC}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_{SC}$:**

$$R_{SC} = \frac{V_{SENSE}}{I_{LIMIT}} = \frac{0.65 \text{ V}}{100 \text{ mA}} = 6.5 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Power Rating of $R_{SC}$:**

$$P_{R_{SC}} = I_{LIMIT}^2 \cdot R_{SC} = (0.1)^2 \cdot 6.5 = 0.065 \text{ W}$$

Use a **0.25 W** resistor (safety factor of ~4).

**Step 5 — Worst-Case Power Dissipation in IC:**
At short circuit, the full input voltage drops across Q1.

$$P_{Q1} = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{LIMIT} = (15 - 0) \cdot 0.1 = 1.5 \text{ W}$$

This is the *maximum* heat the 723 must dissipate with constant current limit. **This is why fold-back is preferred.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fold-Back Current Limit Equation

In fold-back, the sense voltage is split between $R_{SC}$ (in the load path) and a divider (R1, R2) that *partially cancels* the sense voltage as $V_{OUT}$ falls.

**Operating Equations:**

**At Normal Load (Full $V_{OUT}$):** The current limit is at maximum, $I_{MAX}$.

$$V_{SENSE} = V_{BE} + \frac{V_{OUT} \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Wait — let me reformulate carefully. The fold-back network adds a voltage across the sense transistor that is *proportional to $V_{OUT}$*. As $V_{OUT}$ drops, this bias voltage drops, so the *actual* $V_{SENSE}$ required to trip Q2 increases — i.e., the *current* required to trip increases? No — actually the current *decreases* because the bias is subtracted from the trip threshold.

**Corrected Fold-Back Formula:**

The current limit at any $V_{OUT}$ is:

$$I_{LIMIT}(V_{OUT}) = \frac{V_{BE} \cdot (R_1 + R_2) + V_{OUT} \cdot R_2}{R_{SC} \cdot (R_1 + R_2)}$$

**At $V_{OUT} = V_{OUT(NL)}$ (full output):**

$$I_{MAX} = \frac{V_{BE} \cdot (R_1 + R_2) + V_{OUT(NL)} \cdot R_2}{R_{SC} \cdot (R_1 + R_2)}$$

**At $V_{OUT} = 0$ (full short):**

$$I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

**Derivation of $I_{SC}$:**
Setting $V_{OUT} = 0$ in the fold-back equation:

$$I_{LIMIT}(0) = \frac{V_{BE} \cdot (R_1 + R_2) + 0 \cdot R_2}{R_{SC} \cdot (R_1 + R_2)} = \frac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

This is identical to the constant-current case at $V_{OUT} = 0$ — which is a useful sanity check.

**Step-by-Step Design Walk-through (KTU Numerical):**

*Design a fold-back limited 12 V, 1 A max supply. Choose $I_{SC} = 0.2 \text{ A}$.*

**Step A — Choose $R_{SC}$ from short-circuit condition:**

$$R_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{I_{SC}} = \frac{0.65}{0.2} = 3.25 \, \Omega$$

**Step B — Choose fold-back ratio (typically 5:1 to 10:1):**
Pick $I_{MAX}/I_{SC} = 5$, so $I_{MAX} = 5 \cdot 0.2 = 1 \text{ A}$. ✓

**Step C — Solve for (R1 + R2)/R2 ratio:**

The fold-back equation at full output:

$$I_{MAX} = \frac{V_{BE} \cdot (R_1 + R_2) + V_{OUT(NL)} \cdot R_2}{R_{SC} \cdot (R_1 + R_2)}$$

Substituting values:

$$1 = \frac{0.65 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) + 12 \cdot R_2}{3.25 \cdot (R_1 + R_2)}$$

$$3.25 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) = 0.65 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) + 12 R_2$$

$$2.6 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) = 12 R_2$$

$$\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = \frac{12}{2.6} = 4.615$$

$$R_1 = 3.615 \cdot R_2$$

**Step D — Pick standard value for $R_2$:**
$R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega \Rightarrow R_1 = 3.615 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 3.6 \text{ k}\Omega + 15 \, \Omega$ (use 3.6 k standard).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Protection Schemes

| Parameter | No Protection | Constant Current | Fold-Back |
|---|---|---|---|
| Worst-case $P_{Q1}$ at short | $\infty$ (destruction) | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{LIM}$ | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC}$ |
| Number of Resistors | 2 | 3 (+ $R_{SC}$) | 5 (+ $R_{SC}$, R1, R2) |
| Recovery on Short Removal | N/A | Automatic | Automatic |
| Design Complexity | Trivial | Easy | Moderate |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Low | **High** | **High** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of IC 723

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVCC["VCC Unregulated Input Pin 11 12"]
    nodeZener["Internal Zener Reference 7.15 V Pin 6"]
    nodeErrAmp["Error Amplifier Differential Gain 74 dB Pin 4 5 9"]
    nodePassInt["Internal Pass Transistor Q1 Collector Pin 11 Base Pin 10 Emitter Pin 13"]
    nodePassExt["External Pass Transistor 2N3055 Used in High Voltage Mode"]
    nodeSenseR["Current Sense Resistor RSC Pin 2 3"]
    nodeCurLim["Current Limit Transistor Q2 Pin 2 3 10"]
    nodeFoldDiv["Fold Back Divider R1 R2 Network"]
    nodeComp["Frequency Compensation Pin 9 to GND via C"]

```
