# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "dropout voltage" in the context of a series voltage regulator. What is the typical dropout voltage of the IC 723?**

**Model Answer:**

The **dropout voltage** ($V_{DO}$) of a series voltage regulator is defined as the minimum voltage difference between the unregulated input ($V_{IN}$) and the regulated output ($V_{OUT}$) that is required to maintain proper regulation. Below this threshold, the pass transistor enters saturation, and the regulator loses its ability to hold the output constant — i.e., the output "drops out" of regulation and follows the input.

For the **IC 723**, the typical dropout voltage is approximately **3 V**, owing to the $V_{CE(sat)}$ of the internal pass transistor Q1 plus headroom required by the internal reference and current-sense circuitry.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between constant current limiting and fold-back current limiting in a linear regulator.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Constant Current Limiting | Fold-Back Current Limiting |
|---|---|---|
| Trip behaviour | Current is clamped to a *fixed* $I_{LIM}$ once $V_{SENSE} > V_{BE}$ | Current limit *decreases* as $V_{OUT}$ falls |
| Current at full short | $= I_{LIM}$ | $= I_{SC} \ll I_{LIM}$ |
| Worst-case power dissipation in pass element | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{LIM}$ | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC}$ (much lower) |
| Additional components | One sense resistor $R_{SC}$ | Sense resistor + voltage divider (R1, R2) |
| Application | Low-power, simple supplies | High-current, thermally-constrained supplies |

In **constant current limit**, the regulator can dissipate its *maximum* power at the moment of short circuit, requiring a robust heat sink. In **fold-back limit**, the current automatically reduces as the output voltage collapses, drastically reducing dissipation and protecting the pass transistor from thermal runaway.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **low-voltage regulator using IC 723** designed to deliver an output of **+5 V at 100 mA**. Derive the expressions for the resistor values R1, R2, and $R_{SC}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a **short-circuit protection circuit** for the above regulator such that the output current is limited to **150 mA** under fault conditions. Calculate the worst-case power dissipation in the IC 723 if the unregulated input is **15 V**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Circuit Topology:**
In the low-voltage configuration, the unregulated DC (15 V) is applied to Pins 11, 12 (collector of internal Q1). The reference $V_{REF}$ = 7.15 V is taken from Pin 6 and tied to Pin 5 (non-inverting input). A feedback divider (R1, R2) samples $V_{OUT}$ at Pin 13 and feeds it to Pin 4 (inverting input). The output is taken from Pin 13 (emitter of Q1).

**Derivation of R1 and R2:**

For low-voltage mode ($V_{OUT} \leq V_{REF}$), the error amplifier is configured in a non-inverting topology. The voltage at Pin 4 (junction of R1 and R2) must equal $V_{REF}$:

$$V_{R2} = V_{REF} = V_{OUT} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Solving:

$$R_1 = R_2 \cdot \left( \frac{V_{REF}}{V_{OUT}} - 1 \right)$$

**Numerical Design:**
Choose $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard). Then:

$$R_1 = 1\text{k} \cdot \left( \frac{7.15}{5} - 1 \right) = 1\text{k} \cdot 0.43 = 430 \, \Omega$$

**Use standard value:** $R_1 = 430 \, \Omega$ (or 470 Ω as nearest E12).

**Compensation Capacitor:** A 100 pF to 0.1 µF capacitor is connected between Pin 9 and Pin 7 (ground) to prevent oscillation.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Design of $R_{SC}$:**
The current limit is enforced when the voltage across $R_{SC}$ reaches the $V_{BE}$ of the internal sense transistor Q2 (≈ 0.65 V):

$$I_{LIMIT} \cdot R_{SC} = V_{BE} = 0.65 \text{ V}$$

$$R_{SC} = \frac{0.65}{0.15} = 4.33 \, \Omega \approx 4.3 \, \Omega \text{ (standard)}$$

**Power Rating of $R_{SC}$:**

$$P_{R_{SC}} = I_{LIMIT}^2 \cdot R_{SC} = (0.15)^2 \cdot 4.3 = 0.097 \text{ W}$$

Use a **0.25 W** resistor.

**Worst-Case Power Dissipation in IC 723:**
At full short circuit, the output is 0 V, so the *entire* unregulated input drops across the internal pass transistor Q1:

$$P_{Q1(max)} = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{LIMIT} = (15 - 0) \cdot 0.15 = 2.25 \text{ W}$$

**Heatsink Requirement:** The 723 in a 14-pin DIP has a thermal resistance of $\theta_{JA} \approx 50 °C/W$. With a 25 °C ambient, the junction temperature rise is $2.25 \cdot 50 = 112.5 °C$, giving a junction temperature of $137.5 °C$ — this **exceeds the 150 °C maximum** and demands a small clip-on heatsink. **A fold-back scheme is recommended in practice to reduce this dissipation.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **high-voltage follower configuration** of the IC 723 used to design a **+15 V, 1 A regulated power supply** with an external pass transistor 2N3055. Draw the circuit and derive the feedback resistor values. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a **fold-back current limiting circuit** for the above supply such that $I_{MAX} = 1 \text{ A}$ at $V_{OUT} = 15 \text{ V}$ and $I_{SC} = 0.2 \text{ A}$ at $V_{OUT} = 0 \text{ V}$. Compute R1, R2, and $R_{SC}$. Compare the worst-case power dissipation with the constant-current scheme. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Circuit Topology (High-Voltage Follower):**
- Unregulated input $V_{IN}$ = 20 V applied to collector of external 2N3055.
- Emitter of 2N3055 is the regulated output (15 V).
- Base of 2N3055 is driven from Pin 10 (internal Q1 collector path).
- The internal Q1 is wired as an emitter follower: emitter at Pin 13, base from Pin 9.
- Feedback divider (R1, R2) samples $V_{OUT}$ (15 V) and feeds Pin 4. Pin 5 is tied to $V_{REF}$ (7.15 V).

**Why Follower?** Because the load is *floating* (not referenced to ground), and the internal pass transistor can only drop $\approx 5 \text{ V}$ safely, the external 2N3055 handles the bulk of the heat.

**Derivation:**
The same non-inverting feedback equation applies:

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

**Numerical Design (15 V output):**

$$\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = \frac{15}{7.15} = 2.098$$

$$R_1 = 1.098 \cdot R_2$$

Choose $R_2 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard). Then:

$$R_1 = 1.098 \cdot 4.7\text{k} = 5.16 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 5.1 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard)}$$

**Verification:** $V_{OUT} = 7.15 \cdot (5.1\text{k} + 4.7\text{k})/4.7\text{k} = 7.15 \cdot 2.085 = 14.91 \text{ V}$ ✓ (acceptable; tweak $R_1$ to 5.2 k for exact 15 V).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $R_{SC}$ from $I_{SC}$:**

$$R_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{I_{SC}} = \frac{0.65}{0.2} = 3.25 \, \Omega$$

Use **3.3 Ω** (standard E12).

**Step 2 — Compute (R1 + R2)/R2 ratio from $I_{MAX}$ condition:**

Fold-back equation at full output:

$$I_{MAX} \cdot R_{SC} \cdot (R_1 + R_2) = V_{BE} \cdot (R_1 + R_2) + V_{OUT(NL)} \cdot R_2$$

Substituting $I_{MAX} = 1$, $R_{SC} = 3.25$, $V_{BE} = 0.65$, $V_{OUT(NL)} = 15$:

$$1 \cdot 3.25 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) = 0.65 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) + 15 \cdot R_2$$

$$(3.25 - 0.65) \cdot (R_1 + R_2) = 15 \cdot R_2$$

$$2.6 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) = 15 \cdot R_2$$

$$\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = \frac{15}{2.6} = 5.769$$

$$R_1 = 4.769 \cdot R_2$$

**Step 3 — Choose $R_2$ and find $R_1$:**
Pick $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ → $R_1 = 4.77 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard).

**Step 4 — Worst-Case Power Comparison:**

Assume $V_{IN} = 20 \text{ V}$, $V_{OUT} = 15 \text{ V}$ (drop = 5 V across external 2N3055).

| Scheme | Current at Short | Power at Short |
|---|---|---|
| Constant Current Limit | $I_{LIM} = 1 \text{ A}$ | $(20 - 0) \cdot 1 = 20 \text{ W}$ |
| Fold-Back Limit | $I_{SC} = 0.2 \text{ A}$ | $(20 - 0) \cdot 0.2 = 4 \text{ W}$ |

**Fold-back reduces the worst-case power by 5×** — a critical advantage in high-current supplies.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the two output voltage formulas:** The $V_{OUT}$ formula changes topology between low-voltage and high-voltage modes. In low-voltage, the *reference* is the input; in high-voltage, the *output* is the input. Write down the exact topology diagram before applying the formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the bootstrap resistor R3 in the non-follower high-voltage config:** This is a favourite KTU trap. If your high-voltage design uses a *grounded* load (non-follower), you *must* include R3 from $V_{IN}$ to Pin 9.
> 3. **Using 0.7 V instead of 0.65 V for $V_{BE}$:** While both are acceptable, KTU's reference solution uses **0.65 V** as the standard for the 723's internal transistor. Stick to this for consistency.
> 4. **Ignoring the compensation capacitor:** A 723 without the Pin 9 capacitor will oscillate at ~1 MHz. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for omitting it in the circuit diagram.
> 5. **Not specifying the power rating of $R_{SC}$:** A $3.3 \, \Omega$ resistor at 1 A dissipates 3.3 W — students routinely write the resistance but forget the wattage, losing a mark.
> 6. **Fold-back ratio confusion:** The ratio $I_{MAX}/I_{SC}$ is the *fold-back ratio*. A common mistake is to invert it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IC 723 is a 14-pin monolithic voltage regulator** with internal reference ≈ **7.15 V**, internal pass transistor rated 150 mA.
- **Low-voltage mode** ($V_{OUT} \leq 7.15 \text{ V}$): output is tapped from **Pin 13** directly.
- **High-voltage mode** ($V_{OUT} > 7.15 \text{ V}$): requires an **external NPN pass transistor** (e.g., 2N3055) at Pin 10 driven base.
- **Core output equation:** $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$ (with $V_{REF} = 7.15 \text{ V}$).
- **Short-circuit limit:** $R_{SC} = \dfrac{0.65 \text{ V}}{I_{LIMIT}}$.
- **Fold-back current** at $V_{OUT} = 0$ equals the constant-current case ($I_{SC} = V_{BE}/R_{SC}$).
- **Fold-back current** at full $V_{OUT}$ is $I_{MAX}$, designed to be 5–10× larger than $I_{SC}$.
- **Worst-case dissipation** = $V_{IN} \cdot I_{LIMIT(SC)}$ — fold-back reduces this dramatically.
- **Pin 9 compensation capacitor** (100 pF–0.1 µF) is mandatory to prevent oscillation.
- **Pin 7** in low-voltage mode is tied to **ground**; in high-voltage mode it can be tied to a *negative* supply for improved PSRR.
- **Always specify resistor power ratings** — at high currents, $R_{SC}$ can dissipate several watts.
- **External pass transistor** needs a *heatsink* sized for $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{OUT}$ watts.
- **KTU's most-asked question pattern:** "Design a ___V, ___A supply with fold-back protection. Calculate R1, R2, Rsc and the worst-case power dissipation." Memorize the 5-step design procedure.
- **Two sub-modes of high-voltage:** *Follower* (load floating) vs *Non-follower* (load grounded, requires R3).
- **Fold-back equation derivation** hinges on the *bias-cancellation* property: as $V_{OUT}$ drops, the divider biases Q2 *less*, so Q2 needs a *larger* $V_{SENSE}$ to trip — but since $V_{SENSE}$ comes from $I_{OUT} \cdot R_{SC}$, a *smaller* $I_{OUT}$ suffices to trip. The end result: $I_{LIMIT}$ *decreases* with $V_{OUT}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A/D Converters - Counter Ramp and Flash Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** A/D Converters - Counter Ramp and Flash Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** An electronic integrated circuit that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog input signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital output code (usually binary), preserving the signal information within a defined resolution determined by the number of output bits **n**.

In the context of **PCECL407 — Linear Integrated Circuits Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, this experiment focuses on the **hardware realization and functional verification** of two architecturally contrasting ADC topologies implemented using standard op-amps, comparators, and digital logic ICs:

| # | ADC Type | Architectural Class | Defining Trait |
|---|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Counter Ramp ADC** | Counting / Integrating Class | Sequential search using a binary up-counter feeding a DAC |
| 2 | **Flash ADC** | Parallel / Simultaneous Class | All bits resolved at once using a resistor ladder and 2ⁿ−1 comparators |

Both belong to the broader family of **Nyquist-rate converters**, meaning they sample without integrating the input over time (as opposed to dual-slope or sigma-delta types).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Measuring Cup vs. The Laser Scale"**
> 
> - **Counter Ramp ADC** is like a **measuring cup filled drop by drop** from zero. You keep adding one drop at a time and comparing with the unknown liquid. The instant the cup level crosses the unknown level, you read off the count. It's **accurate but painfully slow**.
> 
> - **Flash ADC** is like a **laser height map** that captures the entire unknown height simultaneously using thousands of parallel laser beams. It is **blazingly fast** but needs lots of hardware — every possible height threshold needs its own sensor.

The fundamental engineering trade-off is therefore:
> **Speed ⇄ Hardware Complexity ⇄ Cost**

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Resolution:** Number of discrete output levels = $2^n$, where **n** = number of output bits.
- **Quantization Step (LSB size):** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$
- **Full-Scale Voltage ($V_{FS}$):** Reference voltage supplied to the internal DAC/ladder.
- **Conversion Time ($T_c$):** Time elapsed between start of conversion and availability of valid output.
- **Sampling Rate ($f_s$):** Maximum number of conversions per second.
- **Standard supply rails used in lab:** **±15 V** for op-amps, **+5 V** for TTL/CMOS logic.
- **Typical comparator IC:** **LM311**; **DAC IC:** **DAC0808**; **Counter IC:** **74LS193**; **Encoder IC:** **74LS148**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 lab exam, the viva-panels explicitly test the difference in *conversion time formula* and *hardware count* between Counter Ramp and Flash ADCs. Memorize:
> - Counter Ramp: $T_c = (2^n - 1) \cdot T_{CLK}$
> - Flash: $T_c \approx 1 \cdot T_{prop}$ (single comparator propagation delay)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Counter Ramp ADC — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Block Architecture
The Counter Ramp ADC (also called **Digital Ramp ADC** or **Shaft Encoder ADC** in older texts) consists of four fundamental blocks:

1. **Binary Up-Counter** (e.g., **74LS193**) — Generates a sequence of digital codes from 000…0 to 111…1.
2. **DAC** (e.g., **DAC0808**) — Converts the digital code to an analog ramp voltage.
3. **Comparator** (e.g., **LM311**) — Continuously compares the DAC output with the unknown analog input $V_{in}$.
4. **Control Logic (AND gate + Flip-Flop)** — Latches the counter value and resets the conversion cycle.

### 2.1.2 Working — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** A **START pulse** resets the counter to **000…0** and sets the output of the control flip-flop, enabling the AND gate so clock pulses can reach the counter.
- **Step 2:** With every clock pulse, the counter increments by 1 LSB. The DAC output $V_{DAC}$ therefore rises in discrete steps of $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^n$.
- **Step 3:** The comparator monitors the condition:
$$V_{DAC} < V_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Output} = \text{LOW} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Clock passes} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Counter advances}$$
- **Step 4:** The instant $V_{DAC} \geq V_{in}$, the comparator output switches **HIGH**, blocking the AND gate via the flip-flop.
- **Step 5:** The counter is now **frozen**, and its content is the digital equivalent of $V_{in}$. An **End-of-Conversion (EOC)** flag is raised.

### 2.1.3 Why the name "Counter Ramp"?
The DAC output is a **monotonically rising staircase**, almost a linear ramp when clock frequency is much higher than the input's rate of change. Hence the term **Counter Ramp**.

### 2.1.4 Critical Limitations
- **Conversion time is non-uniform** — depends on input magnitude. Worst case (input near $V_{FS}$) gives maximum time.
- **Slow** — unsuitable for high-frequency or real-time signals (audio, video).
- **Vulnerable to noise** during the long comparison window.

---

## 2.2 Flash ADC — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Block Architecture
A Flash ADC (also called **Parallel ADC** or **Simultaneous ADC**) is the **fastest** ADC topology in existence. It consists of:

1. **Precision Resistor Ladder** — A string of $2^n$ equal resistors between $V_{ref+}$ and $V_{ref-}$, creating $2^n - 1$ equally spaced reference voltages.
2. **Bank of Comparators** — $2^n - 1$ comparators, each comparing $V_{in}$ against one tap on the ladder.
3. **Priority Encoder** — Converts the "thermometer code" output of the comparator bank into a standard binary (or Gray) code.

### 2.2.2 Working — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** The resistor ladder divides $V_{ref}$ into discrete threshold voltages:
$$V_{k} = \frac{k \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^n - 1$$
- **Step 2:** Each comparator $C_k$ has its inverting input at $V_k$ and non-inverting input at $V_{in}$.
  - If $V_{in} > V_k$, then $C_k$ output = **1**.
  - If $V_{in} < V_k$, then $C_k$ output = **0**.
- **Step 3:** The combined output of all comparators forms a **thermometer code** (e.g., `000…0111…1`). All "1"s are bunched at the bottom; the position of the first "0" marks the digital value.
- **Step 4:** The **priority encoder** (typically **74LS148** for 8-to-3 line) decodes this thermometer code into an **n-bit binary** output.
- **Step 5:** **EOC** is essentially instantaneous — the output is valid after a single comparator propagation delay.

### 2.2.3 Why "Flash"?
Because conversion happens in **one clock cycle** (or even asynchronously) — all bits are decided **in a flash**.

### 2.2.4 Critical Limitations
- **Hardware explodes exponentially with resolution**: For **n** bits, you need $2^n - 1$ comparators. A 10-bit Flash ADC needs **1023 comparators**; a 12-bit needs **4095**.
- **Resistor matching** becomes the bottleneck at high resolutions.
- **High power consumption** and **large die area**.
- **Typical practical limit: 8 bits** in standard lab kits (e.g., the **ADC0808** family uses a different architecture internally, but the classic lab experiment uses an **8-bit Flash** demonstrator module).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the #1 viva + numerical question source for this experiment.**

| # | Parameter | Counter Ramp ADC | Flash ADC |
|---|-----------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Number of comparators | **1** | $2^n - 1$ |
| 2 | Number of resistors in ladder | 0 (DAC dependent) | $2^n$ |
| 3 | Resolution levels | $2^n$ | $2^n$ |
| 4 | Step size (LSB) $\Delta V$ | $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ |
| 5 | Conversion time $T_c$ (worst case) | $(2^n - 1) \cdot T_{CLK}$ | $1 \cdot T_{prop}$ (≈ ns) |
| 6 | Conversion time $T_c$ (average) | $\dfrac{2^n - 1}{2} \cdot T_{CLK}$ | $T_{prop}$ |
| 7 | Maximum sampling rate $f_{s,max}$ | $\dfrac{f_{CLK}}{2^n - 1}$ | $\approx \dfrac{1}{T_{prop}}$ |
| 8 | Hardware cost | Low | Very high (exponential) |
| 9 | Power consumption | Low | High |
| 10 | Typical IC / Demonstration | **DAC0808 + LM311 + 74LS193** | **74LS85 + LM339 + 74LS148** |
| 11 | Speed class | **Slow** | **Fastest possible** |
| 12 | Suitability | DC / low-frequency instrumentation | High-speed digital oscilloscopes, video, radar |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Counter Ramp ADC**: Found in **digital voltmeters (DVMs)**, **digital panel meters**, **slow sensor readouts** (temperature, weight), and **lab instrumentation** where cost and resolution matter more than speed.
- **Flash ADC**: Used in **high-definition video digitizers**, **digital storage oscilloscopes (DSOs)**, **radar signal processing**, **satellite communication receivers**, and **wideband software-defined radios (SDR)**.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Architecture — Why These Two Are Taught Together

> [!NOTE]
> KTU chose to pair these two ADC types in a single experiment because they represent the **two extreme ends of the speed-vs-complexity design space**. Studying them together builds engineering intuition for **selection criteria** — when to use which type in a real product.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Conversion Time of Counter Ramp ADC

**Given:**
- Clock frequency: $f_{CLK}$ (Hz)
- Number of bits: $n$
- Input voltage: $V_{in}$
- Full-scale reference: $V_{FS}$

**Find:** Conversion time $T_c$ as a function of $V_{in}$ and the maximum $T_c$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Digital equivalent of $V_{in}$**

The counter starts at 0 and increments by 1 for every clock pulse. The DAC converts the counter value $D$ into:
$$V_{DAC} = D \cdot \Delta V = D \cdot \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2 — Stopping condition**

Conversion terminates when $V_{DAC}$ first equals or exceeds $V_{in}$. Therefore:
$$D \cdot \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \geq V_{in}$$
$$D \geq V_{in} \cdot \frac{2^n}{V_{FS}}$$

**Step 3 — Number of clock pulses required**

Let $D$ be the smallest integer satisfying the inequality. The number of clock pulses elapsed is:
$$N = D = \left\lceil V_{in} \cdot \frac{2^n}{V_{FS}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4 — Conversion time**

Since one clock pulse takes $T_{CLK} = 1/f_{CLK}$:
$$T_c = \frac{N}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} \cdot \left\lceil V_{in} \cdot \frac{2^n}{V_{FS}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 5 — Worst case**

When $V_{in} \approx V_{FS}$ (just below full scale), the counter must traverse all $2^n - 1$ states:
$$T_{c,\text{max}} = \frac{2^n - 1}{f_{CLK}}$$

**Step 6 — Average case**

Assuming a uniformly distributed $V_{in}$ over $[0, V_{FS}]$:
$$T_{c,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{V_{FS}} \int_{0}^{V_{FS}} \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} \cdot \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{FS}} \, dV_{in} = \frac{2^n - 1}{2 \cdot f_{CLK}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Step Size and Quantization Error

**Step 1 — Define Full-Scale**

For an **n-bit** unipolar ADC spanning $[0, V_{FS}]$:
$$V_{FS} = 2^n \cdot \Delta V$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\Delta V$**
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

**Step 3 — Quantization error**

The maximum quantization error (before rounding) is:
$$E_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Suppose $n = 8$, $V_{FS} = 5.12$ V. Then:
$$\Delta V = \frac{5.12}{256} = 0.02 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV}$$
$$E_q = \pm 10 \text{ mV}$$

This means every reading has an inherent uncertainty of ±10 mV regardless of how good the analog front-end is.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Flash ADC Threshold Voltages

**Step 1 — Resistor ladder**

A string of $2^n$ equal resistors $R$ is connected between $V_{ref+}$ and $V_{ref-}$. The current through the string is:
$$I = \frac{V_{ref+} - V_{ref-}}{2^n \cdot R}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage at the $k$-th node (counting from $V_{ref-}$)**
$$V_k = V_{ref-} + k \cdot I \cdot R = V_{ref-} + k \cdot \frac{V_{ref+} - V_{ref-}}{2^n}$$

**Step 3 — Standard symmetric bipolar case**

With $V_{ref+} = +V_{ref}/2$ and $V_{ref-} = -V_{ref}/2$:
$$V_k = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2} + k \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

For $k = 0$: $V_0 = -V_{ref}/2$  
For $k = 2^n - 1$: $V_{2^n-1} = +V_{ref}/2 - V_{ref}/2^n$ (one LSB below $+V_{ref}/2$)

**Step 4 — Decision logic**

Comparator $C_k$ outputs logic **1** iff:
$$V_{in} > V_k$$

The thermometer code's transition point (last "1") gives the binary output $D$:
$$D = k_{\text{max}} \text{ such that } V_{in} > V_k$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem (KTU University Exam, May 2024 Pattern):**
> An 8-bit Counter Ramp ADC uses a clock of frequency **500 kHz** and a full-scale voltage of **10.24 V**. Calculate:
> (a) Step size
> (b) Digital output for $V_{in} = 3.72$ V
> (c) Worst-case conversion time
> (d) Maximum input frequency that can be tracked accurately

### Solution

**Part (a) — Step Size**

$$n = 8 \implies 2^n = 256$$
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10.24}{256} = 0.04 \text{ V} = 40 \text{ mV}$$

**Part (b) — Digital Output for $V_{in} = 3.72$ V**

$$D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\left(\frac{3.72}{0.04}\right) = \text{round}(93) = 93_{10}$$

Converting to binary:
$$93_{10} = 64 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 01011101_2$$

**Part (c) — Worst-Case Conversion Time**

$$T_{c,\text{max}} = \frac{2^n - 1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{256 - 1}{500 \times 10^3} = \frac{255}{500{,}000}$$
$$T_{c,\text{max}} = 5.10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 510 \text{ μs}$$

**Part (d) — Maximum Trackable Input Frequency**

By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem:
$$f_{in,\text{max}} = \frac{f_{s,\text{max}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot T_{c,\text{max}}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 510 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$f_{in,\text{max}} \approx 980.4 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** For an **n-bit** Counter Ramp ADC, $f_{in,\text{max}} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{2 \cdot (2^n - 1)}$. This formula is frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions.

---

## 3.5 Flash ADC Hardware Count Example

> **Problem:** Design a 6-bit Flash ADC. How many comparators and resistors are needed? If each comparator consumes 5 mW, what is the total power?

### Solution

$$N_{\text{comparators}} = 2^n - 1 = 2^6 - 1 = 63$$
$$N_{\text{resistors}} = 2^n = 64$$
$$P_{\text{total}} = 63 \times 5 \text{ mW} = 315 \text{ mW}$$

For comparison, scaling to 10 bits:
$$N_{\text{comparators}} = 1023, \quad P_{\text{total}} = 5.115 \text{ W}$$

This exponential explosion is **why** Flash ADCs in practice rarely exceed 8 bits as monolithic ICs. Higher resolutions use **sub-ranging** or **interpolation** architectures.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Counter Ramp ADC — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK[Clock Source fCLK] --> AND1[AND Gate]
    START[Start Pulse] --> FF[S-R Flip Flop]
    FF --> AND1
    AND1 --> CTR[Binary Up Counter 74LS193]
    CTR -->|8 bit binary| DAC[DAC0808 R 2R Ladder]
    DAC -->|Analog Ramp| CMP[Comparator LM311]
    VIN[Analog Input VIN] --> CMP
    CMP -->|Stop Signal| FF
    CTR --> OUT[Digital Output D0 to D7]
    CMP --> EOC[End of Conversion EOC Flag]

    classDef io fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000

    class CLK,START,VIN,OUT,EOC io
    class AND1,CTR,DAC,CMP logic
    class FF ctrl
```

### Operation Flow of the Above Block Diagram

| Stage | Event | Counter State | DAC Output | Comparator Output |
|-------|-------|---------------|------------|-------------------|
| 0 | START pulse applied | Reset to 00000000 | 0 V | HIGH (since $V_{in} > 0$) |
| 1 | Clock pulses pass through AND | Increments to 00000001 | 0.04 V | HIGH |
| 2 | Counter continues counting | 00000010 to 01011101 | Rises in 40 mV steps | HIGH |
| 3 | Counter = 93, $V_{DAC} = 3.72$ V | 01011101 | ≈ $V_{in}$ | LOW (flip-flop set) |
| 4 | AND gate blocked, clock disabled | Frozen at 93 | Constant | — |
| 5 | EOC flag raised, output latched | Valid digital code available | — | — |

---

## 4.2 Flash ADC — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VREF[VREF Reference Voltage] --> LADDER[Resistor Ladder 2 to the n equal R]
    VRN[VRN Reference Ground] --> LADDER
    LADDER -->|T0 T1 T2 ... T2n minus 1| COMPS[Bank of 2 to the n minus 1 Comparators LM339]
    VIN2[Analog Input VIN] --> COMPS
    COMPS -->|Thermometer Code| ENC[Priority Encoder 74LS148]
    ENC -->|n bit Binary| DOUT[Digital Output D0 to D n minus 1]
    COMPS --> EOC2[End of Conversion within 1 clock cycle]

    classDef io2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef ana fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef dig fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000

    class VREF,VRN,VIN2,DOUT,EOC2 io2
    class LADDER,COMPS ana
    class ENC dig
```

### 8-bit Flash ADC — Comparator Output Thermometer Code Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Analog Input VIN]
        direction LR
        VIN[VIN]:::io
    end

    subgraph COMPS[Comparator Bank LM339 x 255 units]
        direction LR
        C1[C1 Vth 1 LSB]:::cmp
        C2[C2 Vth 2 LSB]:::cmp
        C3[C3 Vth 3 LSB]:::cmp
        CN[C N Vth 255 LSB]:::cmp
    end

    subgraph THERM[Thermometer Code]
        direction LR
        T1[0]:::tc
        T2[0]:::tc
        T3[1]:::tc
        TN[1]:::tc
    end

    subgraph ENC[Priority Encoder 74LS148]
        direction LR
        PE[Encoder Logic]:::enc
    end

    subgraph OUT[Binary Output]
        direction LR
        OB[8 bit Code]:::out
    end

    INPUT --> COMPS
    COMPS --> THERM
    THERM --> ENC
    ENC --> OUT

    classDef io fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef cmp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef tc fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef enc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CR[Counter Ramp ADC]
        direction TB
        CR1[Single Comparator]:::cr
        CR2[DAC generates ramp]:::cr
        CR3[Sequential bit decision]:::cr
        CR4[Time T equals 2 to the n minus 1 over fCLK]:::cr
    end

    subgraph FL[Flash ADC]
        direction TB
        FL1[2 to the n minus 1 Comparators in parallel]:::fl
        FL2[Resistor ladder sets thresholds]:::fl

    end

    CR --- |VS| FL

    classDef cr fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef fl fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Conversion-Time-vs-Resolution Trade-Off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Resolution n bits low] -->|increase n| B[Counter Ramp TC grows exponentially]
    C[Resolution n bits low] -->|increase n| D[Flash TC stays constant Tprop]
    B --> E[Slow for high resolution]
    D --> F[Fast for any resolution]
    E --> G[Choose Counter Ramp for low cost DC apps]
    F --> H[Choose Flash for high speed wideband apps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** This trade-off curve is the **central design philosophy** behind the entire KTU Module-2 experiment. If the examiner asks "Why not always use Flash?", the answer is hidden in the **hardware-count explosion** depicted above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Compare Counter Ramp and Flash ADCs in terms of speed and hardware complexity. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Counter Ramp | Flash |
|-----------|--------------|-------|
| Speed | **Slow** — $T_c = (2^n - 1) \cdot T_{CLK}$ | **Fastest** — $T_c \approx T_{prop}$ |
| Hardware | 1 comparator + DAC + counter | $2^n - 1$ comparators + ladder + encoder |
| Cost | Low | Very high for high resolution |
| Typical use | DC voltmeters, panel meters | Oscilloscopes, video, radar |

Counter Ramp is **simple but sequential**, while Flash is **parallel and instantaneous** but requires exponentially more components as resolution increases. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. Why is the Flash ADC called a "parallel" ADC? What is the major limitation? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A Flash ADC is called **"parallel"** because all $2^n - 1$ comparators operate **simultaneously** on the same analog input, each comparing $V_{in}$ against a different reference voltage from the resistor ladder. All bits of the output are therefore resolved in **one clock cycle** (or a single comparator propagation delay).

**Major Limitation:** The number of comparators grows as $2^n - 1$, so for an **n-bit** ADC, the hardware, power, and die area **explode exponentially** with resolution. This practically limits monolithic Flash ADCs to about **8 bits**. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks, Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **n-bit Counter Ramp ADC**. Derive the expression for its **worst-case conversion time**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **6-bit Counter Ramp ADC** has a clock frequency of **1 MHz** and $V_{FS} = 5.12$ V. Find (i) the step size, (ii) the digital output for $V_{in} = 2.50$ V, and (iii) the worst-case conversion time in microseconds. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The Counter Ramp ADC consists of:
1. A **clock generator** providing pulses at frequency $f_{CLK}$.
2. A **binary up-counter** (e.g., 74LS193).
3. A **DAC** (e.g., DAC0808) that converts the counter's digital output to an analog voltage.
4. A **comparator** (e.g., LM311) that compares the DAC output $V_{DAC}$ with the analog input $V_{in}$.
5. A **control flip-flop and AND gate** that gate the clock to the counter based on the comparator output.

**Working (3 marks):**

- A **START pulse** resets the counter to zero and sets the flip-flop, enabling the AND gate so clock pulses flow to the counter.
- With each clock pulse, the counter increments by 1, and the DAC output rises by one LSB ($\Delta V$).
- The comparator continuously checks whether $V_{DAC} \geq V_{in}$.
- As long as $V_{DAC} < V_{in}$, the comparator output is LOW, the AND gate passes clock pulses, and the counter keeps incrementing.
- The instant $V_{DAC} \geq V_{in}$, the comparator output goes HIGH, which resets the flip-flop and blocks the AND gate. The counter freezes.
- The frozen counter value is the digital equivalent of $V_{in}$. An **End-of-Conversion (EOC)** signal is generated.

**Worst-Case Conversion Time Derivation (1 mark):**

At $V_{in} \approx V_{FS}$, the counter must increment through all $2^n - 1$ states:
$$T_{c,\text{max}} = (2^n - 1) \cdot T_{CLK} = \frac{2^n - 1}{f_{CLK}}$$

**Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $n = 6$, $f_{CLK} = 10^6$ Hz, $V_{FS} = 5.12$ V, $V_{in} = 2.50$ V

**(i) Step Size (2 marks):**
$$2^n = 2^6 = 64$$
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{5.12}{64} = 0.08 \text{ V} = 80 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Digital Output for $V_{in} = 2.50$ V (3 marks):**
$$D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\left(\frac{2.50}{0.08}\right) = \text{round}(31.25) = 31_{10}$$
$$31_{10} = 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 011111_2$$

**(iii) Worst-Case Conversion Time (2 marks):**
$$T_{c,\text{max}} = \frac{2^n - 1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{64 - 1}{10^6} = \frac{63}{10^6} = 63 \text{ μs}$$

**Total: 7 marks**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of an **n-bit Flash ADC**. Derive the threshold voltages of the resistor ladder. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An **8-bit Flash ADC** uses a reference voltage of $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V. Calculate (i) the number of comparators required, (ii) the number of resistors in the ladder, (iii) the step size, and (iv) the threshold voltage of the 100th comparator (counting from the bottom). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

A Flash ADC consists of:
1. A **precision resistor ladder** with $2^n$ equal resistors between $V_{ref+}$ and $V_{ref-}$.
2. A **bank of $2^n - 1$ comparators**, each having its reference input at one tap of the ladder and the signal input connected to $V_{in}$.
3. A **priority encoder** (e.g., 74LS148) that converts the comparator outputs (thermometer code) to a standard binary code.

**Working (2 marks):**

- The resistor ladder creates $2^n - 1$ equally spaced reference voltages.
- All comparators operate in **parallel** on the same $V_{in}$.
- If $V_{in}$ exceeds the $k$-th threshold, comparator $C_k$ outputs 1; otherwise 0.
- The combined outputs form a **thermometer code** (a string of 0s followed by 1s).
- The priority encoder converts this thermometer code into a binary output.

**Threshold Voltage Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $R$ be the resistance of each of the $2^n$ resistors. The current through the ladder is:
$$I = \frac{V_{ref+} - V_{ref-}}{2^n \cdot R}$$

The voltage at the $k$-th tap (counting from $V_{ref-}$) is:
$$V_k = V_{ref-} + k \cdot I \cdot R = V_{ref-} + \frac{k \cdot (V_{ref+} - V_{ref-})}{2^n}$$

For the symmetric case $V_{ref+} = +V_{ref}/2$ and $V_{ref-} = -V_{ref}/2$:
$$V_k = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2} + k \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $n = 8$, $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V (full range from $-V_{ref}/2$ to $+V_{ref}/2$, i.e., $-2.56$ V to $+2.56$ V)

**(i) Number of Comparators (2 marks):**
$$N_{\text{comp}} = 2^n - 1 = 2^8 - 1 = 255$$

**(ii) Number of Resistors in the Ladder (1 mark):**
$$N_R = 2^n = 256$$

**(iii) Step Size (2 marks):**
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.12}{256} = 0.02 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV}$$

**(iv) Threshold Voltage of the 100th Comparator (2 marks):**
$$V_{100} = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2} + 100 \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = -2.56 + 100 \times 0.02 = -2.56 + 2.00 = -0.56 \text{ V}$$

**Total: 7 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the two ADC names in the diagram**: Students often draw the Flash ADC's resistor ladder while explaining the Counter Ramp (or vice versa). Examiner deducts 2–3 marks immediately.
> 
> 2. **Wrong formula for conversion time**: Writing $T_c = 2^n / f_{CLK}$ instead of $(2^n - 1)/f_{CLK}$ costs 1 mark. The "−1" comes from the fact that the counter starts at 0 and stops at $2^n - 1$, traversing exactly $2^n - 1$ states.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the priority encoder** in the Flash ADC: Without it, the output is a thermometer code, not a usable binary code. Examiners expect it explicitly mentioned.
> 
> 4. **Numerical mistakes in binary conversion**: When $D = 93$, the binary should be `01011101`. Reversing bits or sign errors lead to 0 marks for the conversion step.
> 
> 5. **Not specifying the worst-case condition** in Counter Ramp: $T_{c,\text{max}}$ is achieved when $V_{in} \approx V_{FS}$, not at mid-scale. Examiners look for the explicit "worst-case" qualifier.
> 
> 6. **Saying Flash ADC is "always better"**: This is a classic trap. Flash is fastest, but Counter Ramp is preferred for low-cost, low-power, high-resolution DC applications. Examiners award full marks only for **nuanced comparison**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**
> 
> - **Counter Ramp ADC** = Sequential, uses **1 comparator + DAC + up-counter**, slow but cheap.
> - **Flash ADC** = Parallel, uses **$2^n - 1$ comparators + resistor ladder + priority encoder**, fastest but expensive.
> - **Step size (LSB)** for both: $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^n$.
> - **Worst-case conversion time of Counter Ramp**: $T_{c,\text{max}} = (2^n - 1)/f_{CLK}$.
> - **Flash ADC conversion time**: ≈ 1 comparator propagation delay (nanoseconds).
> - **Resistor ladder formula**: $V_k = V_{ref-} + k \cdot (V_{ref+} - V_{ref-})/2^n$.
> - **Thermometer code → Binary code** conversion is done by the priority encoder.
> - **Lab ICs to remember**: DAC0808, LM311, LM339, 74LS193, 74LS148.
> - **Nyquist limit**: Maximum input frequency = $f_{s,\text{max}} / 2$.
> - **Quantization error**: $\pm \Delta V / 2 = \pm V_{FS}/2^{n+1}$.
> - **Practical resolution limit of Flash ADC**: ~8 bits as a monolithic IC.
> - **Counter Ramp** is ideal for **DC voltmeters, panel meters, slow sensors**.
> - **Flash** is ideal for **digital oscilloscopes, video, radar, SDR, wideband comms**.
> - **Trade-off axis**: **Speed ⇄ Hardware Complexity ⇄ Cost ⇄ Power**.
> - **Block diagrams** must show all functional blocks with arrow directions clearly marked.
> - **Always mention START, EOC, clock gating, and priority encoding** in long answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: D/A Converters - R-2R Ladder Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** D/A Converters - R-2R Ladder Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **R-2R Ladder Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a current-summing type data converter that utilizes a repetitive network of two resistor values (**R** and **2R**) to convert an n-bit binary digital input word into a proportional analog output voltage (or current). It is the most widely manufactured DAC topology in monolithic integrated circuits because it requires only two precisely matched resistor values, making it highly compatible with CMOS, bipolar, and thin-film fabrication processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECL407 - Module 2):** The R-2R Ladder DAC is a mandatory experiment. Students must demonstrate the conversion of binary input codes (applied via switches/logic levels) into a corresponding analog output voltage using an op-amp summing stage, and verify the linearity of the transfer characteristic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Flow Distributor"

Imagine a **water distribution network** at a municipal plant. The supply enters a main pipe (the reference voltage $V_{ref}$). At every junction, a **T-junction** splits the flow into two paths:
- One path has **half the resistance** of the other (the **2R branch**).
- The other path has the **full resistance** (the **R branch**).

Because of identical repeating junctions, at every node exactly **half the current** chooses the new branch (going right, toward the "house" / output) and the other **half is shunted** back toward ground. This is precisely how the R-2R ladder works: each bit position contributes a current that is **half of the previous bit's contribution**, giving a natural binary weighting (MSB, MSB/2, MSB/4, ..., LSB) without needing binary-weighted resistor values.

## 1.3 Key Physical/Design Metrics

- **R-value range (typical):** **$2.5\ k\Omega$ to $10\ k\Omega$**
- **Resolution:** Determined by the number of bits **n** (e.g., 4-bit, 8-bit, 12-bit)
- **Full-Scale Output Voltage ($V_{FS}$):** Approximately $V_{ref} \times \frac{(2^n - 1)}{2^n}$
- **Step Size (LSB):** $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$
- **Standard ICs:** **DAC0808** (8-bit), **DAC0832** (8-bit), **AD7524** (10-bit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Binary Weighting in R-2R:**
> Unlike the binary-weighted resistor DAC (which uses $R, 2R, 4R, 8R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$), the R-2R ladder achieves the same binary weighting purely through **current division at each ladder node**, with every resistor having only one of two values: **R or 2R**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Architecture (n-bit R-2R Ladder)

The R-2R ladder network consists of **n parallel input branches**, each with a series resistance of **2R** connected between a digital switch (tied to $V_{ref}$ for logic '1' or ground for logic '0') and the ladder node. The horizontal (ladder) segments are of value **R**, and the terminating end is grounded through a **2R** resistor.

### Structured Operation Logic (Why and How)

1. **Ladder Termination:** The rightmost end is terminated to ground through **2R**. This guarantees that looking *into* any ladder node from the right, the equivalent resistance is always **R** (a foundational property of the R-2R network).
2. **Node Analysis:** At each horizontal node, two paths of resistance **R** meet (one from the left ladder rung, one from the right). Therefore, the current from any **2R** input branch splits **equally** — half going left, half going right.
3. **Binary Current Summation:** The MSB branch injects current $\frac{V_{ref}}{3R}$ into the ladder. At each successive node, the contribution is halved. By the time the current reaches the op-amp summing junction (virtual ground), the contributions form a precise binary sequence: $I_{MSB}, I_{MSB}/2, I_{MSB}/4, \ldots, I_{LSB}$.
4. **Op-Amp I-to-V Conversion:** The summing op-amp (configured as an inverting adder) sums all bit-currents through a feedback resistor $R_f$, producing an analog output voltage $V_o = -I_{total} \cdot R_f$.

## 2.2 Thevenin Equivalent Insight

The single most important property of the R-2R ladder is its **constant input impedance** of **2R** at every node, regardless of switch position. This is because:
- Looking right from any node through the rest of the ladder, the equivalent resistance is **R**.
- The 2R input branch is in series with a switch, but the **parallel combination of the two R paths** at every node yields an effective **2R** seen by the source.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Input current per branch | $I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{3R}$ | Current injected by **each** bit branch (when switch is at $V_{ref}$) | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | LSB step size (voltage) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{3R} \cdot 2^n = \dfrac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{3R}$ (with $R_f = 3R$) → $\Delta V = V_{ref}$ | Voltage change for one LSB increment | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Generalized output voltage | $V_o = -\dfrac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{3R} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{b_k}{2^{n-1-k}}$ | Sum of weighted bit contributions | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Full-Scale output (all 1s) | $V_{FS} = -\dfrac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{3R} \cdot \dfrac{(2^n - 1)}{2^n}$ | Approaching $-V_{ref}$ as $n \to \infty$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Resolution (% FS) | $\% \text{ Res.} = \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$ | Percentage of full scale per LSB | Percent (%) |
| 6 | Input impedance (any node) | $Z_{in} = 2R$ | Constant, independent of bit pattern | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 7 | Feedback resistor (for unity LSB step) | $R_f = 3R$ | Common choice for clean transfer function | Ohms ($\Omega$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $3R$ in the denominator?** Each 2R branch drives a node whose Thevenin equivalent is R (looking right). The total resistance seen by $V_{ref}$ is therefore $2R + R = 3R$, giving the input current $\frac{V_{ref}}{3R}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Equipment:** CD players, digital audio workstations, and music synthesizers (e.g., **DAC0808** in vintage synths).
- **Microcontroller Interfaces:** Generating programmable analog control voltages from digital PWM/code.
- **Function Generators:** DDS (Direct Digital Synthesis) chips use high-resolution R-2R ladders (e.g., **AD9850** at 10-bit).
- **Motor Control & Industrial Automation:** Providing reference voltages to VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives).
- **CRT/LCD Display Drivers:** Legacy video signal reconstruction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of a 4-bit R-2R DAC

Consider a **4-bit R-2R ladder DAC** with reference voltage $V_{ref}$, op-amp feedback resistor $R_f = 3R$, and binary inputs $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ where $b_3$ is the **MSB** and $b_0$ is the **LSB**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Current from Each Bit Branch

Each bit branch, when its switch is closed to $V_{ref}$ (logic '1'), injects a current:
$$I_{branch} = \frac{V_{ref}}{3R}$$

### Step 2 — Account for the Ladder Attenuation

Because of the binary current division at each ladder node, the current reaching the summing junction (op-amp inverting input) from the **k-th bit** (counting from LSB at $k=0$) is:
$$I_k = \frac{V_{ref}}{3R} \cdot \frac{b_k}{2^{n-1-k}}$$

For a 4-bit DAC, the weightings are:

| Bit Position | Weight | Reason |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $b_3$ (MSB) | $1$ | No attenuation |
| $b_2$ | $1/2$ | Halved at one node |
| $b_1$ | $1/4$ | Halved at two nodes |
| $b_0$ (LSB) | $1/8$ | Halved at three nodes |

### Step 3 — Sum the Currents at the Virtual Ground Node

The op-amp's inverting input is a **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0$ V). Applying KCL at this node, all bit-currents flow through $R_f$:
$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{3R} \left( \frac{b_3}{1} + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= \frac{V_{ref}}{3R} \sum_{k=0}^{3} \frac{b_k}{2^{3-k}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Current to Output Voltage via the Op-Amp

The op-amp operates as an **inverting current-to-voltage converter**, with output:
$$V_o = -I_{total} \cdot R_f$$

Substituting $R_f = 3R$:
$$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{3R} \cdot 3R \cdot \left( b_3 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_o &= -V_{ref} \left( b_3 \cdot 2^{-0} + b_2 \cdot 2^{-1} + b_1 \cdot 2^{-2} + b_0 \cdot 2^{-3} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Generalize to n-bit DAC

$$\begin{aligned}
V_o &= -V_{ref} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^{-(n-1-k)} \\
&= -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}
\end{aligned}$$

where $D = b_{n-1} \cdot 2^{n-1} + b_{n-2} \cdot 2^{n-2} + \cdots + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_0 \cdot 2^0$ is the **decimal equivalent** of the binary input word.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (4-bit, $V_{ref} = 8$ V)

Determine $V_o$ for binary input $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1101_2$ ($D = 13$):

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

Step (a) — Convert binary to decimal:
$$D = (1 \times 2^3) + (1 \times 2^2) + (0 \times 2^1) + (1 \times 2^0) = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13$$

Step (b) — Apply the output voltage formula:
$$V_o = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = -8 \cdot \frac{13}{2^4} = -8 \cdot \frac{13}{16}$$

Step (c) — Compute the numerical value:
$$V_o = -8 \times 0.8125 = -6.5 \text{ V}$$

**Verification by summation form:**
$$\begin{aligned}
V_o &= -8 \left( 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 0 \cdot \frac{1}{4} + 1 \cdot \frac{1}{8} \right) \\
&= -8 \left( 1 + 0.5 + 0 + 0.125 \right) \\
&= -8 \times 1.625 = -6.5 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 4-bit R-2R DAC Transfer Table ($V_{ref} = 8$ V)

| Decimal Input (D) | Binary $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ | Output Voltage $V_o$ (V) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0000 | 0.000 |
| 1 | 0001 | −0.500 |
| 2 | 0010 | −1.000 |
| 3 | 0011 | −1.500 |
| 4 | 0100 | −2.000 |
| 5 | 0101 | −2.500 |
| 6 | 0110 | −3.000 |
| 7 | 0111 | −3.500 |
| 8 | 1000 | −4.000 |
| 9 | 1001 | −4.500 |
| 10 | 1010 | −5.000 |
| 11 | 1011 | −5.500 |
| 12 | 1100 | −6.000 |
| 13 | 1101 | **−6.500** |
| 14 | 1110 | −7.000 |
| 15 | 1111 | −7.500 |

> [!NOTE]
> **LSB Step Size:** $\Delta V = 0.5$ V (i.e., $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{8}{16} = 0.5$ V). The output is perfectly linear with the digital input.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (4-bit R-2R Ladder with Op-Amp Summing Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef source fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef node fill:#f4b183,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef opamp fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef term fill:#bfbfbf,stroke:#595959,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px

    Vref[VREF REFERENCE VOLTAGE]:::source

    subgraph BIT3[BIT 3 MSB]
        SW3[DIGITAL SWITCH B3]:::node
        R3A[RESISTOR 2R]:::node
    end

    subgraph BIT2[BIT 2]
        SW2[DIGITAL SWITCH B2]:::node
        R2A[RESISTOR 2R]:::node
    end

    subgraph BIT1[BIT 1]
        SW1[DIGITAL SWITCH B1]:::node
        R1A[RESISTOR 2R]:::node
    end

    subgraph BIT0[BIT 0 LSB]
        SW0[DIGITAL SWITCH B0]:::node
        R0A[RESISTOR 2R]:::node
    end

    N3[LADDER NODE 3]:::node
    N2[LADDER NODE 2]:::node
    N1[LADDER NODE 1]:::node
    R_H3[HORIZONTAL R]:::node
    R_H2[HORIZONTAL R]:::node
    R_H1[HORIZONTAL R]:::node
    R_TERM[TERMINATION 2R TO GROUND]:::term

    Vref --> SW3
    SW3 --> R3A --> N3
    Vref --> SW2
    SW2 --> R2A --> N2
    Vref --> SW1
    SW1 --> R1A --> N1
    Vref --> SW0
    SW0 --> R0A --> R_TERM

    N3 --- R_H3 --- N2
    N2 --- R_H2 --- N1
    N1 --- R_H1 --- R_TERM

    N3 --> SUMJ[VIRTUAL GROUND SUMMING JUNCTION]:::opamp
    SUMJ --> OPAMP[OP AMP UA741 INVERTING INPUT]:::opamp
    OPAMP --> RF[FEEDBACK RESISTOR RF EQUALS 3R]:::opamp
    RF --> VOUT[ANALOG OUTPUT VOLTAGE VO]:::out
    OPAMP --> GND[NON INVERTING INPUT TO GROUND]:::opamp
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow and Functional Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef stage fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f3864,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef proc fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px

    S1[DIGITAL INPUT WORD BN-1 ... B0]:::stage
    S2[SWITCH MATRIX BINARY CONTROLLED]:::stage
    S3[R 2R LADDER NETWORK CURRENT DIVIDER]:::stage
    S4[OPERATIONAL AMPLIFIER I TO V CONVERTER]:::stage
    S5[ANALOG OUTPUT VOLTAGE VO]:::stage

    P1[Each switch routes VREF or GND to its 2R branch]:::proc
    P2[Ladder halves the current contribution at every node]:::proc
    P3[Op amp inverts and scales summed current by RF]:::proc
    P4[Produces linear staircase analog waveform]:::proc

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5

    S2 -.-> P1
    S3 -.-> P2
    S4 -.-> P3
    S5 -.-> P4
```

## 4.3 Key Signal Path Explanation

1. **Digital Input Stage** — Binary word $b_{n-1} \ldots b_0$ drives CMOS/bipolar switches (e.g., CD4053, 74LS151).
2. **Switch Matrix** — Each bit closes either to $V_{ref}$ ('1') or GND ('0'). For 0 V logic inputs, the 2R branch carries **no current** (the switch shorts to ground).
3. **R-2R Ladder** — The active branches inject currents that are successively halved at each node. The **constant 2R input impedance** ensures clean binary weighting.
4. **Op-Amp Summing Stage** — Virtual ground absorbs all bit-currents, and $R_f$ converts the summed current to a proportional analog voltage.
5. **Analog Output** — $V_o$ swings in a clean **staircase pattern** as the digital input is incremented.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**State the function of the R-2R ladder network in a Digital-to-Analog Converter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The R-2R ladder network in a DAC is used to convert a digital binary input word into a proportional analog output by means of a repetitive network of two resistor values (R and 2R). It generates binary-weighted currents at each node through successive current division. Each successive bit contributes a current that is half of the previous bit's contribution, producing the binary weighting $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, \ldots, 2^{n-1}$ without requiring large-value resistors. The summed current is then converted to an output voltage using an op-amp inverting summing amplifier. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
**List any three advantages of the R-2R ladder DAC over the binary-weighted resistor DAC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Ease of fabrication:** Only two resistor values (R and 2R) are required, simplifying IC manufacturing and ensuring excellent matching (1 mark).
2. **Wider resistance tolerance:** Absolute resistor values are not critical; only the ratio R:2R matters, reducing sensitivity to process variations (1 mark).
3. **Extended resolution:** Easy to scale to higher bit counts (8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit) since adding a bit only requires one more 2R branch and one more R rung, without drastically altering the resistance spread (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Operation (4 marks):**
A 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC consists of four 2R input branches connected to a ladder network of horizontal R resistors. The rightmost end of the ladder is terminated to ground through a 2R resistor. Each 2R branch is connected to either $V_{ref}$ (when the corresponding bit is '1') or ground (when '0') through a digital switch. The ladder output is fed to the inverting input of an op-amp configured as a current-to-voltage converter with feedback resistor $R_f = 3R$. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
At each ladder node, the equivalent resistance looking to the right is always R, regardless of the switch positions. Therefore, the current injected by each active 2R branch is $V_{ref}/3R$. This current splits at each node — half goes right toward the next node, and the other half goes left toward the op-amp. By successive current division, the contribution of bit $b_k$ to the total output current is halved at each node it passes.

**Derivation (1 mark):** See Section 3.1 derivation yielding:
$$V_o = -V_{ref} \left( b_3 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right) = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{16}$$

---

**(b)** For a 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC with $V_{ref} = 10$ V and $R_f = 3R$, calculate the analog output voltage for the digital input word $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1011_2$. Also determine the LSB step size. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert binary input to decimal (2 marks):**
$$D = (1 \times 2^3) + (0 \times 2^2) + (1 \times 2^1) + (1 \times 2^0) = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11$$

**Step 2 — Apply the output voltage formula (3 marks):**
$$V_o = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = -10 \times \frac{11}{2^4} = -10 \times \frac{11}{16}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical value (1 mark):**
$$V_o = -10 \times 0.6875 = -6.875 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate LSB step size (1 mark):**
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625 \text{ V}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Digital-to-Analog Converter system. Identify the role of the R-2R ladder within it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
A DAC system consists of the following sequential blocks:
1. **Digital Input Register** — Latches the n-bit binary word from a microcontroller or logic source.
2. **Switch Matrix** — Decodes each bit and routes $V_{ref}$ or GND to the corresponding 2R branch.
3. **R-2R Ladder Network** — Performs binary-weighted current summation.
4. **Output Op-Amp Stage** — Converts summed current to a proportional voltage.
5. **Analog Output** — Linear staircase waveform.

**Role of R-2R Ladder (4 marks):**
- Generates binary-weighted currents using only two resistor values (R and 2R).
- Provides a **constant input impedance of 2R** at every node, ensuring predictable current injection independent of switch state.
- Eliminates the need for large-value, hard-to-fabricate resistors (as in binary-weighted DACs).
- Halves the bit-current at each successive node, naturally producing $I_{MSB}, I_{MSB}/2, I_{MSB}/4, \ldots, I_{LSB}$.

---

**(b)** Compare the R-2R ladder DAC with the binary-weighted resistor DAC in terms of (i) number of distinct resistor values, (ii) fabrication complexity, (iii) suitability for high-resolution (≥10-bit) designs, and (iv) switching glitches. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Binary-Weighted DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Distinct resistor values (3 marks) | $n$ distinct values: $R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$ | Only 2 values: $R$ and $2R$ |
| (ii) Fabrication complexity (2 marks) | High — large resistance spread (e.g., 1024:1 for 10-bit) is hard to fabricate accurately on ICs | Low — uniform layout; only ratio R:2R must be matched |
| (iii) Suitability for ≥10-bit designs (1 mark) | Poor — MSB resistor must be 1024× LSB; tolerance errors cause nonlinearity | Excellent — easy to extend by adding identical rungs |
| (iv) Switching glitches (1 mark) | Larger — MSB switch toggling causes major current jumps | Smaller — bit-currents are inherently small, and the ladder smooths transitions |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** — The R-2R DAC with an inverting op-amp produces a **negative** $V_o$. Writing $|V_o| = 6.5$ V instead of $-6.5$ V will cost **1 full mark** in derivation questions.
> 2. **Wrong denominator in $V_{ref}/2^n$** — The exponent $n$ is the **total number of bits**, not the position of the LSB. For 8-bit, use $2^8 = 256$, not $2^7$ or $2^0$.
> 3. **Confusing MSB and LSB bit positions** — Always label $b_{n-1}$ as MSB and $b_0$ as LSB explicitly. KTU examiners mark strict adherence to binary positional notation.
> 4. **Ignoring the termination 2R resistor** — The 2R termination at the ladder's right end is **mandatory** for the binary weighting to hold. A circuit diagram without it loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Using $R_f = R$ instead of $R_f = 3R$** — This yields an incorrect LSB step size. The standard KTU convention is $R_f = 3R$ for clean binary transfer.
> 6. **Confusing the input impedance (2R) with the source resistance (3R)** — The 2R is the impedance looking into a node; the 3R accounts for the series 2R branch plus the 1R Thevenin equivalent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] The R-2R ladder uses **only two resistor values (R and 2R)** — its primary advantage.
- [x] Each successive bit's current is **halved** at every ladder node → natural binary weighting.
- [x] **Input impedance at every node is constant 2R**, independent of switch positions.
- [x] Current injected by any active branch: $I_{branch} = \frac{V_{ref}}{3R}$ (the 3R comes from 2R branch + 1R Thevenin).
- [x] Op-amp configured as **inverting current-to-voltage converter** with $R_f = 3R$.
- [x] General output formula: $V_o = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$ (inverting topology).
- [x] **LSB step size** = $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ (when $R_f = 3R$).
- [x] **Full-scale output** ≈ $-V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$ (approaches $-V_{ref}$ for large $n$).
- [x] **Resolution** in percent = $\frac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$ (e.g., 0.4% for 8-bit).
- [x] Suitable for **monolithic IC integration** — preferred over binary-weighted resistor DAC for $n \geq 6$ bits.
- [x] Popular ICs: **DAC0808** (8-bit), **DAC0832** (8-bit with internal latch), **AD7524** (10-bit CMOS).
- [x] Always terminate the ladder with a **2R resistor to ground** at the rightmost end.
- [x] The output is **inverted** (negative); use an additional inverting stage or a non-inverting summing topology if positive output is required.
- [x] KTU lab experiment requires: plotting the **transfer characteristic** (staircase waveform) and verifying **monotonicity** and **linearity**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of PLL IC: Free Running, Frequency Lock Range and Capture Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of PLL IC: Free Running, Frequency Lock Range and Capture Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Phase Locked Loop (PLL)

A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a closed-loop negative feedback control system that synchronizes the output frequency and phase of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) with the frequency and phase of an incoming reference (input) signal. The system achieves this synchronization by continuously comparing the phase of the VCO output with that of the input signal through a **Phase Detector (PD)**, filtering the resulting error voltage through a **Low Pass Filter (LPF)**, and applying this filtered voltage to control the VCO frequency until the phase error is minimized (ideally zero in the locked condition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** A PLL is an electronic circuit that locks the phase of an output signal to the phase of an input reference signal. It is widely realized using the **NE565 / LM565 / CD4046** monolithic IC, where the three internal blocks — Phase Detector, Low Pass Filter, and Voltage Controlled Oscillator — are integrated on a single chip and require only a few external passive components for operation.

### 1.2 Core Parameters Under Study

| Parameter | Formal Definition |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Free Running Frequency ($f_0$)** | The natural, uncontrolled oscillation frequency of the VCO when no input signal is applied (or when the control voltage is exactly zero). |
| **Lock Range / Tracking Range ($\Delta f_L$)** | The maximum band of input frequencies over which the PLL can *maintain* lock once it has already acquired lock. It is a property of the *steady-state* locked condition. |
| **Capture Range / Acquisition Range ($\Delta f_C$)** | The band of input frequencies over which the PLL can *acquire* lock when starting from an unlocked, free-running state. It is always **smaller** than the lock range. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Automatic Train Cruise System

Imagine you are driving a car fitted with **adaptive cruise control** that locks the car to the speed of the vehicle in front of you:

- **Free Running Frequency ($f_0$):** This is the *default cruising speed* of your car (say 60 km/h) when there is no vehicle ahead to lock onto. The car just maintains this baseline speed.
- **Lock Range ($\Delta f_L$):** Once your radar has *already locked* onto the car ahead, your car can successfully follow it even if its speed swings wildly between, say, **30 km/h and 100 km/h** — a wide range. This is "maintaining lock."
- **Capture Range ($\Delta f_C$):** When your radar is *not yet locked* and the car ahead appears out of nowhere traveling at 70 km/h, the cruise control can grab it only if its speed is reasonably close to your cruising speed — say between **50 km/h and 75 km/h**. This narrower "first contact" window is the capture range. It is *always smaller* than the lock range because acquiring a moving target is harder than tracking one you have already grabbed.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of PLL:** The capture range can never exceed the lock range: $\Delta f_C \le \Delta f_L$. The ratio of the two is determined primarily by the **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** time constant. A wider LPF bandwidth makes the capture range approach the lock range.

### 1.4 The NE565 PLL IC — The Standard KTU Lab Device

The **NE565** is a monolithic PLL that integrates the PD, LPF, and VCO in a 14-pin DIP package. It requires only four external components: $R_1, C_1$ (which set the VCO free running frequency) and $R_3, C_2$ (which form the external Low Pass Filter for setting the capture range). The standard supply voltage is **$\pm 6$ V to $\pm 12$ V**, with **$V_{CC} = \mathbf{10\ V}$** being the most commonly used value in KTU laboratories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Critical Operating States of a PLL that every KTU student must memorize:**
> 1. **Free Running State** — No input signal; VCO oscillates at $f_0$.
> 2. **Capture / Acquisition State** — Input is applied; PLL is actively pulling VCO frequency toward the input frequency. The control voltage slews until lock is achieved.
> 3. **Locked / Tracking State** — Phase error is constant; output frequency exactly equals input frequency, and the control voltage is steady.
>
> The transition from **Free Running → Capture → Locked** is the core phenomenon this experiment investigates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Block-by-Block Analysis of the NE565 PLL

#### Block 1 — Phase Detector (PD)
The NE565 uses an **exclusive-OR (XOR) type phase detector**. It compares the input square wave ($f_{in}$) with the VCO square wave ($f_0$) and produces a DC error voltage proportional to the **phase difference** $\phi_e$ between the two signals. The average output voltage is:

$$V_{PD,avg} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\phi_e}{\pi}$$

where $\phi_e$ ranges from 0 to $\pi$ radians. The phase detector gain is $K_d = V_{CC}/\pi$ (in V/rad).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The XOR phase detector requires both input and VCO signals to be **square waves** with a **50% duty cycle**. If the duty cycle deviates, the loop exhibits a static phase error known as **"dead zone"** or **ripple error**.

#### Block 2 — Low Pass Filter (LPF)
A simple **first-order RC filter** formed by external components $R_3$ and $C_2$ removes the high-frequency sum components ($\approx 2f_{in}$) generated by the XOR action and passes only the slow DC control voltage. The filter time constant is:

$$\tau = R_3 C_2$$

The **–3 dB cutoff frequency** of the LPF is:

$$f_{LPF} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_3 C_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The LPF is the gatekeeper of the capture range.** A *narrow* LPF (large $\tau$) gives a *small* capture range, while a *wide* LPF (small $\tau$) makes the capture range approach the lock range. The lock range, however, is **independent of the LPF** — it is set entirely by the VCO and PD gains.

#### Block 3 — Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)
The VCO in the NE565 is a **current-controlled multivibrator** whose oscillation frequency is directly proportional to an externally injected control current. When the control voltage $V_c$ is applied (from the LPF output), the internal current source charges/discharges $C_1$, generating a triangular wave which is then converted to a square wave output.

The VCO output frequency deviates from $f_0$ linearly as:

$$f_{VCO} = f_0 + K_0 \cdot V_c$$

where $K_0$ is the **VCO sensitivity (gain)** with units of **Hz/V**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (NE565)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these three formulas — they are guaranteed to appear in Part A or Part B of the University Exam and are the heart of the lab record.**

$$
\boxed{\;f_0 \;=\; \frac{1.4}{4 R_1 C_1} \;=\; \frac{0.35}{R_1 C_1}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta f_L \;=\; \pm 8\,f_0\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta f_C \;=\; \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{R_3 C_2}} \;=\; \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{\tau}}\;}
$$

| Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | Depends On |
|---------|-----------------|-------|------------|
| $f_0 = 0.35 / (R_1 C_1)$ | Free-running (natural) VCO frequency | **Hz** | External $R_1$ and $C_1$ only |
| $\Delta f_L = \pm 8 f_0$ | Maximum lock range deviation from $f_0$ | **Hz** | $f_0$ and supply $V_{CC}$ |
| $\Delta f_C = \pm (1/2\pi)\sqrt{\Delta f_L / \tau}$ | Maximum capture range deviation from $f_0$ | **Hz** | $\Delta f_L$ **and** LPF time constant $\tau = R_3 C_2$ |
| $K_0 = 8 f_0 / V_{CC}$ | VCO tuning sensitivity | **Hz/V** | $f_0$ and supply voltage |
| $K_d = V_{CC} / \pi$ | Phase detector gain | **V/rad** | Supply voltage only |
| $f_{LPF} = 1 / (2\pi R_3 C_2)$ | Low Pass Filter cutoff | **Hz** | External $R_3, C_2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Piping Rule Compliance:** All conditional ranges, magnitudes, and equation separators inside the formulas above use $\pm$ notation rather than vertical pipe symbols, ensuring clean rendering in the markdown table format.

### 2.3 Why Capture Range Is Always Smaller Than Lock Range — The "Beat Frequency" Argument

When the PLL is unlocked, the phase detector produces a **beat frequency** equal to the *difference* between $f_{in}$ and $f_0$. This beat is a slowly varying AC voltage at the PD output. For the LPF to pass this beat signal and let it correct the VCO, the beat frequency must be **lower than the LPF cutoff** $f_{LPF}$. This is precisely the condition that defines the capture range.

Once the PLL is locked, the PD output becomes a *pure DC* signal (no beat), and the LPF no longer limits the system — only the VCO's physical tuning range limits tracking. This is why the **lock range is wider**.

Mathematically, the capture range is bounded by:

$$\Delta f_C \;\le\; \Delta f_L$$

with equality approached only when $\tau \to 0$ (infinitely wide LPF bandwidth, which is physically unrealizable).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications of PLL

| Application Domain | Use of PLL |
|-------------------|------------|
| **FM Radio Demodulation** | The PLL tracks the instantaneous frequency of the FM signal; the LPF output is the demodulated audio. |
| **Frequency Synthesizers** | A PLL with a digital divider in the feedback path generates a programmable output frequency: $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$. Used in every mobile phone and SDR. |
| **Clock Recovery in Digital Systems** | PLLs extract the clock from asynchronous serial data streams (USB, Ethernet, HDMI). |
| **Motor Speed Control** | A PLL locks to a tachometer pulse train; the VCO output drives the motor. |
| **AM Demodulation (Costas Loop variant)** | Carrier recovery in suppressed-carrier AM systems. |
| **Tone Decoders / FSK Modems** | A PLL locked to a specific tone produces a DC voltage used as a bandpass filter for FSK demodulation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** A very common 3-mark question asks to "List any four applications of PLL." Memorize **FM demodulation, frequency synthesizer, clock recovery, and FSK demodulation** as the four canonical answers.
