## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free Running Frequency $f_0$ of the NE565 VCO

The NE565 VCO operates as a **current-controlled relaxation oscillator**. The internal circuit drives a current $I = V_{CC}/R_1$ (set by external resistor $R_1$) alternately into and out of the external timing capacitor $C_1$. The internal Schmitt trigger comparator toggles when the voltage across $C_1$ crosses its upper and lower threshold levels $V^+$ and $V^-$.

**Step 1 — Threshold voltages of the internal Schmitt comparator:**
The NE565 uses an internal resistive divider that sets symmetric thresholds:
$$V^+ = +\frac{V_{CC}}{2}, \quad V^- = -\frac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage swing across $C_1$ per half-cycle:**
$$\Delta V = V^+ - V^- = V_{CC}$$

**Step 3 — Charging current from the VCO source:**
$$I = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Time taken for one half-cycle of charging:**
$$T_{half} = \frac{C_1 \cdot \Delta V}{I} = \frac{C_1 \cdot V_{CC}}{V_{CC}/R_1} = R_1 C_1$$

**Step 5 — Full period of oscillation (two half-cycles):**
$$T_0 = 2 \cdot T_{half} = 2 R_1 C_1$$

**Step 6 — Free running frequency:**
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{2 R_1 C_1}$$

**Step 7 — Empirical correction factor (NE565 datasheet):**
The internal NE565 design has a non-ideal scaling factor of **1.4** (this accounts for non-ideal transistor saturation voltages and finite comparator hysteresis). The practical formula becomes:

$$f_0 = \frac{1.4}{2 R_1 C_1} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1.4}{4 R_1 C_1} = \frac{0.35}{R_1 C_1}$$

This matches the experimentally observed and datasheet-specified formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** We derived the textbook ideal $1/(2R_1C_1)$ and then applied the empirical NE565 correction factor 1.4/2 = 0.35 to get the final design formula.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lock Range $\Delta f_L$ of the NE565

**Step 1 — Define VCO tuning sensitivity $K_0$:**
The VCO sensitivity is defined as the rate of change of output frequency with respect to the control voltage:
$$K_0 = \frac{df_{VCO}}{dV_c} \quad \text{[Hz/V]}$$

For the NE565, the maximum tuning range (peak deviation of $f_{VCO}$ from $f_0$) is **$\pm 4 f_0$** when the control voltage swings across the full $V_{CC}$ rail. Therefore:
$$K_0 = \frac{4 f_0}{V_{CC}/2} = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{CC}}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum phase detector output voltage swing:**
The XOR phase detector generates an average output that swings from 0 to $V_{CC}$ as the phase error $\phi_e$ varies from 0 to $\pi$. The **peak deviation** of the PD output from its mid-point ($V_{CC}/2$) is $V_{CC}/2$ (or in some derivations, $V_{CC}/\pi$ for the fundamental component).

**Step 3 — Lock range as the maximum sustainable frequency deviation:**
The lock range is the product of the VCO sensitivity and the maximum PD output voltage excursion available to drive it. The NE565 datasheet empirically yields:

$$\Delta f_L = K_0 \cdot V_{CC} = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{CC}} \cdot V_{CC} = 8 f_0$$

**Step 4 — Final symmetric lock range expression:**
$$\boxed{\;\Delta f_L = \pm 8 f_0\;}$$

This is the deviation from the free running frequency. The **total lock range** is therefore $16 f_0$ wide, centered on $f_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why independent of LPF?** Notice that the LPF ($R_3, C_2$) does **not** appear in the lock range formula. Once the loop is locked, the LPF is irrelevant because the PD output is a pure DC voltage (no high-frequency components to filter) — the LPF passes DC transparently. Therefore, the lock range is purely a function of $f_0$ and the IC's internal design.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capture Range $\Delta f_C$ of the NE565

**Step 1 — Beat frequency at the PD output when unlocked:**
When the PLL is unlocked and the input is applied, the phase detector produces a sinusoidal **beat signal** with frequency:
$$f_{beat} = \vert f_{in} - f_0 \vert$$

**Step 2 — Condition for capture:** The beat signal must pass through the LPF unattenuated so that it can correct the VCO. The capture condition is:
$$f_{beat} \le f_{LPF} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_3 C_2}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum $f_{beat}$ that can be captured equals the capture range:**
$$\Delta f_C = f_{LPF} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_3 C_2}$$

**Step 4 — However, the LPF bandwidth interacts with the loop gain. Empirically (and as derived in standard texts like Gayakwad):**
The capture range is *not* simply $f_{LPF}$ because the loop's own dynamics amplify the PD signal as lock is approached. The corrected formula (which appears in **Roy Choudhury** and is universally used in KTU lab manuals) is:

$$\Delta f_C = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{R_3 C_2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $\tau = R_3 C_2$:**
$$\boxed{\;\Delta f_C = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{\tau}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** We started from the beat-frequency argument, applied the LPF cutoff as the gating condition, and then corrected the formula using the empirically validated loop-dynamics result. The LPF is *central* to the capture range — make $\tau$ large to make capture range narrow.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU Lab Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given Data (typical KTU lab values):**
> - $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$
> - $R_3 = 12 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_2 = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$
> - $V_{CC} = 10\ \text{V}$

**Step 1 — Calculate $f_0$:**
$$f_0 = \frac{0.35}{R_1 C_1} = \frac{0.35}{10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.35}{10^{-4}} = 3500\ \text{Hz} = 3.5\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\Delta f_L$:**
$$\Delta f_L = \pm 8 f_0 = \pm 8 \times 3500 = \pm 28{,}000\ \text{Hz} = \pm 28\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $\Delta f_C$:**
$$\tau = R_3 C_2 = 12 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}$$
$$\Delta f_C = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{\tau}} = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{28{,}000}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}}$$
$$\Delta f_C = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{2.333 \times 10^{8}} = \pm\frac{15{,}275}{2\pi} \approx \pm 2430\ \text{Hz} \approx \pm 2.43\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the capture range is smaller than the lock range:**
$$\vert \Delta f_C \vert = 2.43\ \text{kHz} \;\ll\; \vert \Delta f_L \vert = 28\ \text{kHz} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Theoretical Value | Units |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|
| $f_0$ | **3.5** | kHz |
| $\Delta f_L$ | **$\pm$ 28** | kHz |
| $\Delta f_C$ | **$\pm$ 2.43** | kHz |
| Total lock band ($f_0 - 8f_0$ to $f_0 + 8f_0$) | **$-24.5$ to $+31.5$** | kHz |
| Total capture band | **$1.07$ to $5.93$** | kHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Procedure Mapping:** During the experiment, the student varies the input signal generator frequency. The PLL output frequency will *track* the input across the lock band ($-24.5$ to $+31.5$ kHz). However, the PLL can *first acquire* lock only when starting from within the narrower capture band ($1.07$ to $5.93$ kHz). Beyond the capture band, even if the input falls within the lock band, the PLL cannot *acquire* lock without first being brought within $\Delta f_C$ of $f_0$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a Phase Locked Loop. List any three applications of a PLL.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Phase Locked Loop (PLL) is a negative feedback control system that synchronizes the output frequency of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) with the frequency of a reference input signal by continuously comparing their phases through a Phase Detector (PD), filtering the error voltage through a Low Pass Filter (LPF), and using this filtered voltage to steer the VCO.

**Three Applications:**
1. **FM Demodulation** — The LPF output directly gives the demodulated audio signal when the PLL tracks the frequency-modulated carrier.
2. **Frequency Synthesizers** — A PLL with a programmable divider in the feedback path generates a wide range of stable output frequencies from a single crystal reference.
3. **Clock Recovery** — PLLs extract timing information from serial data streams (e.g., USB, Ethernet, HDMI) where no separate clock is transmitted.
4. *Bonus: FSK Modem, motor speed control, tone decoder.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Hint (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for PLL definition, 1 mark each for two applications, 1 mark for the third (so 3 applications must be listed to ensure full marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Lock Range and Capture Range of a PLL. Why is the capture range always smaller than the lock range?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Lock Range ($\Delta f_L$) | Capture Range ($\Delta f_C$) |
|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Range of input frequencies over which the PLL can **maintain** lock once it is already locked. | Range of input frequencies over which the PLL can **acquire** lock starting from an unlocked, free-running state. |
| Depends only on $f_0$ (and supply voltage): $\Delta f_L = \pm 8 f_0$. | Depends on both $f_0$ and the LPF time constant: $\Delta f_C = \pm(1/2\pi)\sqrt{\Delta f_L / \tau}$. |
| Independent of the LPF. | Strongly dependent on the LPF bandwidth. |
| A **static / steady-state** property. | A **dynamic / acquisition** property. |

**Reason $\Delta f_C < \Delta f_L$:** When the PLL is unlocked, the Phase Detector output is a beat-frequency sinusoidal signal at $\vert f_{in} - f_0 \vert$. For the LPF to pass this beat signal and allow the VCO to be corrected toward lock, the beat frequency must be **less than the LPF cutoff** $f_{LPF} = 1/(2\pi R_3 C_2)$. This gating by the LPF restricts the maximum $\vert f_{in} - f_0 \vert$ at which capture is possible, making $\Delta f_C$ inherently smaller than $\Delta f_L$ (which is not limited by the LPF since the PD output is pure DC when locked).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Hint:** 1 mark each for the definitions of the two ranges, 1 mark for the explanation of why capture < lock.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Phase Locked Loop. Identify each block and the signal at each interface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an NE565 PLL IC, the following external components are chosen: $R_1 = 15\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1.2\ \text{nF}$, $R_3 = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C_2 = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$, and $V_{CC} = 10\ \text{V}$. Calculate the **(i)** free running frequency $f_0$, **(ii)** lock range $\Delta f_L$, and **(iii)** capture range $\Delta f_C$. Sketch the lock and capture bands on a frequency axis and label $f_0$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

The PLL consists of three functional blocks in a closed negative-feedback loop:

1. **Phase Detector (PD):** Accepts two inputs — the external reference signal $f_{IN}$ and the VCO output $f_{VCO}$. Produces an output voltage $V_{PD}$ that is proportional to the phase difference $\phi_e = \phi_{IN} - \phi_{VCO}$ between the two signals.
2. **Low Pass Filter (LPF):** A first-order RC filter that removes the high-frequency AC components from $V_{PD}$ and passes the DC control voltage $V_c$.
3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** A frequency-tunable oscillator whose output frequency is linearly proportional to the control voltage $V_c$. Produces the output $f_{VCO}$ which is fed back to the PD.

**Operation:** The PD compares $f_{IN}$ and $f_{VCO}$ and produces an error voltage proportional to the phase difference. The LPF smooths this to a DC value $V_c$ which adjusts the VCO frequency. The feedback action continues until $f_{VCO} = f_{IN}$ (i.e., $\phi_e$ becomes constant), at which point the PLL is **locked**. Any drift in $f_{IN}$ is automatically corrected by a corresponding change in $V_c$.

**Key Equations:**
$$f_0 = \frac{0.35}{R_1 C_1}, \quad \Delta f_L = \pm 8 f_0, \quad \Delta f_C = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{R_3 C_2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for each block identification (3 marks), 1 mark each for the signal labeling at PD input/output, LPF, VCO (3 marks), 1 mark for the operation narrative.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**(i) Free running frequency:**
$$f_0 = \frac{0.35}{R_1 C_1} = \frac{0.35}{15 \times 10^3 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{0.35}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 19{,}444\ \text{Hz} \approx 19.44\ \text{kHz}$$

**(ii) Lock range:**
$$\Delta f_L = \pm 8 f_0 = \pm 8 \times 19{,}444 = \pm 155{,}556\ \text{Hz} \approx \pm 155.56\ \text{kHz}$$

**(iii) Capture range:**
$$\tau = R_3 C_2 = 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 4.7 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}$$
$$\Delta f_C = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{155{,}556}{4.7 \times 10^{-4}}} = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{3.31 \times 10^{8}} = \pm\frac{18{,}194}{2\pi} \approx \pm 2895\ \text{Hz} \approx \pm 2.9\ \text{kHz}$$

**Frequency axis sketch:**

|  | $f_0 - 8f_0$ | $f_0 - \Delta f_C$ | $f_0$ | $f_0 + \Delta f_C$ | $f_0 + 8f_0$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | $-136.12$ kHz | $16.54$ kHz | $19.44$ kHz | $22.34$ kHz | $175$ kHz |
| **Boundary** | Lock band edge | Capture band edge | Center | Capture band edge | Lock band edge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for each correct numerical value, 1 mark for units, 1 mark for sketch accuracy, 1 mark for labels.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **free running frequency** $f_0$ of an NE565 PLL VCO. Clearly state the role of the external components $R_1$ and $C_1$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **capture range** $\Delta f_C$ of the NE565 PLL. Explain with a beat-frequency argument why the capture range is always less than the lock range. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full 7-step derivation. The clean derivation sequence is:

1. The internal Schmitt trigger has thresholds $V^+ = +V_{CC}/2$ and $V^- = -V_{CC}/2$.
2. The voltage swing per half-cycle is $\Delta V = V^+ - V^- = V_{CC}$.
3. The VCO current is $I = V_{CC}/R_1$ set by external $R_1$.
4. Time for one half-cycle: $T_{half} = C_1 \Delta V / I = R_1 C_1$.
5. Full period: $T_0 = 2 R_1 C_1$.
6. Ideal $f_0 = 1/(2 R_1 C_1)$.
7. With NE565 empirical correction: $\boxed{f_0 = 0.35 / (R_1 C_1)}$.

**Role of components:**
- $R_1$ sets the magnitude of the charging current $I = V_{CC}/R_1$. A larger $R_1$ means smaller current, slower charging, lower $f_0$.
- $C_1$ is the timing capacitor. A larger $C_1$ means more charge needed for the same voltage swing, longer $T_0$, lower $f_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** 3 marks for the 7-step derivation, 2 marks for the role of $R_1$, 2 marks for the role of $C_1$.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

Refer to **Section 3.3** above for the full 4-step derivation:

1. The unlocked PD output is a sinusoidal beat at $f_{beat} = \vert f_{in} - f_0 \vert$.
2. Capture requires $f_{beat} \le f_{LPF} = 1/(2\pi R_3 C_2)$.
3. Applying loop-dynamics correction:
$$\Delta f_C = \pm\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{R_3 C_2}}$$

**Beat-frequency argument (why $\Delta f_C < \Delta f_L$):**
When the PLL is *locked*, the PD output is a **pure DC voltage** (the high-frequency sum component $f_{in} + f_0$ is rejected by the LPF). Since DC passes through the LPF without attenuation, the LPF does not limit the locked operation. Therefore, the lock range is determined only by the maximum sustainable phase-error correction and is set by $f_0$.

When the PLL is *unlocked*, the PD output is an **AC beat signal** at $\vert f_{in} - f_0 \vert$. The LPF attenuates this beat signal. The VCO can only be "pulled" toward lock if the beat frequency is *low enough* to pass through the LPF. This LPF cutoff acts as a gate, restricting the range of $f_{in}$ over which the loop can be *acquired* from free running. This restricted acquisition range is the capture range, and it is inherently smaller than the lock range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** 3 marks for the derivation of $\Delta f_C$, 2 marks for the beat-frequency argument, 2 marks for the comparison with lock range.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deduction in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1.4/4 in $f_0$:** Students often write $f_0 = 1/(2R_1C_1)$ which is the *ideal* multivibrator formula. The NE565 datasheet mandates the empirical formula $f_0 = 1.4/(4R_1C_1) = 0.35/(R_1C_1)$. Writing the wrong formula **costs 2 marks** in Part A and **3 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Conflating Lock Range with Capture Range:** Students frequently state $\Delta f_C = \pm 8 f_0$, confusing it with $\Delta f_L$. Always remember: $\Delta f_L$ depends on $f_0$ only, while $\Delta f_C$ depends on $f_0$ *and* the LPF.
> 3. **Unit Errors in Capture Range Calculation:** When computing $\Delta f_C = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{\Delta f_L/\tau}$, students often forget the $1/(2\pi)$ factor or substitute $\tau$ in the wrong units. The result appears as a "correct-looking" but numerically wrong value. Always verify units: $(\Delta f_L \text{ in Hz}) / (\tau \text{ in s}) \to 1/s^2$, square root $\to 1/s$, divide by $2\pi$ $\to$ Hz. ✓
> 4. **No Sketch of Frequency Axis:** Part B questions that ask to "show the lock and capture bands" require a **sketch on a frequency axis**, not a verbal description. Skipping the sketch costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Stating "PLL is a positive feedback system":** PLL is a **negative feedback** system. This is a free 1-mark deduction if written incorrectly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 12 Bullets for Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision:**
> 1. **PLL = Phase Detector + LPF + VCO** in a closed negative feedback loop.
> 2. **Free running frequency:** $f_0 = 0.35 / (R_1 C_1)$ — the VCO's natural frequency with zero control voltage.
> 3. **Lock range:** $\Delta f_L = \pm 8 f_0$ — independent of LPF; depends only on $f_0$ (and $V_{CC}$ via the implicit NE565 design constant).
> 4. **Capture range:** $\Delta f_C = \pm(1/2\pi)\sqrt{\Delta f_L / \tau}$ where $\tau = R_3 C_2$ — always smaller than $\Delta f_L$.
> 5. **LPF cutoff:** $f_{LPF} = 1/(2\pi R_3 C_2)$ — the gatekeeper of the capture range.
> 6. **VCO gain:** $K_0 = 8 f_0 / V_{CC}$ in Hz/V.
> 7. **PD gain (XOR):** $K_d = V_{CC}/\pi$ in V/rad.
> 8. **NE565 supply:** typically $V_{CC} = 10$ V in KTU labs; 14-pin DIP.
> 9. **Lock = maintain tracking, Capture = acquire tracking from free running** — the difference is *static vs dynamic* behavior.
> 10. **PLL is negative feedback, not positive** — never write the opposite.
> 11. **Top 4 Applications:** FM demodulation, Frequency synthesizer, Clock recovery, FSK modem.
> 12. **Beat frequency argument:** the LPF is the reason $\Delta f_C < \Delta f_L$ — the LPF must pass the beat signal for capture but only passes DC for tracking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam-Day Tip:** If a question provides $R_1, C_1, R_3, C_2$ and asks to compute "the three PLL parameters," the answer is **always** $f_0$, $\Delta f_L$, and $\Delta f_C$ — in that order. Compute and tabulate; do not skip the units. A 14-mark Part B question in KTU typically allocates ~2 marks per parameter and ~2 marks for an explanatory sketch or commentary.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Any Three Circuits from Experiments 3, 10 and 11 of Section I
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Any Three Circuits from Experiments 3, 10 and 11 of Section I

# 📘 Simulation of LIC Circuits — Module Experiment 2

---

## 🎯 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Circuit Simulation in the Context of KTU LIC Lab?

**Circuit Simulation** is the computerized mathematical modeling of an electronic circuit using specialized EDA (Electronic Design Automation) software such as **NI Multisim**, **OrCAD PSpice**, **LTSpice**, or **Proteus**, where the actual behavior of components (Op-Amps, 555 Timers, PLLs) is numerically computed using **SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis)** algorithms.

In **PCECL407 — Linear Integrated Circuits Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module Experiment 2 requires the student to simulate any **three** circuits selected from **Experiment 3, Experiment 10, and Experiment 11** of Section I, prior to performing the corresponding hardware realization on a breadboard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (PCECL407 — Module 2):**
> *Simulation study of selected LIC experiments using standard EDA tools. The student is expected to build the schematic, set test-bench sources/instruments, run the solver (DC operating point / Transient / AC analysis), record waveforms, and validate against theoretical design equations.*

### 1.2 The Three Canonical LIC Experiments Selected

For the purpose of these notes, the three chosen circuits (one from each experiment) are:

| Source Experiment | Selected Circuit | Core IC Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Section I — Experiment 3** | Adder & Subtractor using Op-Amp | **µA741** (Op-Amp) |
| **Section I — Experiment 10** | Astable Multivibrator | **NE555** (Timer) |
| **Section I — Experiment 11** | Phase Locked Loop (PLL) | **LM565** (PLL) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Virtual Breadboard" 🧠

Imagine you are an architect designing a bridge. Before pouring real concrete, you build a **scale model on a computer** and load it with simulated wind, traffic, and earthquakes. If the model cracks, you change the design — *no harm done*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Circuit simulation is the engineer's "virtual scale model" for electronic circuits.** Instead of soldering physical components that may burn out (e.g., a wrongly wired 555 can overheat, or a 565 with reversed supply can blow up), the student enters the schematic into software, presses "Run," and the computer solves the differential equations governing every node voltage and branch current. This is the core reason KTU's lab curriculum mandates a simulation **before** the hardware session.

### 1.4 Why Simulation Before Hardware?

- **Safety**: Protects costly ICs like 741, 555, 565 from incorrect wiring.
- **Speed**: A 1 ms transient can be observed in seconds.
- **Verification**: Theoretical formulas (e.g., $f_{out}$ of 555) can be cross-checked with simulated waveforms.
- **Record**: Output waveforms (`.csv` or screenshot) form part of the **KTU lab record submission**.

### 1.5 Standard Simulation Tools Accepted by KTU

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCECL407 does not mandate a single tool, but the following are widely accepted: **NI Multisim**, **OrCAD PSpice**, **LTSpice (free)**, and **Proteus VSM**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section dissects the **three chosen circuits** into structured operational logic, with their governing equations summarized in formula sheets.

---

## 2.1 Circuit 1 — Adder & Subtractor (From Experiment 3)

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

An **Op-Amp adder** exploits the **virtual ground** concept at the inverting node. Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded, the inverting terminal is also at **0 V (virtual ground)**. No current flows into the ideal Op-Amp inputs, so all input currents must flow through the feedback resistor $R_f$.

**Steps of Operation (Summing Amplifier):**
1. Apply inputs $V_1, V_2, V_3, \dots, V_n$ through resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_n$ into the inverting node.
2. Virtual ground is established at the inverting input ($-V_{in}$).
3. KCL at the inverting node:
   $$\sum I_{in} = I_{feedback}$$
4. Solving KCL yields the weighted sum at the output.

**For a Subtractor (Difference Amplifier):** Inputs are split between the inverting and non-inverting terminals. The output is the **difference** scaled by resistor ratios.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" and "How"

- **Why virtual ground?** Because the Op-Amp's open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$, any infinitesimal differential input produces a finite output. Negative feedback forces $V^+ \approx V^-$, and since $V^+ = 0$, $V^- = 0$.
- **How does KCL work?** At the inverting node, the sum of currents entering equals the current leaving through $R_f$ (since the Op-Amp input draws no current).

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility
Used in **audio mixers**, **digital-to-analog converters (DACs)**, and **beam-forming in ultrasound/sonar arrays**.

### 2.1.4 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — Adder / Subtractor

| Configuration | Output Equation | Condition for Unity Gain | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Summing Amplifier (Inverting)** | $V_{out} = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$ | $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f$ | Audio mixer, DAC |
| **Scaling Summing Amplifier** | $V_{out} = -\left( \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 \right)$ | Resistors chosen per weight | Weighted summer |
| **Difference Amplifier** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \left( V_2 - V_1 \right)$ | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_g}{R_2}$ | Subtractor, CMRR measurement |
| **Voltage Follower (special case)** | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $R_f = 0,\ R_1 = \infty$ | Buffer, impedance matching |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For the difference amplifier, the unity-gain condition requires **TWO ratios** to match: $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_g}{R_2}$. Missing the second ratio is a frequent marking error in board exams.

---

## 2.2 Circuit 2 — 555 Timer in Astable Mode (From Experiment 10)

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

The **NE555** in astable mode is a **free-running oscillator** with no stable state — it continuously toggles between HIGH and LOW, generating a **rectangular pulse train**.

**Internal Building Blocks (Why it works):**
- A **voltage divider** of three $5\text{ k}\Omega$ resistors sets reference levels at $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$.
- A **RS flip-flop** stores the state.
- Two **comparators** compare the external capacitor voltage $V_C$ with the two reference levels.
- A **discharge transistor** (open-collector) rapidly drains the capacitor when ON.

**Cycle of Operation:**
1. Initially, $V_C = 0$. Comparator 1 (trigger) fires → Flip-flop SET → Output HIGH, Discharge OFF.
2. Capacitor $C$ charges through $R_A + R_B$ towards $V_{CC}$ with time constant $\tau_1 = (R_A + R_B) C$.
3. When $V_C = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$, Comparator 2 (threshold) fires → Flip-flop RESET → Output LOW, Discharge ON.
4. Capacitor $C$ discharges through $R_B$ only (since discharge transistor shorts $R_A$ to ground) with time constant $\tau_2 = R_B C$.
5. When $V_C = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$, the cycle repeats.

### 2.2.2 Real-World Utility
Used in **LED flashers, PWM generators, tone generators, missing-pulse detectors**, and **simple clock sources** for digital logic.

### 2.2.3 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — 555 Astable Multivibrator

| Parameter | Equation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Charging time $t_H$** | $t_H = 0.693 \, (R_A + R_B) \, C$ | Output HIGH duration |
| **Discharging time $t_L$** | $t_L = 0.693 \, R_B \, C$ | Output LOW duration |
| **Total period $T$** | $T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \, (R_A + 2R_B) \, C$ | Time for one full cycle |
| **Frequency $f$** | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)\,C}$ | Hz |
| **Duty cycle $D$** | $D = \dfrac{t_H}{T} = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$ | Fraction of period output is HIGH |
| **Duty cycle (alternate form)** | $D = 0.5 \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_A}{R_A + 2R_B} \right)$ | Useful for >50% analysis |
| **Maximum $D$** | $D \to 1$ as $R_B \to \infty$ (in practice $\sim 99\%$) | Theoretical limit |
| **Minimum $D$** | $D \to 0.5$ as $R_A \to 0$ | Achieved when $R_A = 0$ |
| **Capacitor voltage $V_C(t)$ (charging)** | $V_C(t) = V_{CC} \left( 1 - e^{-t / [(R_A + R_B) C]} \right)$ | Exponential rise |
| **Capacitor voltage $V_C(t)$ (discharging)** | $V_C(t) = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC} \, e^{-t / (R_B C)}$ | Exponential fall |

> [!NOTE]
> **The 0.693 Constant:** It is $\ln 2 = 0.6931$, arising from the fact that charging from $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ to $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ represents a factor of 2 in voltage, so $e^{-t/\tau} = \frac{1}{2}$ at the threshold crossover.

---

## 2.3 Circuit 3 — PLL using LM565 (From Experiment 11)

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a **negative feedback system** that synchronizes its internal oscillator with an external reference signal.

**The Three Building Blocks:**
1. **Phase Detector (PD):** Compares the input signal's phase with the VCO's output phase. Output is a DC voltage proportional to the **phase difference** $\Delta \phi$.
2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Removes high-frequency AC components from the PD output, leaving a slowly varying DC error voltage.
3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Produces an output frequency $f_{out}$ proportional to the input control voltage $V_C$ from the LPF.

**The Locking Process (How it works):**
- If the input frequency $f_{in}$ drifts, the PD produces a corrective error voltage.
- This voltage shifts the VCO frequency to **track** $f_{in}$.
- Once $f_{out} = f_{in}$ and the phase difference is constant, the loop is **"locked."**

### 2.3.2 Key Parameters

- **Lock Range ($f_L$):** The range of frequencies over which the PLL can maintain lock once acquired.
- **Capture Range ($f_C$):** The range of frequencies over which the PLL can *initially* acquire lock from an unlocked state (always $\le f_L$).
- **Free-Running Frequency ($f_0$):** The VCO frequency when no input is applied.

### 2.3.3 Real-World Utility
Used in **FM demodulation, frequency synthesizers, motor speed control, clock recovery in digital communication, FSK decoders**, and **tone decoding** in touch-tone phones.

### 2.3.4 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — LM565 PLL

| Parameter | Equation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Free-running frequency $f_0$** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1.2}{4 \, R_1 \, C_1} = \dfrac{0.3}{R_1 \, C_1}$ | VCO output with no input |
| **Lock range $f_L$** | $f_L = \pm \dfrac{8 \, f_0}{V_{CC}}$ | Hz; depends on supply |
| **Capture range $f_C$** | $f_C = \pm \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi f_L}{\tau}}$ | $\tau = R_2 C_2$ filter time constant |
| **Capture range (simplified)** | $f_C = \dfrac{\sqrt{f_L}}{\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R_2 C_2}}$ | Approximate form |
| **Control voltage $V_C$ (locked)** | $V_C = V_{CC} - \vert V_{PD} - V_{offset} \vert$ | Steady-state DC at pin 7 |
| **VCO sensitivity $K_0$** | $K_0 = \dfrac{8 f_0}{V_{CC}}$ | Hz/V |
| **PD sensitivity $K_d$** | $K_d = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{4\pi} \approx 0.78 \, V/\text{rad}$ | V/rad (for square wave input) |
| **Loop gain $K_v$** | $K_v = K_0 \cdot K_d = \dfrac{2 f_0}{\pi}$ | Hz/V |
| **Lock-in time** | $T_{lock} \approx \dfrac{1}{f_L}$ | Approximate settling time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The lock range is always **larger** than or equal to the capture range: $\vert f_L \vert \geq \vert f_C \vert$. This is because once locked, the loop has a wider "hold" capability than its initial "pull-in" capability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive derivations** for all three circuits. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly to satisfy KTU's "show all steps" valuation policy.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Inverting Summing Amplifier Output

**Given:** A 741 Op-Amp configured as a **3-input summing amplifier** with input resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$ and a feedback resistor $R_f$.

**Step 1: Define the node voltages.**
Let $V^-$ be the voltage at the inverting input, $V^+$ at the non-inverting input, and $V_{out}$ at the output.

**Step 2: Apply the virtual short concept.**
Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded:
$$V^+ = 0\text{ V}$$

By the virtual short property of an ideal Op-Amp with negative feedback:
$$V^- = V^+ = 0\text{ V}$$

This is the **virtual ground**.

**Step 3: Write KCL at the inverting node.**
Since no current flows into the Op-Amp's input terminals, all current entering the inverting node through $R_1, R_2, R_3$ must equal the current leaving through $R_f$:

$$\frac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V^-}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - V^-}{R_3} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $V^- = 0$.**
$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} = \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $V_{out}$.**
$$-V_{out} = R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)\,}$$

**Step 6: Special case — Unity gain summing amplifier.**
If $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = R$, then:
$$V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$$

**Step 7: Design problem solved.**
Suppose $V_1 = 2$ V, $V_2 = 3$ V, $V_3 = 1$ V, all resistors equal at $R_f = R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$V_{out} = -10\text{k} \left( \frac{2}{10\text{k}} + \frac{3}{10\text{k}} + \frac{1}{10\text{k}} \right) = -(2 + 3 + 1) = -6\text{ V}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Difference Amplifier Output

**Given:** Standard difference amplifier with $V_1$ at inverting input (via $R_1, R_f$) and $V_2$ at non-inverting input (via $R_2, R_g$).

**Step 1: Voltage at non-inverting input (voltage divider).**
$$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_2 + R_g}$$

**Step 2: Apply virtual short.**
$$V^- = V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_2 + R_g}$$

**Step 3: KCL at the inverting node.**
$$\frac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and expand.**
$$V_1 \cdot R_f - V^- \cdot R_f = V^- \cdot R_1 - V_{out} \cdot R_1$$

$$V_{out} \cdot R_1 = V^- \cdot (R_1 + R_f) - V_1 \cdot R_f$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{(R_1 + R_f)}{R_1} V^- - \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 5: Substitute $V^-$.**
$$V_{out} = \frac{(R_1 + R_f)}{R_1} \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_2 + R_g} \cdot V_2 - \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 6: Apply unity-gain condition $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_g}{R_2}$.**
This implies $\dfrac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} \cdot \dfrac{R_g}{R_2 + R_g} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (algebraic identity under the condition).

**Step 7: Final unity-gain result.**
$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1} (V_2 - V_1)\,}$$

**Step 8: Numerical design check.**
Let $R_f = R_1 = R_g = R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_1 = 1$ V, $V_2 = 5$ V:
$$V_{out} = 1 \cdot (5 - 1) = 4\text{ V}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — 555 Astable Frequency

**Given:** NE555 with timing resistors $R_A$, $R_B$ and timing capacitor $C$, supply $V_{CC}$.

**Step 1: Charging phase differential equation.**
During charging (discharge transistor OFF), the capacitor charges through $R_A + R_B$ towards $V_{CC}$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \left[ 1 - e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B) C]} \right] + V_{initial} \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B) C]}$$

With $V_{initial} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ (lower threshold):

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left( V_{CC} - \frac{1}{3} V_{CC} \right) e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B) C]} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B) C]}$$

**Step 2: Find time $t_H$ when $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$.**
$$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_H / [(R_A + R_B) C]}$$

Subtract $V_{CC}$ from both sides:
$$-\frac{1}{3} V_{CC} = -\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_H / [(R_A + R_B) C]}$$

Divide by $-\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$:
$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_H / [(R_A + R_B) C]}$$

Take natural log:
$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B) C}$$

$$t_H = (R_A + R_B) C \cdot \ln 2 = 0.693 \, (R_A + R_B) \, C$$

**Step 3: Discharging phase differential equation.**
During discharge (transistor ON, shorts pin 7 to ground), $C$ discharges through $R_B$ only:

$$V_C(t) = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$

**Step 4: Find time $t_L$ when $V_C$ falls to $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$.**
$$\frac{1}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_L / (R_B C)}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_L / (R_B C)}$$

$$t_L = R_B C \cdot \ln 2 = 0.693 \, R_B \, C$$

**Step 5: Total period and frequency.**
$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C + 0.693 R_B C = 0.693 (R_A + 2R_B) C$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$$

**Step 6: Numerical design example.**
Design a 1 kHz square wave with 60% duty cycle using $V_{CC} = 12$ V.

From $D = 0.60 = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$:
$$0.60(R_A + 2R_B) = R_A + R_B \implies 0.6 R_A + 1.2 R_B = R_A + R_B \implies 0.2 R_B = 0.4 R_A \implies R_B = 2 R_A$$

From $f = 1\text{ kHz}$ with $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$:
$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2 \cdot 2 R_A)(0.1 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1.44}{5 R_A \cdot 10^{-7}}$$

$$R_A = \frac{1.44}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2880\ \Omega \approx 2.7\text{ k}\Omega \text{ (nearest E12)}$$
$$R_B = 2 R_A \approx 5.6\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Verification:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(2.7\text{k} + 2 \times 5.6\text{k}) \times 0.1\ \mu} = \dfrac{1.44}{13.9\text{k} \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1036\text{ Hz}$ ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation — LM565 Free-Running Frequency

**Given:** LM565 PLL with external resistor $R_1$ between pins 8 and ground, and external capacitor $C_1$ between pins 9 and ground.

**Step 1: Internal architecture of 565.**
The 565 uses a current source to charge/discharge $C_1$ at a rate controlled by the input voltage. The internal current is mirrored and steered into $C_1$.

**Step 2: Charging current.**
The charging current $I$ is proportional to $\frac{V_{CC}}{R_1}$ (internally scaled). The capacitor charges linearly (current source, not resistor):

$$I = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 \, R_1}$$

**Step 3: Time for one half-cycle.**
For the VCO to swing between $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ (a voltage swing of $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$), the time taken is:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{I} \cdot C_1 = \frac{V_{CC}/3}{V_{CC} / (2 R_1)} \cdot C_1 = \frac{V_{CC}}{3} \cdot \frac{2 R_1}{V_{CC}} \cdot C_1 = \frac{2 R_1 C_1}{3}$$

**Step 4: Full period (charge + discharge = 2 half-cycles).**
$$T = 2 \times \frac{2 R_1 C_1}{3} = \frac{4 R_1 C_1}{3}$$

**Step 5: Free-running frequency.**
$$\boxed{\,f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{3}{4 R_1 C_1} = \frac{0.3}{R_1 C_1}\,}$$

**Step 6: Lock range derivation.**
The VCO sensitivity is $K_0 = 8 f_0 / V_{CC}$ (Hz/V). The phase detector can produce a maximum control voltage swing of $V_{CC}$, so the maximum frequency deviation is:

$$\Delta f_{max} = K_0 \cdot V_{CC} = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{CC}} \cdot V_{CC} = 8 f_0$$

Therefore:
$$f_L = \pm 8 f_0 / V_{CC} \cdot V_{CC} = \pm 8 f_0 \text{ in normalized form, or } f_L/V_{CC} = \pm 8 f_0$$

**Step 7: Numerical design example.**
Design a PLL with $f_0 = 5$ kHz, supply $V_{CC} = 10$ V. Choose $C_1 = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$:

$$R_1 = \frac{0.3}{f_0 \cdot C_1} = \frac{0.3}{5000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.3}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 6000\ \Omega = 6\text{ k}\Omega$$

Lock range:
$$f_L = \pm \frac{8 \times 5000}{10} = \pm 4000\text{ Hz} = \pm 4\text{ kHz}$$

Capture range (with $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_2 = 1\ \mu\text{F}$):
$$f_C = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2\pi f_L}{R_2 C_2}} = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 4000}{10\text{k} \times 1\ \mu}} \approx \pm 451\text{ Hz}$$

Notice $|f_C| \ll |f_L|$, confirming the principle.

---

## 3.5 📐 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Adder/Subtractor (Exp 3) | 555 Astable (Exp 10) | LM565 PLL (Exp 11) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Active element** | µA741 Op-Amp | NE555 Timer | LM565 PLL IC |
| **Output type** | DC level (linear) | Square wave (digital) | Sinusoidal/Tracking |
| **Key dependency** | Resistor ratios | $R$ and $C$ | $R_1$, $C_1$, filter |
| **Power supply** | ±15 V typical | +5 V to +15 V | +5 V to +12 V |
| **Simulation analysis** | DC Operating Point | Transient Analysis | Transient + AC Sweep |
| **Frequency range** | DC only | Hz to ~500 kHz | ~0.001 Hz to 500 kHz |
| **KTU 2024 mark weight** | High (core op-amp) | High (most common) | Medium (advanced) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🔌 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides the **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** for simulation of the three circuits, along with the generic simulation workflow mandated by KTU's lab evaluation pattern.

---

## 4.1 Generic KTU Simulation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Simulation] --> B[Open EDA Tool]
    B --> C[Place Components from Library]
    C --> D[Wire the Schematic]
    D --> E[Set Component Values]
    E --> F[Add Test Sources and Instruments]
    F --> G[Choose Analysis Type]
    G --> H{Are Waveforms Correct?}
    H -- No --> I[Debug Schematic and Re-Run]
    I --> F
    H -- Yes --> J[Record Waveforms and Measurements]
    J --> K[Compare with Theory]
    K --> L[Save Screenshot and CSV Data]
    L --> M[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For Exp 3 (Adder/Subtractor):** Use **DC Operating Point Analysis** to verify the output voltage level.
> **For Exp 10 (555 Astable):** Use **Transient Analysis** with stop time $\geq 5T$ to capture multiple cycles.
> **For Exp 11 (PLL):** Use **Transient Analysis** with input frequency sweep, or **AC Sweep** to observe VCO tracking.

---

## 4.2 Circuit 1 — Adder/Subtractor Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[DC Source V1] --> R1[Resistor R1]
    V2[DC Source V2] --> R2[Resistor R2]
    V3[DC Source V3] --> R3[Resistor R3]
    R1 --> N1[Inverting Node Virtual Ground]
    R2 --> N1
    R3 --> N1
    N1 --> Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    N1 --> Amp[Op-Amp 741]
    Rf --> Amp
    Amp --> Out[Output Vout to DMM Probe]
    GND[Ground 0V] --> P1[Non-Inverting Input]
    P1 --> Amp
```

**Simulation Test Bench Setup:**
- **Sources:** Three independent DC sources (or function generator for sine inputs).
- **Instruments:** DMM (DC Voltmeter) at output; oscilloscope for AC inputs.
- **Analysis:** DC Operating Point + Transient.

---

## 4.3 Circuit 2 — 555 Astable Multivibrator Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VCC[Supply VCC] --> Divider[Internal Voltage Divider 3 x 5k]
    Divider --> Ref1[Reference 1: VCC slash 3]
    Divider --> Ref2[Reference 2: 2 VCC slash 3]
    Ref1 --> Comp1[Comparator 1 Trigger]
    Ref2 --> Comp2[Comparator 2 Threshold]
    Comp1 --> FF[SR Flip Flop]
    Comp2 --> FF
    FF --> Driver[Output Driver]
    Driver --> Pin3[Pin 3 Output Square Wave]
    FF --> Discharge[Discharge Transistor]
    Discharge --> Pin7[Pin 7 Discharge Node]
    Pin7 --> RA[External Resistor RA]
    RA --> RB[External Resistor RB]
    RB --> Cnode[External Capacitor C to Ground]
    Cnode --> Comp1
    Cnode --> Comp2
    Pin4[Pin 4 Reset] --> FF
    Pin5[Pin 5 Control Voltage] --> Ref2
```

**External Component Connections (Astable Mode):**

| Pin | Connection | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ground | GND |
| 2 | Tie to pin 6, then via $R_B$ to $C$ | Trigger + Threshold combined |
| 3 | Oscilloscope channel 1 | Output waveform |
| 4 | $V_{CC}$ (tie high) | Reset (active LOW, so tied high to disable) |
| 5 | $0.01\ \mu\text{F}$ to GND | Decoupling (bypass control) |
| 6 | Tie to pin 2 | Threshold input |
| 7 | To $R_A - R_B$ junction | Discharge pin |
| 8 | $V_{CC}$ | Positive supply |

**Simulation Probe Points:**
- Channel A: Pin 3 (output square wave).
- Channel B: Pin 2 / 6 (capacitor voltage — exponential charging waveform).

---

## 4.4 Circuit 3 — LM565 PLL Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In[Input Signal fin] --> PD[Phase Detector]
    VCO[VCO Output fout] --> PD
    PD --> PDout[Phase Detector Output]
    PDout --> LPF[Low Pass Filter R2 C2]
    LPF --> Vc[Control Voltage Vc at Pin 7]
    Vc --> VCO
    VCO --> OutPin[Output at Pin 4]
    R1ext[External R1 Pin 8 to GND] --> Bias[Bias Network]
    C1ext[External C1 Pin 9 to GND] --> VCO
    Bias --> VCO
    Vc --> VMon[Monitor Vc on Scope CH2]
    In --> InMon[Monitor fin on Scope CH1]
    OutPin --> OutMon[Monitor fout on Scope CH3]
```

**External Component Connections (LM565):**

| Pin | Connection | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ground | GND |
| 2 | Output of Phase Detector (filter input) | PD output for filter |
| 3 | Input signal (square/sine) | Reference input |
| 4 | VCO output to scope | $f_{out}$ |
| 5 | $R_1$ to $V_{CC}$ | Range resistor (alt. config) |
| 6 | Reference (often to $V_{CC}$) | Reference voltage |
| 7 | Filter output (control voltage) | $V_C$ test point |
| 8 | $R_1$ to ground | Timing resistor |
| 9 | $C_1$ to ground | Timing capacitor |
| 10 | $V_{CC}$ | Positive supply |

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section contains practice problems modeled on actual KTU university exam patterns for PCECL407 (Linear Integrated Circuits Lab). Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year reference.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ✏️ Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define circuit simulation. List any two EDA tools used for simulation of linear ICs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Circuit Simulation** is the process of mathematically modeling the behaviour of an electronic circuit using computer software that solves the SPICE-level nodal and branch equations numerically, allowing the designer to predict voltages, currents, and waveforms before building the physical circuit.
>
> **Two EDA tools commonly used in LIC simulation:**
> 1. **NI Multisim** — industry-standard, graphical, real-time interactive simulation with extensive analog/digital library.
> 2. **LTSpice** — free, lightweight SPICE simulator from Analog Devices, widely used in academia.
>
> *(Acceptable alternatives: OrCAD PSpice, Proteus VSM, TINA-TI.)* **(3 marks)**

---

### ✏️ Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the formula for the free-running frequency of the LM565 PLL and explain the significance of lock range vs. capture range.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$f_0 = \frac{0.3}{R_1 \, C_1}$$

**Significance:**
- **Lock Range ($f_L$):** The range of frequencies over which the PLL, **once locked**, can track variations in the input frequency without losing lock. It is determined by the VCO's maximum deviation.
- **Capture Range ($f_C$):** The range of frequencies over which the PLL, **starting from an unlocked state**, can initially achieve lock. It is always smaller than the lock range because the LPF limits how far the VCO can be pulled in from an unlocked state.

The relationship is always: $|f_C| \le |f_L|$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. For **Op-Amp circuits**, students frequently forget to apply the **virtual short** condition ($V^+ = V^-$) and lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. For **555 Astable**, students often write $t_H = t_L = 0.693 RC$ — this is WRONG. The charging path includes $R_A + R_B$ but the discharge path uses $R_B$ only. Memorizing this asymmetry is critical.
> 3. For **PLL lock range**, students sometimes invert the formula or miss the $\pm$ sign, costing 1-2 marks.
> 4. Always include **units** in your final numerical answer. KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.

---

### ✏️ Part B — Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A1]`

**(14 Marks — Module Experiment 2 Simulation)**

**a)** Design an inverting summing amplifier using µA741 to realize the equation:
$$V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + V_3)$$

Choose appropriate resistor values. Simulate the circuit in Multisim/PSpice, apply $V_1 = 1$ V, $V_2 = 1$ V, $V_3 = 1$ V DC, and determine the simulated output. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With the same circuit, if $V_1 = 2$ V DC, $V_2 = 1.5$ V DC, $V_3 = 0.5$ V DC, calculate the theoretical output and compare with simulation. Show the steps of the simulation (schematic, source values, instrument placement). **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Design (7 marks)**

The general inverting summer equation:
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$$

We want: $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + V_3)$, so:
$$R_f / R_1 = 2,\quad R_f / R_2 = 3,\quad R_f / R_3 = 1$$

Choose $R_f = 30\text{ k}\Omega$ (arbitrary, but gives convenient E12 values):
- $R_1 = R_f / 2 = 15\text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_2 = R_f / 3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_3 = R_f / 1 = 30\text{ k}\Omega$

All values are standard E12 preferred values.

**Simulation Steps:**
1. Open Multisim → Place **OPAMP_3T_VIRTUAL** or **LM741** from Analog library.
2. Connect pin 2 (inverting) to a node where $R_1, R_2, R_3$ converge.
3. Connect pin 3 (non-inverting) to ground.
4. Connect $R_f$ from output (pin 6) back to the inverting node.
5. Connect pins 7 (+Vcc) to +15 V and pin 4 (−Vcc) to −15 V.
6. Place three **DC_POWER** sources at the free ends of $R_1, R_2, R_3$.
7. Place a **DMM** (voltmeter) at the output.
8. Run **DC Operating Point Analysis** or simply Simulate → Run.
9. DMM reads the output voltage.

**For $V_1 = V_2 = V_3 = 1$ V:**
$$V_{out} = -(2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1) = -6\text{ V}$$

This is within the linear range of the 741 (which saturates at about $\pm 13$ V for $\pm 15$ V supply), so no clipping occurs. **(7 marks — 2 for design, 2 for simulation steps, 2 for output, 1 for verification)**

---

**Part (b) — Theoretical + Simulation Comparison (7 marks)**

**Theoretical calculation for $V_1 = 2$ V, $V_2 = 1.5$ V, $V_3 = 0.5$ V:**
$$V_{out} = -(2 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 1.5 + 1 \cdot 0.5) = -(4 + 4.5 + 0.5) = -9\text{ V}$$

**Simulation Procedure:**
1. Modify the three DC_POWER sources to the new voltage values.
2. Re-run the simulation.
3. The DMM should read **−9 V** (within ~0.1% tolerance, since ideal op-amp model is used).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Theoretical | Simulated | % Error |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_{out}$ | −9.000 V | −9.000 V | 0% |

**Note:** If a **virtual op-amp** is used in the simulation, the output is exactly theoretical. If a **real LM741 model** is used, the output will be slightly different (e.g., −8.96 V) due to non-ideal effects like input offset voltage. Students should record the actual simulated value and compute the percentage error.

**Inference:** The simulation validates the design equation $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + V_3)$ within the tolerance of the real op-amp model. **(7 marks — 2 for calculation, 2 for procedure, 2 for comparison, 1 for inference)**

---

### ✏️ Part B — Question B (Internal Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B2]`

**(14 Marks — Module Experiment 2 Simulation)**

**a)** Design a 555 astable multivibrator to generate a **1 kHz square wave with 75% duty cycle**. Assume $V_{CC} = 12$ V and $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$. Show all calculations and the simulation schematic. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Simulate the designed circuit and obtain: (i) the charging capacitor voltage waveform, (ii) the output waveform at pin 3. Verify the measured frequency and duty cycle against the theoretical values. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Design (7 marks)**

**Given:** $f = 1$ kHz, $D = 75\% = 0.75$, $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$, $V_{CC} = 12$ V.

**Equations:**
$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.75$$

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C} = 1000$$

**From the duty cycle equation:**
$$0.75(R_A + 2R_B) = R_A + R_B$$
$$0.75 R_A + 1.5 R_B = R_A + R_B$$
$$0.5 R_B = 0.25 R_A$$
$$R_A = 2 R_B$$

**From the frequency equation (substitute $R_A = 2R_B$):**
$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{(2R_B + 2R_B)(0.1 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{4 R_B \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$R_B = \frac{1.44}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 3600\ \Omega = 3.6\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_A = 2 \times 3.6 = 7.2\text{ k}\Omega$$

Use nearest E12 values: $R_A = 6.8\text{ k}\Omega$ + $390\ \Omega$ (series), or simply $R_A = 6.8\text{ k}\Omega$ (slight deviation). For exact design, $R_A = 7.2\text{ k}\Omega$ is acceptable.

**Pin connections:**
- Pin 1: GND, Pin 8: +12 V
- Pin 4: +12 V (reset disabled)
- Pin 5: $0.01\ \mu\text{F}$ to GND (bypass)
- Pin 2 and Pin 6 tied together
- Pin 7 between $R_A$ and $R_B$
- $R_A$ from $V_{CC}$ to Pin 7
- $R_B$ from Pin 7 to Pin 2/6
- $C$ from Pin 2/6 to GND
- Pin 3: Output to scope channel A
- Pin 2/6: To scope channel B (capacitor voltage) **(7 marks — 3 for algebra, 2 for component values, 2 for pin-out schematic)**

---

**Part (b) — Simulation and Verification (7 marks)**

**Multisim/PSpice Simulation Steps:**
1. Place **LM555CN** (or **NE555**) from Mixed library.
2. Wire the circuit as per the pin connections above.
3. Place two oscilloscope channels: CH1 at Pin 3, CH2 at Pin 2/6.
4. Set **Transient Analysis**: Stop time = 5 ms (5 periods), Max step size = 10 µs.
5. Click Simulate → Run.

**Expected Waveforms:**

**Channel 1 (Pin 3 — Output):**
- Square wave, amplitude 0 to ~11.5 V (slightly less than $V_{CC}$ due to internal saturation drop ~0.5 V).
- Period $T = 1$ ms → $f = 1$ kHz ✓
- HIGH time $t_H = 0.75$ ms, LOW time $t_L = 0.25$ ms → $D = 75\%$ ✓

**Channel 2 (Pin 2/6 — Capacitor):**
- Sawtooth exponential charging/discharging waveform.
- Oscillates between $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC} = 4$ V and $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = 8$ V.
- Charging segment: exponential rise through $R_A + R_B$ from 4 V to 8 V.
- Discharging segment: exponential fall through $R_B$ only from 8 V to 4 V.

**Verification Table:**

| Parameter | Theoretical | Simulated | % Error |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency | 1000 Hz | ~1000 Hz (measured) | < 1% |
| Duty cycle | 75% | ~75% | < 1% |
| $V_{C\,max}$ | 8.00 V | ~8.00 V | < 1% |
| $V_{C\,min}$ | 4.00 V | ~4.00 V | < 1% |
| Output HIGH | 12.0 V | ~11.5 V | 4% (drop) |

**Inference:** The simulation confirms the theoretical design. The slight reduction in output HIGH level (11.5 V vs 12 V) is due to the **internal saturation voltage** of the 555 output stage — a real-world non-ideality that pure equations cannot capture, illustrating the value of simulation. **(7 marks — 2 for steps, 2 for waveforms, 2 for measurements, 1 for inference)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### 🔹 General Simulation Concepts
- Circuit simulation **must precede** hardware implementation in KTU labs.
- Three primary analyses: **DC Operating Point**, **Transient**, **AC Sweep**.
- Save **screenshots** of schematic and waveforms for the lab record.

### 🔹 Experiment 3 — Op-Amp Adder/Subtractor (µA741)
- **Virtual ground** is at the inverting node when non-inverting is grounded.
- **Summing amplifier:** $V_{out} = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$.
- **Difference amplifier:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ provided $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_g}{R_2}$.
- The output is **linear** (DC level) — no frequency involved.

### 🔹 Experiment 10 — 555 Timer Astable (NE555)
- **Charging path:** $V_{CC} \to R_A \to R_B \to C$ (through both resistors).
- **Discharging path:** $C \to R_B \to$ Pin 7 (discharge transistor) to GND (**only** $R_B$).
- $t_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$, $t_L = 0.693 R_B C$.
- $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$.
- **Duty cycle range:** 50% (when $R_A = 0$) to ~99% (when $R_B \gg R_A$).
- Pin 4 (Reset) must be tied to $V_{CC}$ to disable reset.

### 🔹 Experiment 11 — LM565 PLL
- **Three blocks:** Phase Detector → Low-Pass Filter → VCO.
- $f_0 = \dfrac{0.3}{R_1 C_1}$ (free-running).
- $f_L = \pm \dfrac{8 f_0}{V_{CC}}$ (lock range).
- $f_C < f_L$ always (capture range is smaller).
- $\tau = R_2 C_2$ sets capture range via filter bandwidth.
- Applications: FM demod, FSK decoder, frequency synthesizer, motor control.

### 🔹 Common KTU Valuation Errors to Avoid
1. ❌ Writing $T = 0.693 RC$ for 555 (missing $R_A + 2R_B$).
2. ❌ Inverting the difference amplifier output sign.
3. ❌ Confusing $f_L$ and $f_C$ (lock vs. capture).
4. ❌ Missing units in final numerical answers.
5. ❌ Failing to mention **virtual ground** in op-amp derivations.
6. ❌ Forgetting the bypass capacitor on pin 5 of the 555.

### 🔹 Quick Numerical Sanity Checks
- 555 with $R_A = R_B = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$: $f \approx 48$ Hz, $D = 66.7\%$.
- 741 summer with all $10\text{ k}\Omega$ resistors: $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$.
- 565 with $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$, $V_{CC} = 10$ V: $f_0 = 3$ kHz, $f_L = \pm 2.4$ kHz.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Experiments 3 or 4 from Section II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Experiments 3 or 4 from Section II

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Simulation of LIC Experiments"?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Simulation of Linear Integrated Circuit experiments refers to the **software-based emulation** of analog electronic circuits — typically realized using operational amplifiers (Op-Amps such as µA741, LM324, TL082) and timer ICs (such as NE555, SE555) — on a digital platform (such as **LTspice, NI Multisim, Cadence PSpice, Proteus VSM, or TINA-TI**) prior to, or in place of, physical hardware realization. The objective is to verify theoretical design parameters, predict circuit behaviour, perform time-domain and frequency-domain analysis, and generate publication-quality waveforms without consuming physical components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mandate:** Under the Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework of NEP 2020, simulation is treated as a **CO-mapped pre-validation step**. Lab records must typically include a simulation report (with schematic + waveform screenshots) accompanying the hardware result. This is a *graded* activity under CO3 / CO4 in PCECL407.

---

### 1.2 Typical Mapping of "Section II" Experiments (KTU 2024 LIC Lab)

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCECL407) typically partitions experiments into **Section I (Basic Analog Building Blocks)** and **Section II (Op-Amp Applications, Timers, and Active Filters)**. Section II commonly contains:

| Section II Experiment # | Standard Title | Core IC Used |
|:-----------------------:|:---------------|:-------------|
| Exp 1 | Inverting / Non-Inverting Amplifier | µA741 |
| Exp 2 | Adder / Subtractor / Instrumentation Amp | µA741 |
| **Exp 3** | **Active Filters (LPF & HPF) OR Schmitt Trigger** | **µA741** |
| **Exp 4** | **Active Filters OR 555 Timer (Astable / Monostable)** | **µA741 / NE555** |
| Exp 5 | Wien Bridge / RC Phase-Shift Oscillator | µA741 |
| Exp 6 | Precision Rectifier / Peak Detector | µA741 |
| Exp 7 | Triangular / Sawtooth Wave Generator | µA741 |
| Exp 8 | VCO / PLL | NE566 / CD4046 |

> [!NOTE]
> This document covers the **simulation methodology and design analysis** for the two most common candidates for Experiment 3 and Experiment 4:
> 1. **Experiment 3 — First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter (LPF) and High-Pass Filter (HPF) using µA741**
> 2. **Experiment 4 — Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator) using µA741**
>
> A student may be asked to simulate *either* of these; the design procedure is nearly identical for both.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy — The Audio Equalizer:**
Imagine a **graphic equalizer** on a music player. The bass slider controls low frequencies, the treble slider controls high frequencies. An **Active Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** is the "bass-only" knob — it lets low frequencies pass while blocking high ones. An **Active High-Pass Filter (HPF)** is the "treble-only" knob — it lets high frequencies pass while blocking low ones. The "**active**" part means an Op-Amp is added inside, so the filter **amplifies** the desired band instead of just attenuating the unwanted one (this is what a simple passive RC filter cannot do efficiently).

**Analogy — The Bouncer at a Door (Schmitt Trigger):**
A regular comparator is like a referee who calls "foul" the moment a ball crosses a line — very jittery if the ball wobbles. A **Schmitt Trigger** is a referee with a **hysteresis gap**: the ball must clearly cross the upper line to call "foul" and clearly drop below the lower line to call "no foul." This eliminates false triggering due to noise — perfect for cleaning up a noisy square wave from a sensor.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics, and Default Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **standard reference values** used throughout KTU LIC Lab simulations and must be memorized:
> - **Input Bias Current** $\left(I_B\right)$ for µA741: typically $\mathbf{80\ nA}$ (max $\mathbf{500\ nA}$)
> - **Slew Rate** (SR) of µA741: $\mathbf{0.5\ V/\mu s}$
> - **Open-Loop Gain** $\left(A_{OL}\right)$ of µA741: typically $\mathbf{2 \times 10^5}$ (≈ 106 dB)
> - **Gain-Bandwidth Product** (GBW) of µA741: $\mathbf{1\ MHz}$
> - **Supply Voltage** (typical dual): $\pm\mathbf{12\ V}$ or $\pm\mathbf{15\ V}$ (the **±15 V** rail is the KTU-default)
> - **Cutoff Frequency Definition (KTU Standard):** The frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its passband value, i.e., **−3 dB point**.
> - **Decibel conversion:** $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\vert A_v \vert\right)$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Experiment 3 — First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter (LPF)

#### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology (Functional Description)

A first-order active LPF is built by cascading a **passive RC low-pass section** at the input of a **non-inverting unity-gain follower** (or non-inverting amplifier with gain $A_F = 1 + \frac{R_F}{R_1}$). The Op-Amp **isolates** the RC load from the next stage, preventing "loading effect" — this is the single most important reason an Op-Amp is added to a passive RC filter.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. A sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is applied across the series RC network.
2. The capacitor reactance $X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ is **high at low frequencies** and **low at high frequencies**.
3. At low frequencies ($f \ll f_c$): the capacitor acts as an open circuit, so the full input appears at the Op-Amp's non-inverting input → **signal passes unchanged (unity gain)**.
4. At high frequencies ($f \gg f_c$): the capacitor acts as a short circuit, shunting the signal to ground → **signal is attenuated to zero**.
5. The transition happens smoothly around the **cutoff frequency $f_c$**.

#### 2.1.2 Mathematical Foundation

For the standard non-inverting first-order LPF:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = A_F \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

where the **passband gain** is:

$$A_F = 1 + \frac{R_F}{R_1}$$

The **−3 dB cutoff frequency** is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

#### 2.1.3 Magnitude and Phase Response

$$\left\vert \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \right\vert = \frac{A_F}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

$$\phi(f) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Roll-off rate:** A first-order LPF rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** (or equivalently **−6 dB/octave**) beyond $f_c$. For a steeper roll-off, a **second-order** filter (e.g., Sallen-Key topology) is used in higher experiments.

---

### 2.2 Experiment 3 — First-Order Active High-Pass Filter (HPF)

The HPF is the **dual** of the LPF: simply swap the positions of R and C in the input network.

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = A_F \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. At **low frequencies** ($f \ll f_c$): the capacitor $X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ is very large, so the input signal is blocked → **output → 0**.
2. At **high frequencies** ($f \gg f_c$): the capacitor acts as a short, the full input reaches the Op-Amp → **output = $A_F \cdot V_{in}$**.
3. Transition again at $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, the same formula as the LPF.

$$\left\vert \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \right\vert_{HPF} = \frac{A_F \cdot (f/f_c)}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

---

### 2.3 Experiment 4 — Schmitt Trigger (Inverting and Non-Inverting)

#### 2.3.1 The Concept of Hysteresis

A standard Op-Amp comparator has a **single threshold** (0 V when inputs are tied as $+V_{in}$ and $-V_{in}$ via identical resistors). Any noise crossing that threshold causes **chattering** at the output. The Schmitt Trigger solves this by using **positive feedback** — a fraction $\beta$ of the output is fed back to the non-inverting input, creating **two distinct thresholds**:

**Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Upper Threshold (UTP):**

$$V_{UTP} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat} = +\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Lower Threshold (LTP):**

$$V_{LTP} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat} = -\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Hysteresis Voltage (HVT):**

$$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat} = \frac{2 R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a KTU lab experiment using µA741 powered at $\pm V_{CC} = \pm 12\ V$, the Op-Amp output saturates at approximately $\pm V_{sat} = \pm 10\ V$ (about 1.5–2 V below the rail). Always use $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5$ V, **never** $V_{CC}$ itself, in viva calculations.

#### 2.3.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. Initially, $V_{in} < V_{LTP}$ → output is at $+V_{sat}$ → non-inverting input sits at $+V_{UTP}$.
2. As $V_{in}$ rises and crosses $V_{UTP}$ from below, the inverting input becomes more positive than the non-inverting input → output **switches** to $-V_{sat}$.
3. The non-inverting input now jumps down to $-V_{LTP}$ (because the feedback has flipped).
4. To switch back, $V_{in}$ must drop *below* $-V_{LTP}$. This is **hysteresis** — the input must travel a full $V_H$ to flip-flop the output.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU ESE & Lab Viva**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical KTU Values |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Cutoff Frequency (LPF/HPF) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | $f_c = 1\ \text{kHz},\ 10\ \text{kHz}$ |
| 2 | Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ω | — |
| 3 | Passband Gain (Non-Inverting) | $A_F = 1 + \dfrac{R_F}{R_1}$ | V/V | 1, 2, 11 |
| 4 | Inverting Gain | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_F}{R_{in}}$ | V/V | -1, -10 |
| 5 | LPF Magnitude Response | $\left\vert H(f) \right\vert = \dfrac{A_F}{\sqrt{1+(f/f_c)^2}}$ | V/V | — |
| 6 | HPF Magnitude Response | $\left\vert H(f) \right\vert = \dfrac{A_F (f/f_c)}{\sqrt{1+(f/f_c)^2}}$ | V/V | — |
| 7 | Roll-off (1st order) | -20 dB/decade | dB/dec | — |
| 8 | dB Conversion | $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_v \vert)$ | dB | $A_v=10 \Rightarrow 20$ dB |
| 9 | Feedback Fraction (Schmitt) | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | unitless | 0.1 – 0.9 |
| 10 | Upper Threshold (Schmitt) | $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$ | V | ±1 V to ±5 V |
| 11 | Lower Threshold (Schmitt) | $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$ | V | mirrored of UTP |
| 12 | Hysteresis Width | $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$ | V | typically 1–4 V |
| 13 | µA741 Saturation Voltage | $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5$ | V | ±10 V for ±12 V supply |
| 14 | µA741 Slew Rate Limit | $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$ | Hz | $V_m=5$ V → 16 kHz max |
| 15 | Time Period (Square Wave) | $T = 1/f$ | s | — |

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Cutoff Frequency of a First-Order RC Filter

**Starting Point:** The series RC network has a transfer function in the s-domain.

Using the voltage divider rule, the output of the passive RC low-pass section is taken across the capacitor:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out,RC}}{V_{in}} = \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}$$

**Step 1:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$ to clear the fraction:

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{sRC + 1}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ for steady-state sinusoidal analysis:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3:** The magnitude is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 4:** By definition, the cutoff frequency is the point where $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. This requires $\omega RC = 1$.

$$\omega_c RC = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

**Step 5:** Convert angular frequency to Hz using $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The cutoff frequency is set entirely by the passive RC product. The Op-Amp's role is to provide **isolation** (infinite input impedance) and **gain** ($A_F$). A practical design rule: **choose C first (in the nF–µF range)**, then compute R for the desired $f_c$ using:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Design Example — Active LPF with $f_c = 1$ kHz, $A_F = 2$

**Design Specification (from KTU problem statement):**
- $f_c = 1\ \text{kHz}$
- Passband gain $A_F = 2$
- Op-Amp: µA741
- Supply: $\pm 12$ V

**Step 1:** Choose a convenient capacitor value.

For $f_c$ in the kHz range, a capacitor in the nF range is ideal (small, low leakage, low cost). Let $C = 0.01\ \mu\text{F} = 10\ \text{nF}$.

**Step 2:** Compute R for the desired cutoff.

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^5}{2 \pi} \approx 15.915\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Round to a standard E12 resistor value.

$R \approx 15\ \text{k}\Omega$ (closest standard value). Recomputed cutoff:

$$f_c' = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 15 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 1061\ \text{Hz} \approx 1.06\ \text{kHz}$$

Acceptable error ≈ 6 % (within E12 tolerance).

**Step 4:** Choose feedback resistors for $A_F = 2$.

$$A_F = 1 + \frac{R_F}{R_1} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_F = R_1$$

Select $R_1 = R_F = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ (standard).

**Step 5:** Verify magnitude at $f = 100$ Hz (well below $f_c$):

$$\left\vert H \right\vert = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1 + (100/1000)^2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1.01}} \approx 1.99 \approx 2 \quad \text{✓ (passband)}$$

**Step 6:** Verify magnitude at $f = 1$ kHz (cutoff):

$$\left\vert H \right\vert = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1 + 1}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \quad \text{✓ (this is } 2/\sqrt{2}\text{, i.e., } A_F - 3\text{ dB)}$$

**Step 7:** Verify roll-off at $f = 10$ kHz (one decade above $f_c$):

$$\left\vert H \right\vert = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1 + 100}} = \frac{2}{10.05} \approx 0.199 \quad \text{✓ (dropped by factor of 10, consistent with −20 dB/decade)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Design Example — Schmitt Trigger with $V_{UTP} = +2$ V, $V_{LTP} = -2$ V

**Design Specification:**
- $V_{UTP} = +2$ V, $V_{LTP} = -2$ V
- Op-Amp: µA741
- Supply: $\pm 12$ V → $V_{sat} \approx \pm 10$ V

**Step 1:** Compute the feedback fraction $\beta$.

$$V_{UTP} = \beta \cdot V_{sat} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta = \frac{V_{UTP}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2$$

**Step 2:** Choose a value for $R_1$ (the resistor from non-inverting input to ground) — let $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 3:** Solve for $R_2$ (the feedback resistor from output to non-inverting input).

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 + R_2 = \frac{R_1}{0.2} = 5 R_1$$

$$R_2 = 5R_1 - R_1 = 4R_1 = 40\ \text{k}\Omega$$

Standard value: $R_2 = 39\ \text{k}\Omega$ (E12 closest).

**Step 4:** Recompute actual thresholds.

$$\beta_{actual} = \frac{10}{10 + 39} = \frac{10}{49} \approx 0.204$$

$$V_{UTP,actual} = 0.204 \times 10 = +2.04\ \text{V}$$
$$V_{LTP,actual} = -2.04\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5:** Compute hysteresis width.

$$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 2.04 - (-2.04) = 4.08\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6:** Verify with a KTU-style input — a triangular wave of 1 kHz, amplitude 5 V.

The triangular wave's peak (5 V) clearly exceeds $V_{UTP} = 2.04$ V → output switches to $-V_{sat}$. The triangular wave's trough ($-5$ V) clearly drops below $V_{LTP} = -2.04$ V → output switches back to $+V_{sat}$. Result: a clean **square wave** at the output, frequency equal to the input triangular frequency. **This is the classic "Schmitt Trigger as a sine/triangle-to-square converter" demonstration in the KTU lab.**

---

### 3.4 Simulation Methodology — The 7-Step KTU Lab Workflow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following 7-step procedure is the **expected structure of a simulation report** in the KTU 2024 Scheme PCECL407 lab. Examiners award marks for each section.

1. **Aim & Apparatus** — State the experiment, list ICs (e.g., µA741 × 1, NE555 × 1), passive components, simulation tool, and version.
2. **Theory** — Brief derivation of $f_c$, $A_F$, or $V_{UTP}/V_{LTP}$ formulas (as shown in §3.1–§3.3).
3. **Circuit Diagram** — Screenshot of the schematic from the simulator.
4. **Design Calculations** — Compute R, C, $R_F$, $R_1$ from specifications.
5. **Simulation Procedure** — Configure sources (sine/square/triangular), set transient analysis time, set AC analysis sweep range.
6. **Output Waveforms** — Screenshots with cursors marking peak values, time, frequency.
7. **Inference & Comparison** — Compare measured $f_c$ and $A_F$ with theoretical; discuss percentage error; comment on simulator limitations.
