## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *cutoff frequency* ($f_c$) of a first-order active low-pass filter. State the formula relating $f_c$ to the circuit components.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The cutoff frequency $f_c$ of a first-order active low-pass filter is defined as the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its passband (DC) value, corresponding to a power loss of **−3 dB** [1 mark]. It is given by the formula:
> $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \quad \text{Hz}$$
> where $R$ is the series resistance and $C$ is the shunt capacitance in the input RC network [1 mark]. Beyond $f_c$, the output magnitude rolls off at a rate of **−20 dB/decade** for a first-order filter [1 mark].

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is *hysteresis* in a Schmitt Trigger? Why is it necessary in comparator circuits?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Hysteresis in a Schmitt Trigger refers to the **intentional difference between the upper threshold voltage ($V_{UTP}$) and the lower threshold voltage ($V_{LTP}$)** that the input must cross before the output changes state [1 mark]. Mathematically, the hysteresis voltage is $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 2\beta V_{sat}$, where $\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ is the positive feedback fraction [1 mark]. It is necessary because it provides **noise immunity** — without hysteresis, any small noise ripple around a single threshold would cause the comparator output to chatter (oscillate rapidly between HIGH and LOW), which is unacceptable in digital logic and pulse-shaping applications [1 mark].

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Question Paper Instruction (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer any ONE full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks, subdivided into two parts of 7 marks each.*

---

#### **Question A — Active Filter Design (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Design a first-order active low-pass filter using µA741 with cutoff frequency $f_c = 5$ kHz and passband gain $A_F = 11$. Use a capacitor $C = 10$ nF. List the calculated component values and draw the circuit diagram. [7 marks]

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute $R$ from the cutoff formula:**

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{\pi} \approx 3183\ \Omega \approx 3.18\ \text{k}\Omega$$

Closest standard E12 value: **$R = 3.3$ kΩ** [2 marks].

**Step 2 — Compute $R_F$ and $R_1$ for $A_F = 11$:**

$$A_F = 1 + \frac{R_F}{R_1} = 11 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_F = 10 R_1$$

Choose $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega \Rightarrow R_F = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$ [1 mark].

**Step 3 — Recompute $f_c$ with the standard $R$ value:**

$$f_c' = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3300 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 4823\ \text{Hz} \approx 4.82\ \text{kHz}$$

Error: $\frac{\vert 5000 - 4823 \vert}{5000} \times 100\% = 3.54\%$ [1 mark].

**Step 4 — Circuit diagram (description, as the schematic is drawn by the student):**

- µA741 powered at $\pm 12$ V (pin 7 = +12 V, pin 4 = −12 V, pin 6 = output)
- $R = 3.3$ kΩ from input source to pin 3 (non-inverting input)
- $C = 10$ nF from pin 3 to ground
- $R_1 = 10$ kΩ from pin 3 to ground (separate path)
- $R_F = 100$ kΩ from pin 6 to pin 2 (inverting input)
- Pin 2 has no other connection (inverting input serves as the feedback summing node)
- Pin 1 (offset null) — typically left open in lab demos [2 marks].

**Step 5 — Verification in dB:**

$$A_F \text{ in dB} = 20 \log_{10}(11) = 20 \times 1.0414 = 20.83\ \text{dB}$$

At $f = f_c$, the magnitude should be $11 / \sqrt{2} \approx 7.78$, which in dB is $20.83 - 3 = 17.83$ dB [1 mark].

---

**(b)** Simulate the filter designed in part (a) in LTspice. Perform an **AC analysis** from 10 Hz to 100 kHz. Plot the magnitude response in dB and identify the −3 dB point. Briefly explain what a Bode plot is. [7 marks]

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — LTspice Schematic Setup (procedure only, schematic drawn by student):**

- Place µA741 from the library `[Opamps]`
- Connect pin 7 to +12 V, pin 4 to −12 V
- Place V1 (SINE, 1 V amplitude, 0 phase) as input
- Add `.op` and `.ac dec 100 10 100k` spice directive for AC analysis
- Add `.tran 0 5m 0 1u` for transient time-domain analysis [1 mark].

**Step 2 — Expected Bode Plot Response (key marks):**

The Bode plot (magnitude in dB vs log frequency) will show:
- Flat passband at **20.83 dB** from 10 Hz up to ~1 kHz
- A gentle "knee" beginning around 1 kHz
- A sharp roll-off through the **−3 dB point at 4.82 kHz** (the value of $f_c'$ computed in 4a)
- A linear descent of −20 dB/decade after $f_c$, reaching roughly 0 dB at ~48 kHz (one decade above) and −20 dB at ~480 kHz [2 marks].

**Step 3 — Definition of Bode Plot:**

A Bode plot consists of two graphs: (i) magnitude in dB versus $\log_{10}(f)$ and (ii) phase in degrees versus $\log_{10}(f)$. It is the standard frequency-domain visualization tool for linear time-invariant systems and is constructed automatically by spice simulators using AC analysis. The −3 dB point on the magnitude plot defines the cutoff frequency $f_c$ [2 marks].

**Step 4 — Practical Issue (Bonus):**

> **Important — Slew-Rate Limitation:** For transient simulation, the maximum undistorted sine frequency the µA741 can reproduce is $f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5} \approx 15.9$ kHz for a 5 V sine. If the test frequency is above this, the output will be a **triangular wave** (slew-rate limited), not a sine. This is a common KTU viva question [1 mark].

**Step 5 — Inference (1 mark):**

The simulated $f_c$ matches the theoretical $f_c'$ within 3.5 %, confirming the design. The minor deviation is due to the use of standard (E12) resistor values and µA741's non-ideal open-loop gain and input capacitance.

---

#### **Question B — Schmitt Trigger Design (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Design an **inverting Schmitt Trigger** using µA741 powered from $\pm 12$ V rails, with upper threshold voltage $V_{UTP} = +4$ V and lower threshold voltage $V_{LTP} = -4$ V. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 10$ V. Compute the feedback resistor values and sketch the input-output waveform when a triangular wave of amplitude 6 V, 1 kHz is applied. [7 marks]

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the feedback fraction $\beta$:**

$$V_{UTP} = \beta \cdot V_{sat} \Rightarrow \beta = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

**Step 2 — Choose $R_1$:**

$R_1$ is the resistor from the non-inverting input (pin 3) to ground. A noise-immune value: $R_1 = 10$ kΩ [1 mark].

**Step 3 — Compute $R_2$:**

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 + R_2 = \frac{10}{0.4} = 25\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_2 = 25 - 10 = 15\ \text{k}\Omega$$

Closest standard value: **$R_2 = 15$ kΩ** (E12 exact) [1 mark].

**Step 4 — Verify the actual thresholds:**

$$\beta_{actual} = \frac{10}{10 + 15} = \frac{10}{25} = 0.4 \quad \text{✓ Exact}$$

$$V_{UTP} = 0.4 \times 10 = +4\ \text{V}, \quad V_{LTP} = -4\ \text{V} \quad \text{✓ As specified}$$

**Step 5 — Compute hysteresis width:**

$$V_H = 2\beta V_{sat} = 2 \times 0.4 \times 10 = 8\ \text{V}$$ [1 mark].

**Step 6 — Sketch and explain the waveform (textual description for a 6 V, 1 kHz triangular input):**

- The triangular wave sweeps between **−6 V and +6 V** [0.5 marks].
- When the input rises through **+4 V**, the output switches from $+V_{sat} = +10$ V to $-V_{sat} = -10$ V [0.5 marks].
- The output remains at −10 V as the input falls past +4 V (because the new threshold is $V_{LTP} = -4$ V).
- The output switches back to +10 V only when the input crosses **−4 V** on its way down [0.5 marks].
- The output is a **clean square wave** of frequency 1 kHz (equal to the input triangular frequency) with amplitude $\pm 10$ V [1 mark].
- The duty cycle of the output square wave is determined by the *time* the triangular wave spends above +4 V vs. below −4 V, which for a symmetric triangular wave is **50 %** [0.5 marks].

---

**(b)** Simulate the Schmitt Trigger designed in part (a) in any standard spice tool. Apply (i) a noisy sine wave and (ii) a slow triangular wave. Show with screenshots that the noisy sine wave produces a clean square output. [7 marks]

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — LTspice Setup:**

- Place µA741
- Pin 7 → +12 V, pin 4 → −12 V, pin 6 → output
- $R_1 = 10$ kΩ from pin 3 to GND
- $R_2 = 15$ kΩ from pin 6 to pin 3
- Input V1 applied to pin 2 (inverting input)
- Pin 3 receives only the feedback from $R_2$ [1 mark].

**Step 2 — Source Configurations:**

- Noisy sine: `SINE(0 5 1k)` plus an additional random noise voltage of amplitude 0.5 V (modelled using a PWL noise source or by summing two sine sources) [1 mark].
- Triangular: `PULSE(-6 6 0 0.25m 0.25m 0.25m 1m)` or `SINE(0 6 1k)` — for a triangular wave in LTspice, use a `PWL` file or a `SFFM` with appropriate parameters [1 mark].

**Step 3 — Run Transient Analysis:**

`.tran 0 5m 0 10u` to capture 5 full periods with 10 µs resolution.

**Step 4 — Expected Observations (the marks-bearing part):**

(i) **Noisy sine input:**
- The noisy sine wave at the input fluctuates rapidly around the threshold values.
- *Without* the Schmitt Trigger, a regular comparator would produce a **chattering output** with multiple transitions per cycle due to the noise.
- *With* the Schmitt Trigger, the output transitions only at $V_{UTP} = +4$ V (going up) and $V_{LTP} = -4$ V (going down). The hysteresis "absorbs" the noise — the output is a **clean square wave** at 1 kHz with amplitude $\pm 10$ V [2 marks].

(ii) **Triangular input:**
- The output is a **clean square wave** synchronized to the input.
- Each peak of the triangular wave (above +4 V) triggers one transition; each trough (below −4 V) triggers the opposite transition.
- The output has **50 % duty cycle** for a symmetric triangular input [1 mark].

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The simulation confirms the design and demonstrates the **noise-immunity benefit** of hysteresis. This is the core engineering reason Schmitt Triggers are used at the front end of every digital input pin in real-world micro-controllers (the "Schmitt-trigger input" is a standard feature in STM32, 8051, CMOS logic families) [1 mark].

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using $V_{CC}$ instead of $V_{sat}$:** A student who computes $V_{UTP} = 0.4 \times 12 = 4.8$ V instead of $0.4 \times 10 = 4$ V will lose 1 mark. The Op-Amp output **never reaches the supply rail** — always subtract 1.5–2 V for saturation.
> 2. **Confusing inverting and non-inverting Schmitt Trigger formulas:** In the inverting version, $V_{in}$ is at pin 2 and feedback goes to pin 3. In the non-inverting version, $V_{in}$ is at pin 3 and feedback still goes to pin 3 (from output) — the threshold formulas have **opposite sign conventions**. Misnaming costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert the magnitude ratio to dB:** KTU papers frequently ask for the "gain in dB." Writing $A_F = 2$ without the dB conversion loses 1 mark. Always state both: $A_F = 2 = 6.02$ dB.
> 4. **Not specifying the Op-Amp supply voltage:** A complete design must mention $V_{CC} = \pm 12$ V (or $\pm 15$ V). A schematic without power pins is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Plugging $f$ (Hz) where the formula wants $\omega$ (rad/s):** A student who writes $R = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ and then substitutes $f = 2\pi \times 1000$ has double-applied $2\pi$. The 1-mark deduction is for not recognizing the difference between $f$ and $\omega$.
> 6. **Missing the simulation tool name and version:** KTU 2024 ESE answers that mention only "simulation" without specifying "LTspice XVII" or "Multisim 14.2" lose 1 mark for lack of reproducibility.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This to Your Wall Before the Exam**

**Active LPF & HPF (Experiment 3):**
- ☐ LPF blocks high frequencies; HPF blocks low frequencies.
- ☐ Cutoff frequency formula: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — **same for both LPF and HPF**.
- ☐ Passband gain: $A_F = 1 + \frac{R_F}{R_1}$ for non-inverting topology.
- ☐ Roll-off rate of a first-order filter: **−20 dB/decade** beyond $f_c$.
- ☐ At $f = f_c$, the output magnitude drops to $\frac{A_F}{\sqrt{2}}$, i.e., **−3 dB**.
- ☐ Design sequence: **Choose C first (nF range), then compute R** for desired $f_c$.

**Schmitt Trigger (Experiment 4):**
- ☐ Two thresholds: $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$ and $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$.
- ☐ Hysteresis: $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat} = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$.
- ☐ Feedback fraction: $\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$.
- ☐ Inverting Schmitt Trigger: input at pin 2, feedback to pin 3.
- ☐ µA741 saturation: $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5$ V (never use the rail itself).
- ☐ Primary use: **noise immunity** at comparator outputs, square-wave generation from triangular/sine inputs.

**Simulation Workflow (Universal):**
- ☐ AC analysis → Bode plot → identify −3 dB cutoff.
- ☐ Transient analysis → time-domain waveform → measure amplitude, period, duty cycle.
- ☐ Always report **simulator name + version**, supply voltage, peak input voltage.
- ☐ Always include a **comparison table**: theoretical value vs simulated value vs % error.
- ☐ Always discuss **sources of error**: E12 tolerance, Op-Amp non-ideality, slew rate, finite GBW.

**µA741 Critical Specs (memorize verbatim):**
- ☐ Slew Rate = **0.5 V/µs**, GBW = **1 MHz**, $A_{OL}$ = **2 × 10⁵**, $I_B$ = **80 nA (typ)**.

**KTU Report Writing Tips:**
- ☐ Aim, Theory, Circuit Diagram, Design, Simulation Steps, Waveforms, Inference — **all 7 sections mandatory**.
- ☐ **Print screenshots in colour** if possible; mark cursors with values visible.
- ☐ Reference page numbers from the KTU-recommended textbook — **do not invent** references.
